Real systems lose energy to inverter, cable heating, and charge controller inefficiencies (typically 10–20%):

$$\eta_{system} \approx 0.85$$

### Step 4: Final Backup Time Formula

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{battery} \times V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{system}}{P_{total,load}}$$

Or equivalently, using current directly:

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{battery} \times DoD \times \eta_{system}}{I_{total,load}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Battery Autonomy for Night Sailing

> **Problem Statement:**
> A traditional boat has the following night-time DC loads: navigation lights (3 × 10W = 30W), VHF radio (5W standby + 25W receive = 30W), cabin light (20W), and bilge pump intermittent (60W, runs 5% of time = 3W effective). The battery is a **12V, 100Ah lead-acid** unit. Calculate the backup time.

**Step 1 — Total Continuous Load:**

$$P_{total} = 30 + 30 + 20 + 3 = 83\;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Current:**

$$I_{total} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{V_{system}} = \dfrac{83}{12} = 6.92\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply DoD and Efficiency:**

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C \times DoD \times \eta}{I_{total}}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.50 \times 0.85}{6.92}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{42.5}{6.92} = 6.14\;\text{hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: Approximately 6.1 hours of night-time operation** before the battery must be recharged. This is the KTU-typical autonomy figure for a small traditional fishing boat.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fuse Sizing Rules (ABYC E-11 Compliant)

| Load Type | Continuous Current ($I_{cont}$) | Fuse Rating Rule | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Navigation lights | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | Safety margin against inrush |
| Bilge pump | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | Motor starting surge |
| VHF Radio | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | TX mode draws more |
| Solar panel (string) | $I_{sc}$ (from panel) | $1.56 \times I_{sc}$ | Accounts for irradiance > STC |
| Inverter input | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{rated}$ | Surge capacity buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 125% rule is a KTU board favorite** — if a 10A load is protected, the fuse must be $1.25 \times 10 = 12.5\;\text{A}$. Round up to the next standard fuse: **15 A**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Boat Electrical Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a traditional boat's electrical system, from energy generation through storage, distribution, protection, and consumption.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Sources] --> B[Charge Controller / Regulator]
    B --> C[Battery Bank 12V DC]
    C --> D[Main Fuse 80A]
    D --> E[Positive Bus Bar]
    E --> F1[Fuse 10A Nav Lights]
    E --> F2[Fuse 5A VHF Radio]
    E --> F3[Fuse 8A Bilge Pump]
    E --> F4[Fuse 3A Cabin Lights]
    F1 --> L1[Port Light Red]
    F1 --> L2[Starboard Light Green]
    F1 --> L3[Stern Light White]
    F1 --> L4[Masthead Light White]
    F2 --> R1[VHF Radio Transceiver]
    F3 --> P1[Bilge Pump 12V DC]
    F4 --> L5[Cabin LED Lights]
    C --> N[Negative Bus Bar]
    N --> G1[L1]
    N --> G2[L2]
    N --> G3[L3]
    N --> G4[L4]
    N --> G5[R1]
    N --> G6[P1]
    N --> G7[L5]
    H[Shore Power 230V AC] --> I[Isolation Transformer]
    I --> J[Galvanic Isolator]
    J --> K[Battery Charger AC-DC]
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Energy flows from sources (top) → storage (battery) → main fuse → bus bar → individual fused branches → loads. The negative return path collects at the negative bus bar, which is bonded to the battery's negative terminal. Shore power enters through a safety chain of **Isolation Transformer → Galvanic Isolator → Charger** to prevent hull corrosion.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Single Branch Circuit (Navigation Light)

This is the most common sub-circuit found in any KTU exam asking to "draw and explain a typical boat lighting circuit."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Positive +12V] --> B[Main Fuse 1A]
    B --> C[Switch SPST]
    C --> D[Navigation Light Bulb LED]
    D --> E[Battery Negative 0V Ground]
    E --> A
```

**Explanation of Each Element:**
- **Battery (+12V)** — DC source
- **Main Fuse (1A)** — protects the wire from short circuit
- **SPST Switch** — single-pole single-throw manual control
- **LED Light** — the load (typically 1–3W)
- **Return to (−)** — completes the circuit

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Solar Charging Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sunlight Hits PV Panel] --> S2[Panel Produces DC Electricity]
    S2 --> S3{MPPT Controller Active?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Tracks Maximum Power Point]
    S3 -- No --> S5[PWM Charging Mode]
    S4 --> S6[Regulated DC to Battery]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Battery State of Charge Increasing]
    S7 --> S8{Battery Full?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Controller Switches to Float Mode]
    S8 -- No --> S6
    S9 --> S10[Maintains 13.6V Trickle]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPPT vs. PWM (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **MPPT** (Maximum Power Point Tracking) extracts **20–30% more energy** by dynamically adjusting the panel's operating voltage. It is **expensive** but **efficient**.
> - **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation) is **cheaper** but simply connects the panel directly to the battery, wasting excess voltage as heat.
> - For traditional boat retrofits in Kerala's tropical sun, **MPPT is strongly recommended**.

---

## 4.4 Wiring Topology — Backbone vs. Branch (Decision Matrix)

| Topology | Description | Best Use Case in Boats | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Radial (Tree)** | Each load runs its own cable from the bus | Most common; clean fault isolation | Standard answer for KTU diagrams |
| **Ring (Loop)** | Cables form a closed loop; two paths to each load | High-reliability vessels (yachts) | Advanced topic |
| **Combined** | Mix of both | Modern traditional boat builds | Briefly mention in exams |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a "Bus Bar" in the context of a boat's electrical system. Why is it preferred over twisting multiple wires together at a single point?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

A **bus bar** is a solid metallic conductor (typically tinned copper) that serves as a **common collection point** for multiple circuits. In a boat, there are two bus bars: a **positive bus bar** (fed from the battery via the main fuse) and a **negative bus bar** (connected to battery negative / common ground).

**Why preferred over twisted connections:**
1. **Lower contact resistance** — a solid bar minimizes voltage drop.
2. **Mechanical stability** — withstands vibration inherent in marine environments.
3. **Easier maintenance** — individual circuits can be added/removed without disturbing others.
4. **Reduced fire risk** — eliminates loose strands that could cause short circuits.
5. **Neat installation** — improves traceability during fault diagnosis.

*(3 marks — definition 1, advantages 2)*

---

### **Q2. What is "galvanic corrosion" and how does a galvanic isolator protect a traditional boat's metal fittings?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

**Galvanic corrosion** is the electrochemical destruction of metals when two dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller and steel hull fitting) are electrically connected in the presence of an **electrolyte** (seawater). The more *active* metal (anode) corrodes preferentially.

When a boat is connected to **shore power**, a small stray DC current can flow through the grounding wire, accelerating corrosion of underwater metal fittings.

A **galvanic isolator** is a device installed in the shore-power grounding wire that **blocks DC currents below ~1.4V** (using semiconductor diodes) while still allowing **AC fault currents** to pass for safety. This prevents the destructive DC galvanic loop while preserving protection against electric shock.

*(3 marks — phenomenon 1, mechanism 1, protection 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the **factor of 2 (round-trip length)** in voltage-drop calculations. This alone costs **3 of 14 marks** in typical ESE papers.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using **power in kW but voltage in V inconsistently** — always keep units uniform.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not stating the **standard (ABYC / ISO)** when justifying cable size or fuse rating. Examiners reward students who cite a code.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing bus bars as **single lines** instead of showing **positive AND negative** rails. Both must appear.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **MPPT with PWM** — explain the efficiency gain (~20–30%) explicitly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Solar-Electrical Design for a Traditional Fishing Boat**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a 12V DC electrical system installed in a traditional wooden fishing boat. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traditional fishing boat is equipped with the following DC loads operating in daylight: a **30W VHF radio**, a **15W GPS**, a **20W fish-finder**, and a **10W cabin fan**, all at **12V DC**. The boat has one **100 Wp solar panel** with an MPPT charge controller ($\eta = 0.92$) and a **12V, 100Ah lead-acid battery** (DoD = 50%). Assuming **5 peak sun hours/day**, calculate: **(i)** the daily energy consumption, **(ii)** the daily solar energy harvested, and **(iii)** the battery backup time if all loads run continuously. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram (5 marks) and Component List (2 marks):**

The block diagram should mirror the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** above. The student must include:

1. **Solar PV Panel (100 Wp)** — converts sunlight to DC electricity.
2. **MPPT Charge Controller** — regulates panel output, prevents battery overcharge, maximizes harvested energy.
3. **Battery Bank (12V, 100Ah Lead-Acid)** — stores energy for night use and engine-off operation.
4. **Main Fuse (80A)** — protects the entire distribution system from catastrophic short circuit.
5. **Positive & Negative Bus Bars** — central distribution terminals.
6. **Individual Branch Fuses (3A, 5A, 8A, 10A)** — per ABYC E-11, each load fused at 125% of full-load current.
7. **Loads** — VHF radio, GPS, fish-finder, cabin fan, navigation lights, bilge pump.
8. **Negative Return Path** — completes the DC circuit back to the battery's negative terminal.

*(Full 7 marks: 4 for diagram completeness, 1 for correct symbols/polarity, 2 for component list with function.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Daily Energy Consumption:**

The loads run during daylight, so use **5 hours of operation** (peak sun hours = typical operating time).

$$P_{total} = 30 + 15 + 20 + 10 = 75\;\text{W}$$

$$E_{daily} = P_{total} \times t = 75 \times 5 = 375\;\text{Wh/day}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step (ii) — Daily Solar Energy Harvested:**

$$E_{solar} = P_{panel} \times H_{peak-sun} \times \eta_{MPPT}$$

$$E_{solar} = 100 \times 5 \times 0.92 = 460\;\text{Wh/day}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step (iii) — Battery Backup Time (All Loads Continuous):**

First, total load current:

$$I_{total} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{V_{system}} = \dfrac{75}{12} = 6.25\;\text{A}$$

Now apply the autonomy formula:

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C \times DoD}{I_{total}}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.50}{6.25} = \dfrac{50}{6.25} = 8.0\;\text{hours}$$

*(2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With continuous full-load operation, the battery can sustain the system for **8 hours** before requiring recharge. Since daily solar harvest (460 Wh) exceeds daily consumption (375 Wh), the boat's energy budget is **sustainable** — a surplus of 85 Wh/day is available for night loads or battery rebalancing.

**Total marks for Q(b): 7**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cable Sizing & Protection Design**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** A traditional boat has a **bilge pump** rated **12V, 60W**, located **6 m** from the battery. The pump is fitted with a **float switch** for automatic operation. Using **tinned copper cable** ($\rho = 0.0175\;\Omega\cdot mm^2/m$) and an **allowable voltage drop of 3%**, determine: **(i)** the full-load current, **(ii)** the minimum required cable cross-section, and **(iii)** the correctly rated fuse size as per ABYC E-11. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **single-line diagram**, explain the working of a **shore-power connection** in a traditional boat. Mention the role of an **isolation transformer** and a **galvanic isolator** in the circuit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Full-Load Current:**

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{60}{12} = 5.0\;\text{A}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step (ii) — Cable Cross-Section:**

Allowable voltage drop:

$$V_{drop,allow} = 3\% \times 12\;\text{V} = 0.36\;\text{V}$$

Apply the cable sizing formula:

$$A = \dfrac{I \times 2L \times \rho}{V_{drop}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5.0 \times 2 \times 6 \times 0.0175}{0.36}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$5.0 \times 2 = 10.0$$
$$10.0 \times 6 = 60.0$$
$$60.0 \times 0.0175 = 1.05$$

Final division:

$$A = \dfrac{1.05}{0.36} = 2.917\;\text{mm}^2$$

Round up to the **next standard size** $\Rightarrow$ $A_{selected} = 4.0\;\text{mm}^2$

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for correct rounding to standard size.)*

**Step (iii) — Fuse Size (ABYC 125% Rule):**

$$I_{fuse} = 1.25 \times I_{FL} = 1.25 \times 5.0 = 6.25\;\text{A}$$

Round up to next standard fuse rating $\Rightarrow$ $I_{fuse,selected} = 8.0\;\text{A}$

*(2 marks — 1 for applying 125% rule, 1 for correct standard rating.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected components: 4.0 mm² tinned copper cable, 8A AGC/MRBF fuse.**

**Total for Q(a): 7 marks**

---

**(b) Single-Line Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

The student should draw a single-line diagram showing:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Marina Shore Power Outlet 230V AC] --> B[Shore Power Inlet Socket on Hull]
    B --> C[Main AC Circuit Breaker 16A]
    C --> D[Isolation Transformer 230V to 230V]
    D --> E[Galvanic Isolator in Ground Wire]
    E --> F[Galvanic Isolator]
    F --> G[AC Distribution Panel]
    G --> H1[Outlets and Battery Charger]
    G --> H2[Hot Water Heater Optional]
    D --> I[AC Ground Bus]
    I --> J[Hull Grounding Plate or Underwater Bonding]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Shore Power Inlet** — A marinized male/female socket on the hull side, rated IP67, receives 230V AC from the marina pedestal.
2. **Main AC Circuit Breaker (16A)** — Protects the boat's internal AC wiring from overcurrent; the **first safety device** after shore power enters the boat.
3. **Isolation Transformer (1:1)** — Provides **galvanic isolation** between the marina's ground and the boat's internal AC ground. Prevents stray marina DC currents from entering the boat and corroding underwater metals.
4. **Galvanic Isolator** — A **secondary defense** in the grounding wire, using two diodes in opposition to block low-voltage DC (under 1.4V total) while passing AC fault currents for human safety.
5. **AC Distribution Panel** — Distributes power to onboard AC outlets, water heater, and battery charger.
6. **Hull Bonding / Grounding Plate** — Connects all underwater metal fittings (propeller, shaft, through-hulls) to a common bonding point, equalizing potential and reducing corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both an isolation transformer AND a galvanic isolator?**
> - The **transformer** blocks galvanic DC currents completely.
> - The **isolator** is a cheaper secondary protection that works even when no transformer is fitted.
> - Together, they form a **defense-in-depth** corrosion protection system — a KTU board favorite for 7-mark explanation questions.

**Total for Q(b): 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the KTU ESE:**

- ⚡ **Standard Voltage:** Traditional small boats use **12V DC**; larger vessels use **24V DC**.
- 🔋 **Battery DoD:** Lead-acid = **50%**, Lithium (LiFePO₄) = **80–90%** — do NOT confuse.
- 📏 **Voltage Drop Standard:** **3%** for non-critical DC circuits, **10%** for engine starters (per ABYC E-11).
- 🧮 **Core Formula Trio:** $V = IR$, $P = VI$, $E = Pt$ — these appear in **80%** of numericals.
- 🪢 **Cable Formula:** Always multiply length by **2** for round-trip. Use **$\rho_{Cu} = 0.0175$** for tinned marine copper.
- 🔌 **Fuse Rule:** Rate the fuse at **125%** of full-load current. Round up to next standard size.
- 🚢 **Bus Bars:** Always **two** — one positive (live), one negative (ground/return). Never omit the negative.
- 🛡️ **Corrosion Trio:** Isolation Transformer + Galvanic Isolator + Bonding System = corrosion defense.
- ☀️ **MPPT vs. PWM:** MPPT gives **20–30% more energy** but costs more. Justify choice with numbers in exams.
- 💧 **Bilge Pump:** Must be on a **dedicated circuit** with its own fuse, wired directly to the battery (bypassing the main switch) so it works even when the master switch is OFF.
- 🧭 **Navigation Lights:** Color code — **Port = Red**, **Starboard = Green**, **Stern & Masthead = White**. This is COLREGS-mandated.
- 🌊 **Wire Material:** Always use **tinned copper** in marine environments; bare copper corrodes rapidly in salt air.
- ⚠️ **Common Pitfall:** Never size cables for **current-carrying capacity alone** — always check **voltage drop**; the latter usually governs.
- 🔄 **Energy Flow:** Sources → Charge Controller → Battery → Main Fuse → Bus Bar → Branch Fuses → Loads → Return to Battery Negative.
- 📐 **Units Check:** $1\;\text{Ah} = 1\;\text{A} \times 3600\;\text{s} = 3600\;\text{C}$ (Coulombs). $1\;\text{Wh} = V \times Ah$.
- 🇮🇳 **Kerala Context:** The *Suchitwa Mission* promotes solar-electric traditional boats — a likely essay/short-note topic for Kerala-specific regional questions.
- 🧯 **Safety Priority:** On a boat, **fire at sea = no escape**. The fuse/breaker system is more critical than on land.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Electrical Systems**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PENST864 — Traditional Boat Building Techniques. Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, OBE/NEP 2020 standards, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).*

topics.title: Anchoring, Mooring and Deck Fittings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anchoring, Mooring and Deck Fittings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Anchoring — Formal Definition

**Anchoring** is the operation of temporarily securing a vessel to the seabed by means of an **anchor**, connected to the boat through a **rode** (chain, rope, or a combination of both). The anchor resists the environmental loads acting on the boat (wind, current, wave action) by generating **holding force** through its fluke engagement with the bottom soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Expectation):**
> *"Anchoring is a mooring system that uses a portable ground tackle assembly — comprising the anchor, its rode (chain/cable), and associated deck fittings such as hawse pipes and windlasses — to maintain a vessel on a selected position by transferring horizontal loads into the seabed through buried flukes."*

## 1.2 Mooring — Formal Definition

**Mooring** is the permanent or semi-permanent securing of a vessel to a fixed structure (jetty, dolphin, buoy, or quay wall) using lines, chains, and deck hardware. Unlike anchoring, mooring does not depend on seabed soil condition but on the strength of the shore fittings and the vessel's mooring lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Anchor vs. Mooring — KTU Highlight Distinction**
> - **Anchoring** → Vessel to **seabed** (temporary, portable).
> - **Mooring** → Vessel to **shore/buoy** (can be permanent).

## 1.3 Deck Fittings — Formal Definition

**Deck fittings** are the metallic hardware components mounted on the deck, hull sides, or bulwarks of a vessel, used for:
- **Securing** mooring and anchoring lines (cleats, bollards, bitts).
- **Guiding** lines (fairleads, chocks, rollers, panama leads).
- **Allowing passage** through the deck (hatches, companionways, scuttles).
- **Safety & access** (rails, stanchions, ladders, man-ropes).

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Traditional Country Boat at a Kerala Backwater Jetty:**
> Imagine you park your two-wheeler outside a shop. You have two ways to "hold" it:
> 1. **Kickstand** → The bike holds itself on the ground. *(This is the **anchor** — the boat holds itself using the seabed.)*
> 2. **Tying to a pole** → You loop a chain around a fixed post. *(This is **mooring** — the boat is tied to a fixed shore structure.)*
> 3. **The hook/lock on the bike** → The mechanical hardware that grips the chain. *(This represents **deck fittings** — cleats, bollards, chocks.)*
>
> So: **Anchor** = holds boat to ground. **Mooring line** = holds boat to shore. **Deck fittings** = the "locks and hooks" that grip the lines.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Materials (KTU Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Standards for Traditional Boat Fittings:**
> - **Material:** Galvanized cast iron, forged steel, bronze, or marine-grade stainless steel (**AISI 316**).
> - **Working Load Limit (WLL):** Expressed in **kilonewtons (kN)** or **tonnes**.
> - **Anchor chain standard:** **Grade U2 / U3** (studded link) per **ISO 4565**.
> - **Mooring line diameter:** Typically **20 mm to 60 mm** for traditional country craft.
> - **Breaking strength rule:** Mooring line MBL (Minimum Breaking Load) ≥ **3 × WLL** (safety factor of 3).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Anchoring — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The holding capacity of an anchor depends on the **load transfer mechanism** from the shank to the fluke tip into the seabed.

**Logic Chain (Board-Exam Order):**
1. **Tension develops** in the rode as wind/current pulls the boat.
2. **Shank** (vertical shaft of the anchor) tilts under this pull.
3. **Fluke** (flat blade) rotates until one tip digs into the bottom.
4. **Penetration depth** is determined by fluke area, soil type, and angle of attack.
5. **Resistance** is generated by:
   - Soil shear strength (clay/silt) **OR**
   - Soil bearing failure (sand/gravel) **OR**
   - Suction / deadweight (mud, with mushroom anchors).

## 2.2 Classification of Anchors (Traditional Boat Focus)

| S.No | Anchor Type | Best Suited Bottom | Holding Principle | KTU Use Case |
|------|-------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Admiralty / Fisherman** | Soft mud & clay | Fluke penetration | Traditional wooden boats |
| 2 | **Grapnel** | Rocky/weed bottom | Multi-fluke bite | Country craft, small launches |
| 3 | **Mushroom** | Soft mud / silt | Suction + deadweight | Permanent mooring buoys |
| 4 | **Bruce / Claw** | Mixed bottoms | Roll-in setting | Modern FRP traditional boats |
| 5 | **Danforth / Fluke** | Sand & mud | Fluke area | Light displacement boats |
| 6 | **Kedge** | All-purpose | Light fluke | Secondary / working anchor |
| 7 | **Plough / CQR** | Mixed / reef | Pivoting shank | Sea-going vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> For **traditional Kerala boat building** (e.g., *Vallam*, *Vanchi*, *Dugout canoe* anchors are typically:
> - **Stone anchors** (historically), 
> - **Admiralty-pattern iron anchors** (later period),
> - **Grapnel** anchors (small craft).

## 2.3 Anchor Rode Composition

The **rode** = **Shackle** → **Chain** → **Swivel** → **Rope/Wire tail** → **Shackle** to boat.

**Rode Selection Rule (KTU 2024):**

$$ \text{Scope Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Rode Length}}{\text{Water Depth at High Tide}} $$

| Water Depth | Recommended Scope |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Sheltered harbour | 3 : 1 |
| Open harbour | 5 : 1 |
| Exposed anchorage | 7 : 1 to 10 : 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why scope?** A short rode (steep angle) pulls the anchor **upward** → fluke disengages. A long rode (shallow angle) pulls the anchor **horizontally** → fluke digs deep.

## 2.4 Mooring System — Theoretical Layout

A traditional mooring system consists of:

1. **Shore-end fitting** — bollard, ring, dolphin, or quay hook.
2. **Mooring line** — manila rope, polypropylene, or polyester (in modern boats).
3. **Vessel-end fitting** — cleat, bitt, or bollard.
4. **Optional springs & breast lines** — control fore-aft and athwartships movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Mooring Line Configuration (Mediterranean Mooring):**
> - **Head line** (45° forward) — prevents backward motion.
> - **Stern line** (45° aft) — prevents forward motion.
> - **Spring forward** (forward, perpendicular) — prevents backward drift.
> - **Spring aft** (aft, perpendicular) — prevents forward drift.
> - **Breast line** (90° to dock) — keeps vessel off the quay.

## 2.5 Deck Fittings — Function-Based Classification

| Function | Fitting | Location |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **Line termination** | Cleat, Bollard, Bitt | Deck, bulwark |
| **Line guidance** | Fairlead, Chock, Panama lead, Roller | Bulwark, stem, stern |
| **Anchor handling** | Hawse pipe, Cat head, Windlass, Chain stopper | Bow, foredeck |
| **Access** | Hatch, Companionway, Scuttle | Deck, coachroof |
| **Safety** | Rail, Stanchion, Life-line, Ladder | Deck perimeter |
| **Ventilation / Light** | Ventilator cowl, Portlight, Deadlight | Coachroof, hull |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Use |
|-----------|---------|-------|-----|
| Holding force (sand) | $F_H = N_c \cdot c_u \cdot A_f$ | kN | Mud holding capacity |
| Holding force (sand) | $F_H = 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot D^2 \cdot K_p \cdot A_f$ | kN | Granular soil |
| Scope ratio | $S = L_r / d$ | — | Rode length design |
| Breaking load safety | $MBL = 3 \times WLL$ | kN | Mooring line design |
| Anchor weight rule | $W_a = (0.005 \text{ to } 0.010) \cdot \Delta$ | kg | Where $\Delta$ = displacement (kg) |
| Bollard pull load | $P = T \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ | kN | Line tension vector |
| Chain studded weight | $w = 0.022 \cdot d^2$ | kg/m | $d$ = chain dia (mm) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Trap:** Students often confuse **$N_c$** (bearing capacity factor, ~9 for deep foundations) with **$N_q$**. For anchor flukes in clay, use **$N_c = 9$** as a conservative value. Always state assumption.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Traditional Boat Building

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU tests this topic:**
> - **Design safety:** Traditional wooden boats use lighter fittings — a wrong cleat strength = snapped mooring = vessel drift.
> - **Heritage preservation:** Traditional *Aranmul* and *Beach landing craft* in Kerala still use mooring line patterns that must be taught correctly.
> - **Load path analysis:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes understanding the **load path from wind → boat → line → fitting → shore**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Working Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scope Ratio for Safe Anchorage

We derive the **minimum rode length** required to keep the rode angle shallow enough that the anchor fluke remains buried.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the rode:**
Let the water depth at high water be $d$ (metres), and the freeboard from water to bow roller be $h$ (metres). The rode is assumed to form a straight catenary approximated as a straight line from bow to seabed attachment.

The angle of the rode with the horizontal is:

$$ \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d + h}{L_h}\right) $$

where $L_h$ is the **horizontal projection** of the rode.

**Step 2 — Condition for anchor embedment:**
For the fluke to remain buried, the vertical component of tension must be less than the soil bearing resistance. Empirically (and per KTU text standards):

$$ \tan(\alpha) \leq \frac{1}{S} $$

where $S$ is the scope ratio.

**Step 3 — Solving for total rode length:**
Total rode length $L_r$ is the hypotenuse of the right triangle:

$$ L_r = \sqrt{(d + h)^2 + L_h^2} $$

For typical small craft where $h \ll d$, we approximate $L_r \approx \sqrt{d^2 + L_h^2}$.

**Step 4 — Apply the scope rule:**
By definition, $L_r = S \cdot d$. Substituting:

$$ S \cdot d = \sqrt{d^2 + L_h^2} $$

Squaring both sides:

$$ S^2 \cdot d^2 = d^2 + L_h^2 $$

Rearranging:

$$ L_h^2 = d^2 (S^2 - 1) $$

**Step 5 — Numerical verification:**
For $S = 5$, $d = 10$ m:

$$ L_h = 10 \cdot \sqrt{25 - 1} = 10 \cdot \sqrt{24} = 10 \cdot 4.899 = 48.99 \text{ m} $$

Total rode:

$$ L_r = 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ m} $$

Vertical lift on anchor:

$$ V = T \cdot \sin(\alpha) = T \cdot \frac{d}{L_r} = T \cdot \frac{10}{50} = 0.2 \, T $$

So only **20 %** of the tension acts upward — the anchor remains buried. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5:1 scope means 80 % of the pull is horizontal (resisted by soil shear), and only 20 % tries to lift the anchor. This is the KTU-engineered logic behind the scope table.

## 3.2 Derivation: Holding Capacity in Cohesive Soil (Mud/Clay)

The fluke is treated as a **strip footing** tilted at the fluke angle $\beta$ to the horizontal.

**Step 1 — Ultimate holding force:**

$$ F_H = N_c \cdot c_u \cdot A_f $$

where:
- $N_c$ = bearing capacity factor (use **9** for strip anchor in clay, KTU conservative).
- $c_u$ = undrained shear strength of clay (kN/m²).
- $A_f$ = projected fluke area (m²).

**Step 2 — Worked example (KTU-style):**
A traditional boat uses an Admiralty anchor with fluke area $A_f = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$ in mud of $c_u = 12 \text{ kN/m}^2$.

$$ F_H = 9 \times 12 \times 0.08 $$

$$ F_H = 108 \times 0.08 = 8.64 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to tonnes (for Board numerical):**

$$ F_H = \frac{8.64}{9.81} = 0.881 \text{ tonne} $$

**Step 4 — Safety check (factor of safety = 2):**

$$ F_{allow} = \frac{0.881}{2} = 0.440 \text{ tonne} $$

This anchor can safely hold a boat up to **0.44 tonnes** in this mud condition.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mooring Line Load Vector

When a line is pulled at an angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, the **load on the bollard** is the sum of the **vertical lift component** and **friction-held horizontal component**.

**Step 1 — Tension in the line:**

$$ P = T \cdot (\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta) $$

where:
- $T$ = line tension (kN).
- $\theta$ = angle of line from bollard base to mooring hook.
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (~0.2 to 0.3 for rope on metal).

**Step 2 — Numerical (KTU-type):**
If $T = 20 \text{ kN}$, $\theta = 30°$, $\mu = 0.25$:

$$ P = 20 \cdot (\sin 30° + 0.25 \cdot \cos 30°) $$

$$ P = 20 \cdot (0.500 + 0.25 \cdot 0.866) $$

$$ P = 20 \cdot (0.500 + 0.217) $$

$$ P = 20 \cdot 0.717 = 14.33 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Selection of cleat:**
Select a cleat with **WLL ≥ 14.33 kN**. Standard cleat ratings jump in steps (e.g., 10 kN, 20 kN, 35 kN). Hence choose **20 kN** cleat.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision (KTU Board Ready)

| Vessel Type | Displacement | Anchor Type | Rode | Recommended Cleat |
|-------------|--------------|-------------|------|-------------------|
| Dugout canoe (Vallam) | 200 – 500 kg | 5 kg grapnel | 30 m rope | 10 kN cleat |
| Country boat (small) | 1 – 2 t | 10 kg Admiralty | 50 m rope + 5 m chain | 20 kN cleat |
| Beach-landing craft | 5 – 10 t | 25 kg Danforth | 80 m chain-rode | 50 kN cleat |
| Mini trawler | 15 – 30 t | 50 kg Bruce | 100 m chain | 100 kN bollard |
| Sea-going traditional vessel | 50 – 100 t | 100 – 200 kg plough | 200 m chain | 150 kN bitt |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule-of-thumb for anchor weight (Brown's formula, used in KTU boards):**
>
> $$ W_{anchor} = k \cdot \Delta $$
>
> with $k = 0.005$ for sheltered waters, $k = 0.010$ for open sea. For a 20-tonne boat in open water: $W = 0.010 \times 20{,}000 = 200$ kg. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anchor System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Hull] --> B[Bow Roller / Hawse Pipe]
    B --> C[Anchor Shackle]
    C --> D[Anchor Rode]
    D --> E[Anchor Swivel]
    E --> F[Anchor Shank]
    F --> G[Anchor Fluke]
    G --> H[Seabed Soil]
    I[Windlass / Chain Stopper] --> B
    J[Hawse Pipe Gasket] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A → B** → Load enters the vessel.
- **D (Rode)** → Load transferred to bottom.
- **G (Fluke)** → Energy dissipated in soil.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mooring Line Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shore Bollard] --> B[Head Line]
    A --> C[Breast Line]
    A --> D[Spring Forward]
    A --> E[Spring Aft]
    A --> F[Stern Line]
    B --> G[Vessel Bow Cleat]
    C --> H[Vessel Midship Cleat]
    D --> I[Vessel Forward Fairlead]
    E --> J[Vessel Aft Fairlead]
    F --> K[Vessel Stern Cleat]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Functional Block Diagram of Deck Fittings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SEC1[Line Termination Group]
        A1[Cleat]
        A2[Bollard]
        A3[Bitt]
    end
    subgraph SEC2[Line Guidance Group]
        B1[Fairlead]
        B2[Chock]
        B3[Roller]
        B4[Panama Lead]
    end
    subgraph SEC3[Anchor Handling Group]
        C1[Hawse Pipe]
        C2[Windlass]
        C3[Chain Stopper]
        C4[Cat Head]
    end
    subgraph SEC4[Access & Safety Group]
        D1[Hatch]
        D2[Companionway]
        D3[Stanchion]
        D4[Life Rail]
    end
    LOAD[Mooring/Anchor Line Load] --> SEC1
    LOAD --> SEC2
    ANCHOR[Anchor Assembly] --> SEC3
    CREW[Crew Movement] --> SEC4
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Load Path in a Traditional Kerala Vallam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wind on Sail] --> B[Mast Reaction]
    B --> C[Hull Drift Force]
    C --> D[Mooring Line Tension]
    D --> E[Bow Cleat]
    E --> F[Deck Plank]
    F --> G[Keelson / Frame]
    G --> H[Hull Planking]
    H --> I[Displacement to Water]
    D --> J[Stern Cleat]
    J --> F
```

**KTU Insight:** Every deck fitting must be **backed by a wooden pad or metal doubler** inside the hull — without this, the cleat will **rip out** of the plank under load.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between anchoring and mooring. List two deck fittings used in each operation.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Anchoring** is the temporary securing of a vessel to the **seabed** using an anchor and rode, dependent on bottom soil strength.
- **Mooring** is the securing of a vessel to a **fixed shore structure or buoy** using lines, independent of seabed condition.
- **Anchor fittings:** hawse pipe, cat head, windlass, chain stopper.
- **Mooring fittings:** cleat, bollard, bitt, fairlead, chock.
*(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction + 1.5 for fittings list.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**What is scope ratio? Why is a 5:1 to 7:1 ratio recommended for normal anchorages?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Scope ratio** = total rode length ÷ water depth.
- A higher scope ratio makes the rode **shallow and horizontal**, which reduces the **vertical lift component** on the anchor.
- A 5:1 to 7:1 ratio keeps the **fluke buried** in the seabed and maximises holding by converting most of the tension into horizontal soil shear resistance.
*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for justification.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose Either A or B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]**

**Q. (a)** With neat sketches, explain the working principle of an **Admiralty anchor**. Discuss its suitability for traditional wooden boat hulls. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Construction:** The Admiralty anchor consists of:
- A **shank** (long central shaft).
- A **stock** (transverse wooden or iron cross-bar at the top of the shank).
- Two **flukes** (flat triangular blades) at the bottom, joined to the shank through a **crown** and **arms**.
- A **ring or shackle** at the top end (called the *shank eye*) for rode attachment.

(ii) **Working Principle:**
- When the anchor is dropped, it falls with one fluke leading.
- As the rode becomes taut under the boat's pull, the shank is dragged along the seabed, causing the lower fluke to dig into the soil.
- The fluke tip penetrates, and the anchor gradually **rolls** until the fluke is fully buried.
- Holding force is generated by soil shear on the fluke surface.

(iii) **Suitability for traditional wooden boats:**
- Heavy and simple in design — easy to manufacture in a local foundry.
- Excellent holding in **soft mud and clay** — typical of Kerala backwaters and estuaries.
- Robust enough to be carried in the **traditional chain-pipe (hawse hole)** cut into the bow of vallams and dhonis.
- The wooden stock can be **locally replaced** — a key advantage for traditional boatbuilders without easy access to marine hardware stores.
- Disadvantage: heavy and difficult to stow — usually stored on deck or hung on a **cat davit** on larger vessels.

**Q. (b)** Describe the **components of a mooring system** for a traditional boat, including the function of each fitting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Shore-end fittings:**
- **Bollard / Mooring ring / Dolphin** — fixed structure on the jetty to take the line end.

(ii) **Mooring lines:**
- **Head line, stern line** — prevent fore-aft drift.
- **Breast line** — holds boat alongside.
- **Spring lines (forward & aft)** — prevent fore-aft motion.
- Material: manila, sisal (traditional), polypropylene/polyester (modern).

(iii) **Vessel-end fittings:**
- **Cleat** — horn-shaped, takes multiple wraps of rope.
- **Bollard** — vertical post, used for heavy mooring.
- **Bitt** — paired vertical posts for taking heavy spring lines.
- **Fairlead** — guides the line to change direction with low friction.
- **Chock** — U-shaped opening in the bulwark for the line to pass through.
- **Roller / Panama lead** — large-radius lead for heavy synthetic ropes.

(iv) **Load Path:** Wind on hull → hull drift → mooring line tension → cleat → backing pad → hull frame → keelson.

**Working sketch (board):**
Draw bow, cleat with figure-8 wrap, line passing through chock, ending at jetty bollard.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **classification of anchors** based on holding principle. List at least **four anchor types** with their **best-suited bottom conditions**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification by holding principle:**

(i) **Fluke-type anchors** — hold by penetration of a fluke/blade into the soil.
- *Examples:* Admiralty, Danforth, Bruce, Plough, CQR.
- *Suited for:* Sand, mud, clay (penetrable bottoms).

(ii) **Multi-fluke / grapnel-type** — hold by biting into crevices.
- *Examples:* Grapnel (4-fluke), Bruce claw.
- *Suited for:* Rocky, weedy, coral bottoms.

(iii) **Deadweight / suction-type** — hold by sheer weight and suction.
- *Examples:* Mushroom anchor, kedge.
- *Suited for:* Soft mud, permanent mooring in calm water.

(iv) **Pivoting-shank anchors** — fluke pivots relative to shank to align with load.
- *Examples:* CQR plough, Bruce.
- *Suited for:* Mixed bottoms, weed, sand.

**Tabular answer (board):**

| Anchor Type | Holding Principle | Best Suited Bottom |
|-------------|------------------|--------------------|
| Admiralty | Fluke penetration | Mud, clay |
| Danforth | Wide fluke area | Sand, soft mud |
| Grapnel | 4-fluke bite | Rock, weed, coral |
| Mushroom | Deadweight + suction | Soft mud (permanent) |
| Bruce | Claw roll-in | Mixed bottoms |

**Q. (b)** A traditional country boat of displacement **12 tonnes** is to be anchored in a sheltered backwater. Using the formula $W_a = 0.005 \cdot \Delta$, find the **required anchor weight** and the **minimum rode length** for a depth of **4 m**. Use a **5:1 scope**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Anchor weight:**

$$ W_a = 0.005 \cdot \Delta = 0.005 \cdot 12{,}000 \text{ kg} $$

$$ W_a = 60 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2 — Rode length:**

$$ L_r = S \cdot d = 5 \cdot 4 = 20 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Rode composition (traditional practice):**
- 5 m of **galvanised chain** (12 mm studded) + 15 m of **16 mm polypropylene rope**, OR
- 20 m of all-rope rode for very small craft.

**Step 4 — Selection of anchor type:**
A **60 kg Admiralty** or **25 kg Bruce with 60 kg equivalent holding** is appropriate. Since Bruce has 2–3× the holding per kg of weight, a 25 kg Bruce suffices in soft mud.

**Step 5 — Safety check:**
Working tension on rode (assume wind load 5 kN on hull):

$$ T = \frac{F}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{5}{\cos(\tan^{-1}(1/5))} = \frac{5}{0.9806} = 5.10 \text{ kN} $$

This is well within the breaking load of 16 mm polypropylene (~40 kN). ✅

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing scope ratio with shackle safety** — scope is about rode length vs depth, not safety factor.
> 2. **Forgetting the backing pad** — a cleat without internal hull pad is *not* a complete answer; examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "best-suited bottom"** — anchor type is *useless* without specifying the bottom condition.
> 4. **Unit errors in Brown's formula** — displacement $\Delta$ is in **kg**, not tonnes, unless the formula is adjusted.
> 5. **No diagram** — KTU ESE for this module **demands** at least one labelled sketch. Skipping it = −2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong sign in $N_c$ value** — for soft mud, $N_c = 9$ is the *conservative* KTU value; using 5.7 (shallow foundation) loses marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Anchor = seabed grip; Mooring = shore tie-up; Deck fitting = hardware on deck.**
- ✅ **Holding force in clay:** $F_H = 9 \cdot c_u \cdot A_f$.
- ✅ **Brown's anchor weight rule:** $W_a = (0.005$ to $0.010) \cdot \Delta$ (kg).
- ✅ **Scope ratio:** 3:1 (sheltered) → 7:1 to 10:1 (exposed).
- ✅ **Mooring line MBL ≥ 3 × WLL** (safety factor 3).
- ✅ **Mediterranean mooring lines:** Head + Stern + 2 Springs + Breast.
- ✅ **Cleat** = small line (≤ 30 kN); **Bollard** = heavy line; **Bitt** = spring lines.
- ✅ **Hawse pipe + chain stopper + windlass** = anchor handling trio.
- ✅ **Mushroom anchor** → permanent mooring buoy (suction principle).
- ✅ **Always back a cleat with a wooden pad or doubler plate** — *traditional KTU rule for wooden hulls.*
- ✅ **Anchor rode = Shackle → Chain → Swivel → Rope → Shackle.**
- ✅ **Units to remember:** kN (force), mm (chain dia), m (rode length), kg (anchor weight).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-line KTU Examiner's Note:**
> *"Any answer that doesn't connect the fitting to its **load path** through the hull is incomplete. Always end with: 'This fitting transfers the load from the line → deck → frame → keelson → hull → water.'"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Painting of the Boat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Painting of the Boat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boat Painting** is the systematic application of protective, decorative, and functional coatings on the hull, deck, superstructure, and interior surfaces of a boat to ensure its longevity, hydrodynamic efficiency, and resistance against harsh marine environments. It is a critical finishing operation in traditional and modern boat building that integrates principles of surface chemistry, materials science, and marine biology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST864 – Module 2):**
> "Painting of the boat refers to the sequential application of primers, fillers, undercoats, anti-fouling compositions, and finish coats on a properly prepared boat surface, using traditional (e.g., cashew nut shell liquid, fish oil, tar) or modern (e.g., epoxy, polyurethane) coating systems, to achieve waterproofing, anti-fouling, UV resistance, corrosion protection, and aesthetic finish."

## 1.2 Why Painting is Non-Negotiable in Boat Building

A wooden boat is constantly under attack from **three primary enemies**:
1. **Water & moisture** → causes swelling, rot, and fungal decay
2. **Marine organisms** → borers (Teredo navalis), barnacles, algae, slime
3. **Sunlight (UV)** → breaks down lignin, fades wood, cracks varnish

Without paint, a typical Kerala *vallam* would last only **2–3 years**; with proper painting, its life extends to **15–25+ years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term – Anti-Fouling:**
> Anti-fouling paint is a special coating applied below the waterline that slowly releases biocides (traditionally copper/cuprous oxide, now TBT-free) to prevent marine organisms from attaching to the hull. Fouling can increase drag by up to **60%** and fuel consumption by **30%**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Boat as a Human Skin"

Think of a boat's hull as **human skin**:
- **Primer** = Moisturizer (prepares the surface, seals pores)
- **Putty/Filler** = Band-Aid (fills cracks, smooths imperfections)
- **Undercoat** = Foundation cream (uniform base color, hides flaws)
- **Topcoat** = Sunscreen + Makeup (UV protection + looks beautiful)
- **Anti-fouling** = Insect repellent (keeps "bugs" — barnacles — away)

Just as we apply skincare in layers, boat painting is a **multi-layer system**, not a single coat job.

## 1.4 Standard Boat Painting Zones (Critical Concept)

A boat is divided into **four distinct painting zones**, each requiring a different coating system:

| Zone | Location | Paint Type | Function |
|------|----------|------------|----------|
| **Bottom** | Below waterline | Anti-fouling paint | Prevent biofouling |
| **Boot-top** | 25–30 cm band above waterline | Boot-topping paint | Tide-zone protection |
| **Topsides** | Above waterline, hull sides | Marine enamel/epoxy | UV + aesthetics |
| **Deck & Superstructure** | Top surfaces | Non-slip deck paint | Traction + weather |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often forget the **boot-topping** zone. It is the area alternately wet and dry due to wave action and load changes — a high-wear zone requiring extra-thick coating.

## 1.5 The Painting System Hierarchy

Painting is not just "applying color." It follows a strict **layered architecture**:

$$
\text{Surface} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Sanding} \rightarrow \text{Priming} \rightarrow \text{Putty/Filling} \rightarrow \text{Undercoating} \rightarrow \text{Topcoating} \rightarrow \text{Anti-fouling (if below WL)}
$$

Each layer has a specific **dry film thickness (DFT)**, **drying time**, and **purpose** — discussed in detail in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Painting Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

### **Stage 1: Surface Preparation (The Most Critical Step — 70% of Quality)**

> [!WARNING]
> **"A paint job is only as good as the surface preparation."** — This is the golden rule. **80% of paint failures** are due to poor surface prep, not bad paint.

**Sub-steps:**
1. **De-wiring/De-greasing** — Remove oil, wax, salt using turpentine or solvent
2. **Sanding** — Use sandpaper in progression: **80-grit → 120-grit → 220-grit** (coarse to fine)
3. **Dust removal** — Tack cloth or compressed air
4. **Drying** — Wood moisture content must be **< 15%** before priming
5. **Caulking** — Filling seams between planks with cotton + marine glue or polysulfide sealant

**Why it matters:** Paint adheres to surface by **mechanical interlocking** and **chemical bonding**. Any dust/oil prevents this.

---

### **Stage 2: Priming**

**Primer** is the first paint coat, designed to:
- Penetrate wood pores
- Provide adhesion for topcoats
- Seal the surface
- Block tannins (in teak, oak) from bleeding

**Common Primers Used in Boat Building:**

| Primer Type | Base | Use Case | DFT (microns) |
|-------------|------|----------|---------------|
| Wood primer (oil-based) | Alkyd | Traditional wooden hulls | 30–40 |
| Zinc chromate primer | Epoxy/alkyd | Metal fittings, ferrous parts | 25–35 |
| Epoxy primer | Two-part epoxy | High-end, water barrier | 50–100 |
| Red oxide primer | Iron oxide | Rust prevention (steel parts) | 25–40 |

---

### **Stage 3: Putty / Filling (Body Work)**

**Purpose:** Fill dents, nail holes, grain pores, and minor surface defects.

- **Traditional putty:** Linseed oil + chalk powder + white lead (rarely used now)
- **Modern putty:** Polyester putty, epoxy filler
- **Wood filler:** Cellulose-based for fine grain

Applied with a **putty knife**, sanded smooth after curing (**4–6 hours**).

---

### **Stage 4: Undercoating**

An intermediate layer that:
- Builds film thickness
- Provides uniform color base
- Hides primer/putty patches
- Improves topcoat gloss and coverage

Typically **1–2 coats**, each **30–50 microns DFT**, with **8–24 hours** drying between coats.

---

### **Stage 5: Topcoating (Finish Coat)**

The final decorative + protective layer. Options:

| Finish | Material | Pros | Cons |
|--------|----------|------|------|
| **Marine enamel** | Alkyd | Cheap, easy, glossy | Slow dry, UV fades |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Two-part | Hard, durable, UV-resistant | Costly, toxic |
| **Epoxy finish** | Two-part | Waterproof, chemical-resistant | Yellows in UV (needs topcoat) |
| **Varnish (clear)** | Polyurethane/spar | Shows wood grain | High maintenance |
| **Lacquer** | Nitrocellulose | Fast dry, high gloss | Poor outdoor durability |

**Standard:** **2–3 topcoats**, each **40–60 microns DFT**.

---

### **Stage 6: Anti-Fouling (Bottom Paint)**

Applied **only to underwater hull surfaces**, **below the waterline**.

**Mechanism:** Biocide (cuprous oxide Cu₂O, or modern zinc pyrithione) **leaches out slowly** in water, creating a toxic boundary layer that prevents larvae settlement.

**Types of Anti-Fouling:**

| Type | Mechanism | Service Life |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **Contact (conventional)** | Biodegrade top layer | 12–18 months |
| **Controlled Depletion Polymer (CDP)** | Polymers erode steadily | 24–36 months |
| **Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC)** | Hydrolytic smoothing | 36–60 months |
| **Tin-free (TBT-free)** | Environmentally compliant | 36–48 months |

---

## 2.2 Traditional Kerala Boat Painting Materials

Kerala's boat-building tradition uses **indigenous, eco-friendly materials** — a KTU-favorite topic.

| Traditional Material | Source | Function |
|---------------------|--------|----------|
| **Cashew Nut Shell Liquid (CNSL)** | Cashew industry waste | Waterproofing, wood hardening |
| **Fish oil** | Sardine/mackerel oil | Wood preservative, deep penetration |
| **Coconut oil** | Copra | Surface finish, anti-fungal |
| **Neem oil** | Azadirachta indica | Insect/borer repellent |
| **Tar (wood tar)** | Pine/resin distillation | Seam caulking, waterproofing |
| **Turmeric + lime paste** | Curcuma + Ca(OH)₂ | Anti-fungal, bright yellow finish |
| **Charcoal + coconut oil** | Carbon + oil | Black traditional finish |
| **Country paint (mineral)** | Local pigments | Decorative + protective |

> [!NOTE]
> **CNSL Fact:** Cashew Nut Shell Liquid contains **anacardic acid (≈ 90%)**, which is a natural **phenol** with excellent anti-microbial and water-repellent properties. It polymerizes on exposure to air, forming a hard, dark brown film — perfect for boat hulls.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Painting Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These formulas are KTU-exam relevant for estimating paint quantity, coverage area, and film thickness.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{S}{100}$ | Dry Film Thickness from Wet Film | microns |
| 2 | $\text{Coverage Area} = \frac{V \times C}{N}$ | Area covered by paint | m² |
| 3 | $\text{Paint Volume} = \frac{A \times N}{C}$ | Paint required for area | Litres |
| 4 | $\text{WFT} = \frac{\text{DFT} \times 100}{S}$ | Wet film needed for given DFT | microns |
| 5 | $S_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{D_1} \times 100$ | % solids by density difference | % |

**Where:**
- $\text{DFT}$ = Dry Film Thickness
- $\text{WFT}$ = Wet Film Thickness
- $S$ = % Volume Solids of paint
- $V$ = Volume of paint in container (L)
- $C$ = Spreading rate (m²/L) — manufacturer spec, typically **8–12 m²/L**
- $N$ = Number of coats
- $A$ = Surface area to be painted (m²)
- $D_1$ = Density of wet paint, $D_2$ = Density of dry film

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mermaid/Latex Table Symbol Rule:** Notice we used `\vert` or descriptive words like "of" instead of `\vert x \vert` pipes inside tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax. KTU examiners sometimes test calculation of film thickness — know these formulas cold.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Estimating paint quantity for shipyard cost estimation
- **Maintenance Engineering:** Deciding dry-docking intervals (typically every **2–3 years** for re-painting)
- **Marine Biology Compliance:** Anti-fouling selection now governed by **IMO AFS Convention (2008)** banning TBT
- **Traditional Crafts:** Preserving Kerala's intangible heritage (e.g., *Aranmula Kannadi*–style finish on royal boats)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Paint Quantity Estimation (Classic KTU-Type)

### **Problem Statement (Model Question):**
> A traditional wooden fishing boat has a total hull surface area of **85 m²**. The boat requires **1 coat of primer** and **2 coats of marine enamel** finish. The spreading rates are: Primer = **10 m²/L**, Enamel = **12 m²/L**. How many litres of each paint are required? Assume **10% wastage** factor.

---

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total area $A = 85 \text{ m}^2$
- Primer: 1 coat, $C_p = 10 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$
- Enamel: 2 coats, $C_e = 12 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$
- Wastage factor $W = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 1:** Calculate theoretical paint required for primer.

$$
V_p = \frac{A \times N_p}{C_p} = \frac{85 \times 1}{10} = 8.5 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2:** Add wastage to primer quantity.

$$
V_{p,\text{actual}} = V_p \times (1 + W) = 8.5 \times 1.10 = 9.35 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate theoretical paint for enamel (2 coats).

$$
V_e = \frac{A \times N_e}{C_e} = \frac{85 \times 2}{12} = \frac{170}{12} = 14.1667 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4:** Add wastage to enamel quantity.

$$
V_{e,\text{actual}} = 14.1667 \times 1.10 = 15.583 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 5:** Final answer with practical rounding (always round **up** to nearest whole litre).

$$
\boxed{V_{p,\text{actual}} \approx 10 \text{ L (primer)}, \quad V_{e,\text{actual}} \approx 16 \text{ L (enamel)}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Students often forget the wastage factor in KTU exams. This is a **2-mark trap** in Part A and a **3-mark trap** in Part B calculations. Always mention wastage explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Dry Film Thickness (DFT) Calculation

### **Problem Statement:**
> An epoxy primer has **55% volume solids**. It is applied as a **wet film of 100 microns**. Calculate the **dry film thickness (DFT)**.

---

### **Solution:**

**Given:**
- Wet Film Thickness $\text{WFT} = 100 \text{ microns}$
- Volume Solids $S = 55\%$

**Step 1:** Apply the DFT formula.

$$
\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{S}{100}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{DFT} = 100 \times \frac{55}{100}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
\text{DFT} = 100 \times 0.55
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DFT} = 55 \text{ microns}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** When the solvent evaporates from a 100-micron wet film, only **55 microns of solid resin** remains — the rest is solvent that escapes as vapor. This is why **high-solids paints** are preferred for marine use (more paint, less solvent, lower VOC).

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Reverse Calculation — Finding Number of Coats

### **Problem Statement:**
> A boat's topside requires a total **DFT of 120 microns**. Each coat of marine enamel gives a **DFT of 40 microns** and has **40% volume solids**. The applicator can only achieve a **maximum WFT of 120 microns per coat** (to avoid runs/sags). Determine the **minimum number of coats** required.

---

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the DFT achieved per coat at the maximum WFT.

$$
\text{DFT per coat} = \text{WFT}_{\max} \times \frac{S}{100} = 120 \times \frac{40}{100} = 48 \text{ microns}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the number of coats required to reach 120 microns.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{\text{DFT}_{\text{required}}}{\text{DFT per coat}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{48} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.5 \right\rceil = 3
$$

> (We take the **ceiling** — you cannot apply half a coat; you must round up to ensure minimum DFT is met.)

$$
\boxed{N = 3 \text{ coats}}
$$

**Verification:**
$$
\text{Total DFT} = 3 \times 48 = 144 \text{ microns} \geq 120 \text{ microns} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Paint System Selection Guide

| Boat Type | Primer | Undercoat | Topcoat | Anti-Fouling | Drying Time Total |
|-----------|--------|-----------|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Kerala Vallam** (fishing) | CNSL + wood primer | Country paint | Marine enamel | None (small boats) | 24–48 hrs |
| **Tourist Houseboat** | Epoxy primer | PU undercoat | PU enamel (gloss) | Required | 5–7 days |
| **Speedboat (FRP)** | Epoxy primer | PU surfacer | PU topcoat (gel coat) | SPC anti-fouling | 3–5 days |
| **Cargo Barge** | Zinc chromate | Bituminous | Coal-tar epoxy | CDP anti-fouling | 7–10 days |
| **Royal Snake Boat (Chundan)** | Cashew oil + lime | Turmeric-lime | Natural pigment + coconut oil | None | 2 weeks (curing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cultural Note:** The **Chundan Vallam** (snake boat) of Kerala uses a **traditional yellow-turmeric-lime finish** for its iconic golden appearance, often layered with cashew nut oil and then polished with coconut oil. This is a **UNESCO-recognized intangible heritage** practice and is frequently asked in KTU Part A questions.

---

## 3.5 Drying & Curing Stages — Time-Phased Analysis

| Stage | Process | Time | Reversible? |
|-------|---------|------|-------------|
| **Dust-free** | Surface dry to touch, dust won't stick | **15–30 min** | No |
| **Touch-dry** | No transfer on finger touch | **1–2 hours** | No |
| **Hard-dry** | Can withstand light handling | **6–8 hours** | No |
| **Through-dry** | Full thickness cured | **12–24 hours** | No |
| **Full cure** | Chemical cross-linking complete | **7–14 days** | No (for epoxies/PU) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Re-coating before "through-dry" stage causes **wrinkling, lifting, and solvent entrapment**. Always follow the **re-coat interval** specified on the technical data sheet (TDS) — typically **16–24 hours** for enamels, **8–12 hours** for epoxies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boat Painting Zone Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[BOAT STRUCTURE]:::boat
    nodeB[BELOW WATERLINE ZONE]:::zone1
    nodeC[BOOT TOP ZONE]:::zone2
    nodeD[TOPSIDES ZONE]:::zone3
    nodeE[DECK AND SUPERSTRUCTURE]:::zone4
    nodeF[INTERIOR BILGE]:::zone5
    nodeG[Anti-fouling Paint 2 to 3 coats]:::paint1
    nodeH[Boot-topping Paint 2 coats]:::paint2
    nodeI[Marine Enamel 2 to 3 coats]:::paint3
    nodeJ[Non-skip Deck Paint 2 coats]:::paint4
    nodeK[Bilge Paint 2 coats]:::paint5
    nodeL[Primer 1 coat]:::primer
    nodeM[Putty and Filling]:::prep
    nodeN[Surface Preparation Sanding and Cleaning]:::prep2

    nodeA --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeB
    nodeL --> nodeC
    nodeL --> nodeD
    nodeL --> nodeE
    nodeL --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeK

    classDef boat fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef zone1 fill:#003049,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef zone2 fill:#d62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef zone3 fill:#f77f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef zone4 fill:#fcbf49,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef zone5 fill:#003049,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef paint1 fill:#06a77d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef paint2 fill:#06a77d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef paint3 fill:#06a77d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef paint4 fill:#06a77d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef paint5 fill:#06a77d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef primer fill:#8338ec,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef prep fill:#ffb703,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef prep2 fill:#ffb703,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Multi-Layer Paint System Cross-Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[EXTERIOR ENVIRONMENT Air Water UV Marine Organisms]:::env
    nodeB[TOPCOAT Layer 1 40 to 60 microns DFT]:::top
    nodeC[TOPCOAT Layer 2 40 to 60 microns DFT]:::top
    nodeD[UNDERCOAT 30 to 50 microns DFT]:::under
    nodeE[PUTTY OR FILLER Variable Thickness]:::putty
    nodeF[PRIMER 30 to 50 microns DFT]:::primer2
    nodeG[SUBSTRATE WOOD OR METAL HULL]:::wood

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    classDef env fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef top fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef under fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef putty fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef primer2 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef wood fill:#8d6e63,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Painting Process Workflow (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Step 1 Surface Inspection]:::step --> nodeS2[Step 2 Cleaning and Degreasing]:::step
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Step 3 Sanding 80 to 120 to 220 grit]:::step
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Step 4 Dust Removal Tack Cloth]:::step
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Step 5 Caulking Seams]:::step
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Step 6 Primer Application 1 coat]:::step
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Step 7 Drying 12 to 24 hours]:::step
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Step 8 Putty Application and Sanding]:::step
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Step 9 Undercoat 1 to 2 coats]:::step
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Step 10 Topcoat 2 to 3 coats]:::step
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11{Is it Below Waterline?}:::decision
    nodeS11 -->|YES| nodeS12[Step 11 Anti-fouling 2 to 3 coats]:::step
    nodeS11 -->|NO| nodeS13[Step 12 Final Inspection and Curing 7 days]:::step
    nodeS12 --> nodeS13

    classDef step fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#e63946,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define anti-fouling paint. Why is it applied only below the waterline of a boat?**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Anti-fouling paint is a specialized marine coating containing biocides (such as cuprous oxide, cuprous thiocyanate, or modern TBT-free compounds) that is applied to the underwater hull of a boat. Its primary function is to prevent the attachment and growth of marine organisms such as barnacles, algae, mussels, and slime on the hull surface.

**Why Below Waterline Only (1.5 Marks):**
- Anti-fouling biocides are designed to **leach out in water**; they are only effective when submerged.
- Above the waterline, the paint would **dry out and become inert**, losing its function.
- It is **unnecessarily expensive** to apply on topsides where no fouling occurs.
- Anti-fouling paints often have **poor UV resistance** and would degrade quickly in sunlight, causing aesthetic issues.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six traditional materials used for painting boats in Kerala. Mention their functions.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Material | Function |
|---|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Cashew Nut Shell Liquid (CNSL)** | Waterproofing, wood hardening, anti-microbial |
| 2 | **Fish oil** | Deep penetration as wood preservative |
| 3 | **Neem oil** | Natural insect and borer repellent |
| 4 | **Coconut oil** | Surface finish, anti-fungal, polishing |
| 5 | **Turmeric + Lime paste** | Bright yellow finish, anti-fungal properties |
| 6 | **Wood tar / Pine tar** | Seam caulking, waterproofing joints |

*(Any 6 with functions = 3 marks; 1 mark partial for listing without functions.)*

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**
### *As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer ONE of TWO internal choices.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **complete step-by-step procedure for painting a newly constructed wooden boat**, covering surface preparation, priming, filling, undercoating, and topcoating. Include the function of each layer and recommended drying times. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wooden fishing boat has a **hull area of 60 m²** (below waterline) and a **topsides area of 45 m²**. The primer coverage is **8 m²/L**; the anti-fouling paint coverage is **6 m²/L**; the marine enamel coverage is **10 m²/L**. The painting schedule requires: 1 primer coat on the **entire boat (105 m²)**, 2 anti-fouling coats on the **bottom (60 m²)**, and 2 enamel coats on the **topsides (45 m²)**. Calculate the total paint requirement **including 15% wastage**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Surface Preparation (2 Marks):**
- Inspect hull for defects, loose fibers, and knots
- Sand progressively: **80-grit → 120-grit → 220-grit** sandpaper
- Degrease with turpentine or mineral spirits
- Allow wood to dry to moisture content **< 15%**
- Caulk all seams with cotton + marine glue

**2. Priming (1.5 Marks):**
- Apply **1 coat of wood primer** (oil-based or epoxy) using brush or roller
- Functions: seals wood, blocks tannins, provides adhesion
- Dry film thickness: **30–40 microns**
- Drying time: **12–24 hours**

**3. Putty/Filling (1 Mark):**
- Apply putty on dents, nail holes, grain pores
- Sand smooth with **220-grit** after **4–6 hours** curing
- Wipe clean with tack cloth

**4. Undercoating (1 Mark):**
- Apply **1–2 coats** of undercoat
- Functions: builds film thickness, uniform color, hides patches
- DFT: **30–50 microns** per coat
- Drying: **8–24 hours** between coats

**5. Topcoating (1.5 Marks):**
- Apply **2–3 coats** of marine enamel or PU finish
- Functions: UV protection, aesthetics, weatherproofing
- DFT: **40–60 microns** per coat
- Final curing: **7 days** before launch

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Bottom area: $A_b = 60 \text{ m}^2$
- Topsides area: $A_t = 45 \text{ m}^2$
- Total primed area: $A_p = A_b + A_t = 60 + 45 = 105 \text{ m}^2$
- Spreading rates: $C_{\text{primer}} = 8 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$, $C_{AF} = 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$, $C_{\text{enamel}} = 10 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$
- Wastage: $W = 15\% = 0.15$

**Step 1: Primer Quantity (1 coat on 105 m²)**

$$
V_{\text{primer}} = \frac{A_p \times N_p}{C_{\text{primer}}} = \frac{105 \times 1}{8} = 13.125 \text{ L}
$$

With wastage:

$$
V_{\text{primer, actual}} = 13.125 \times 1.15 = 15.094 \text{ L} \approx \mathbf{16 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 2: Anti-fouling Quantity (2 coats on 60 m²)**

$$
V_{AF} = \frac{A_b \times N_{AF}}{C_{AF}} = \frac{60 \times 2}{6} = 20 \text{ L}
$$

With wastage:

$$
V_{AF,\text{actual}} = 20 \times 1.15 = 23 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3: Enamel Quantity (2 coats on 45 m²)**

$$
V_{\text{enamel}} = \frac{A_t \times N_e}{C_{\text{enamel}}} = \frac{45 \times 2}{10} = 9 \text{ L}
$$

With wastage:

$$
V_{\text{enamel, actual}} = 9 \times 1.15 = 10.35 \text{ L} \approx \mathbf{11 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 4: Total Paint Required**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 16 + 23 + 11 = \boxed{50 \text{ L}}
$$

| Paint Type | Quantity (with wastage) |
|------------|-------------------------|
| Primer | **16 L** |
| Anti-fouling | **23 L** |
| Marine Enamel | **11 L** |
| **TOTAL** | **50 L** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **traditional Kerala boat painting methods**, comparing them with modern synthetic paint systems. Highlight the role of **Cashew Nut Shell Liquid (CNSL)** and the cultural significance of **Chundan Vallam (snake boat) painting**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A marine epoxy primer has **60% volume solids** and is applied at a **wet film thickness of 90 microns** per coat. The total dry film thickness specification for the primer layer is **135 microns**. Determine:
   1. The dry film thickness achieved per coat. **(3 Marks)**
   2. The minimum number of coats required. **(2 Marks)**
   3. If the boat has a primed area of **120 m²** and the primer can is **5 L** with coverage **10 m²/L** per coat, how many cans are needed for 2 coats? **(2 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Traditional Kerala Methods (3 Marks):**
- Use of **CNSL (Cashew Nut Shell Liquid)** as a primary wood hardener and waterproofer — applied hot and polymerizes on cooling
- **Neem oil and fish oil** as natural preservatives against borers
- **Turmeric-lime paste** for the iconic golden-yellow finish of snake boats
- **Coconut oil** for final polishing — gives a smooth, water-repellent sheen
- **Wood tar** for caulking plank seams
- Multiple coats with hand-rubbing between layers, often over **2 weeks** of curing

**2. Modern Synthetic Methods (2 Marks):**
- Epoxy primers + PU topcoats → superior water resistance and durability
- Anti-fouling paints with copper biocides (TBT-free after IMO 2008)
- Spray application for uniform thickness
- Faster drying (8–12 hours between coats vs. days for traditional)

**3. CNSL and Chundan Vallam Significance (2 Marks):**
- CNSL contains **anacardic acid** which polymerizes into a hard, dark, water-resistant film
- It is **eco-friendly**, a **byproduct of cashew industry**, and supports **circular economy**
- Chundan Vallams (snake boats) of Kerala — used in **Vallamkali** (boat race) — are painted with turmeric-lime and polished with coconut oil, making them **cultural icons**
- The traditional finish also has **spiritual value**, believed to please the river goddess

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Volume Solids $S = 60\%$
- Wet Film Thickness $\text{WFT} = 90 \text{ microns}$
- Required DFT = 135 microns
- Primed area $A = 120 \text{ m}^2$
- Coverage $C = 10 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$ per coat
- Can size = 5 L
- 2 coats required

**Part (i) — DFT per Coat (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{S}{100} = 90 \times \frac{60}{100}
$$

$$
\text{DFT per coat} = 90 \times 0.60 = \mathbf{54 \text{ microns}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Minimum Number of Coats (2 Marks):**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{\text{DFT}_{\text{required}}}{\text{DFT per coat}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{135}{54} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \lceil 2.5 \rceil = \mathbf{3 \text{ coats}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Number of Cans Required (2 Marks):**

Paint required for 2 coats on 120 m²:

$$
V = \frac{A \times N}{C} = \frac{120 \times 2}{10} = 24 \text{ L}
$$

Number of cans:

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{V}{\text{Can size}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{24}{5} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = \mathbf{5 \text{ cans}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** — Students write "2.5 coats" instead of rounding up to 3. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Ignoring wastage factor** — In real problems, always add 10–15% wastage. If the examiner says "with 10% wastage," you must apply it to BOTH volume and number of cans.
> 3. **Mixing up WFT and DFT** — WFT is **always greater** than DFT. If your answer shows DFT > WFT, you've applied the formula wrong.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert % to decimal** — Many students write $90 \times 60$ instead of $90 \times 0.60$. Both give the same number, but showing the conversion step fetches a method mark.

---

## **Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Memory Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ Painting = **Surface Prep + Primer + Putty + Undercoat + Topcoat (+ Anti-fouling if below WL)**
- ✅ **Four zones** of a boat: Bottom, Boot-top, Topsides, Deck — **each needs different paint**
- ✅ **Boot-top** = 25–30 cm band above waterline (tide zone, high wear)
- ✅ **Anti-fouling** is applied **only below waterline**; uses **Cu₂O** biocide (TBT banned since 2008)
- ✅ **CNSL** = Cashew Nut Shell Liquid — Kerala's traditional waterproofing marvel
- ✅ **DFT = WFT × (% Solids / 100)** — most important formula
- ✅ **Paint quantity** = (Area × Number of coats) / Spreading rate **+ wastage**
- ✅ **Drying stages**: Dust-free (30 min) → Touch-dry (2 hr) → Hard-dry (8 hr) → Full cure (7–14 days)
- ✅ Traditional finishes for **Chundan Vallam** = turmeric + lime + coconut oil polish
- ✅ **Number of coats** = always round **UP** (ceiling function) — never apply half a coat
- ✅ **70% of paint quality** depends on surface preparation — sanding grits: 80 → 120 → 220
- ✅ **Spreading rate** typically: Primer ≈ 8–10 m²/L, Enamel ≈ 10–12 m²/L, Anti-fouling ≈ 6–8 m²/L
- ✅ Always include **10–15% wastage** in practical paint quantity calculations
- ✅ **VOC** (Volatile Organic Compounds) are lower in high-solids paints — better for marine environment
- ✅ Marine paint layers act like **human skin** — moisturizer (primer) → foundation (undercoat) → sunscreen (topcoat) → repellent (anti-fouling)

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Painting of the Boat**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) for PENST864 — Traditional Boat Building Techniques.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
