```mermaid
    style HERIT fill:#f3e5f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** The four-layer architecture above is a favourite KTU board diagram. Students are often asked to *draw and label* a similar block diagram and explain the linkage between legal, technical, safety, and heritage documentation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q: Define the term "warranty" as applicable to a traditionally built wooden boat. List any TWO types of warranties commonly issued.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **warranty** is a formal, legally binding assurance given by the boat builder to the purchaser, promising that the vessel or its components will remain free from defects in materials and workmanship for a specified period, with remedies provided if defects occur.

**Two types of warranties:**

1. **Hull Structural Warranty** — Covers planking, frames, keel, stem, and sternpost for 5–10 years.
2. **Material Warranty** — Covers supplied timber, fasteners, and caulking compounds for 1–5 years.

*(Alternate acceptable types: Workmanship Warranty, Paint/Coating Warranty, Implied Warranty of Seaworthiness.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: What is a "Builder's Specification Manual" and why is it important for traditional boat building?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Builder's Specification Manual** is a comprehensive technical document that records all construction details of a traditional boat — including timber species used, joinery methods, fastening schedules, dimensions, and supplier information.

**Importance:**

1. **Restoration Reference:** Future restorers and surveyors can faithfully rebuild or repair the vessel using the original specifications.
2. **Heritage Preservation:** It documents traditional craft knowledge for future apprentices, supporting UNESCO ICH frameworks.
3. **Warranty Support:** It serves as the technical baseline against which warranty claims for workmanship defects are evaluated.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *warranty* with *guarantee* — a 1-mark question becomes 0 if you interchange the definitions.
> 2. **Do not** omit the *implied* warranty of seaworthiness — it is a favourite examiner's check question.
> 3. When listing manual sections, **use the KTU 6-section reference model** (A–F). Free-form lists lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. For numerical problems, **show every algebraic step**. KTU examiners mark intermediate steps, not just the final answer.
> 5. Heritage/Cultural significance is **not optional** — the KTU rubric for PENST864 explicitly allocates marks for heritage awareness.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Pattern]`

**Q (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different types of warranties applicable to a traditional wooden boat built in Kerala. Discuss the implied warranty of seaworthiness and its significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the structure and contents of a comprehensive Owner's Manual for a traditional *Vallam* (country boat), using the KTU 6-section reference model.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Warranties [7 Marks]:**

The following types of warranties are typically issued for a traditional wooden boat:

1. **Hull Structural Warranty (2 marks):** Covers planking, frames, keel, stem, and sternpost. Typical duration: **5–10 years**. This is the most critical warranty as structural failure at sea endangers life.

2. **Material Warranty (2 marks):** Covers the timber (e.g., *Anjili*, *Plaavu*, *Vembu*), fasteners (copper rivets, galvanized nails), and caulking compounds. Typical duration: **1–5 years**. The moisture content at installation should not exceed **18%**.

3. **Workmanship Warranty (1 mark):** Covers defects arising from poor joinery, improper fastening, or inadequate caulking. Typical duration: **2–5 years**.

4. **Paint/Coating Warranty (1 mark):** Covers anti-fouling paint, varnish, and primer adhesion. Typical duration: **1–3 years**.

5. **Implied Warranty of Seaworthiness (1 mark):** Under common maritime law, every vessel sold carries an *implied* promise that it is fit to be used for its intended purpose on water. Even if the builder writes no warranty at all, this implied warranty may still apply. Its **significance** is that it protects the owner even in the absence of express contractual terms, and it reflects the boat builder's professional duty.

**(b) Owner's Manual for a Vallam — KTU 6-Section Model [7 Marks]:**

| Section | Content (1 mark each, plus 1 mark for integration) |
|---------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Section A — Vessel Particulars** | Boat name, registration number, builder's mark, principal dimensions (LOA, beam, depth), year of construction. |
| **Section B — Construction Record** | Timber species used (e.g., *Anjili* for planking, *Plaavu* for frames), joinery details (carvel or clinker), fastening schedule, caulking material. |
| **Section C — Operating Instructions** | Launching procedure (use of slipway or rollers), mooring protocol, basic navigation rules for Kerala backwaters, max passenger/load capacity. |
| **Section D — Maintenance Schedule** | Daily checks (bilge, leaks), weekly checks (rigging, oarlocks), annual maintenance (anti-fouling repaint, varnish coat), 5-yearly haul-out inspection. |
| **Section E — Safety Procedures** | Life jackets count and locations, fire extinguisher placement, distress signal usage (flares, whistle), emergency contact numbers. |
| **Section F — Heritage Note** | Lineage of the Vallam design, regional significance (Kerala backwater transport), cultural role in festivals and trade. |

The integration mark is awarded for explaining how these six sections **work together** to form a complete owner's manual.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Alternative Pattern]`

**Q (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure for processing a warranty claim for a traditional boat. Include a flow diagram in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A traditional fishing boat has a material warranty of 6 years and a workmanship warranty of 4 years. The owner uses the boat for 900 hours/year against a rated maximum of 1200 hours/year. Calculate: (i) Base warranty duration, (ii) Usage intensity ratio, (iii) Effective warranty period. State the implications of the result.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Warranty Claim Procedure [7 Marks]:**

The **KTU-recommended 7-step procedure** (based on ISO 10002) is:

| Step | Action | Marks |
|------|--------|-------|
| 1 | **Notify** — Owner informs builder within 7 days of defect discovery. | 1 |
| 2 | **Document** — Photograph defect, preserve purchase invoice, record usage logs. | 1 |
| 3 | **Inspect** — Builder's surveyor or naval architect inspects the vessel. | 1 |
| 4 | **Cause-Analyze** — Determine if defect is from material, workmanship, or owner misuse. | 1 |
| 5 | **Coverage-Check** — Verify against warranty terms (using the voiding condition index). | 1 |
| 6 | **Estimate** — Compute repair cost and timeline. | 1 |
| 7 | **Settle** — Execute repair, replace, or refund as per warranty terms. | 1 |

**Flow Diagram (text form, 1 mark):**
`NOTIFY → DOCUMENT → INSPECT → CAUSE-ANALYZE → COVERAGE-CHECK → ESTIMATE → SETTLE`

**(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- Material warranty: $W_m = 6$ years
- Workmanship warranty: $W_w = 4$ years
- Actual usage: $U_m = 900$ hours/year
- Rated maximum usage: $U_{max} = 1200$ hours/year

**(i) Base Warranty Duration [2 marks]:**

$$W_d = \min(W_m, W_w) = \min(6, 4) = 4 \text{ years}$$

**(ii) Usage Intensity Ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\eta = \frac{U_m}{U_{max}} = \frac{900}{1200} = 0.75$$

**(iii) Effective Warranty Period [3 marks]:**

$$W_{eff} = W_d \cdot \left(1 - \eta\right) = 4 \cdot (1 - 0.75) = 4 \cdot 0.25 = 1.0 \text{ year}$$

**Implications [Bonus 1 mark — integrated into the 3 marks for part (iii)]:**

The effective warranty usable by the owner is reduced from the base 4 years to just **1 year** because the boat is being used at 75% of its rated maximum capacity. This means:

- The owner must complete any major warranty claims **within the first year**.
- The builder is justified in offering only 1 year of *effective* coverage for such heavy-use scenarios.
- The owner should consider **upgrading to a higher-rated vessel** or **reducing usage** to preserve warranty value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- ✅ **Warranty** = legal assurance; **Manual** = technical documentation. Never interchange.
- ✅ **Express** vs. **Implied** warranty — both can coexist; the implied warranty of seaworthiness often exists even without written terms.
- ✅ **Five warranty types** to remember: Hull Structural, Material, Workmanship, Paint/Coating, Implied Seaworthiness.
- ✅ **Six manual sections** per KTU model: A (Particulars), B (Construction), C (Operations), D (Maintenance), E (Safety), F (Heritage).
- ✅ **Effective warranty formula:** $W_{eff} = W_d \cdot (1 - U_m/U_{max})$ — favourite KTU 14-mark numerical.
- ✅ **Documentation Completeness Ratio:** $D_c = \frac{N_{present}}{N_{required}} \times 100\%$ — answer with grade band.
- ✅ **Warranty Voiding Index:** $V_i = \sum w_k \cdot c_k$; threshold for voiding is **0.70**.
- ✅ **Moisture content limit** for seasoned boat-building timber: **≤ 18%**.
- ✅ **Hull inspection interval:** every $T_{service}/5$ years.
- ✅ **SOLAS** governs safety manuals; **ISO 10002** governs complaint handling.
- ✅ **Heritage documentation** is non-negotiable — KTU rubric explicitly rewards cultural awareness.
- ✅ Manual revision frequency: $R_f = T_{service}/N_{overhaul}$.
- ✅ The triad of accountability: **Builder ↔ Vessel ↔ Owner**.
- ✅ Warranty claim steps (7 steps): **Notify → Document → Inspect → Cause-Analyze → Coverage-Check → Estimate → Settle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Manual Sections:** *"**P**lease **C**over **O**ur **M**aintenance **S**afely and **H**eritage-wise"* → **P**articulars, **C**onstruction, **O**perations, **M**aintenance, **S**afety, **H**eritage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Safety at Sea
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Safety at Sea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Basic Safety at Sea** refers to the standardized set of preventive measures, equipment, protocols, and emergency response procedures designed to protect the lives of crew and passengers, preserve the vessel, and minimize environmental hazards during marine operations.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST864 – Traditional Boat Building Techniques)**, the term is rooted in two governing frameworks:

1. **SOLAS (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea, 1974)** – Administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.
2. **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (and subsequent amendments)** – The statutory law enforced by the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)**, Government of India, which governs traditional fishing vessels and country craft under specific exemptions and conditional clauses.

For traditional vessels such as *Vallams* (Kerala beach landing craft), *Dhonis* (Lakshadweep), *Masula boats* (Andhra coast), or *Catamarans* (Tamil Nadu), the "basic" safety tier is the minimal compliance regime that balances traditional seamanship with statutory life-saving obligations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy – The Seatbelt Principle:** Just as a seatbelt does not prevent an accident but drastically reduces the severity of injury when one occurs, **Basic Safety at Sea** is a *layered defence system*. The boat, the skill, and the equipment must all work together. If one layer fails, the others are expected to absorb the shock.

Think of a fisherman launching a *vallam* at dawn:
- The **vessel** is the structural shell.
- The **skill** is the captain's navigation knowledge.
- The **equipment** is the life jacket, EPIRB, and fire extinguisher.

The safety framework is the glue that ensures any one of these three being compromised does not lead to catastrophic loss of life.

## 1.3 Classification of Sea Safety

| Tier | Domain | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 – Personal** | Crew-level protection | Life jackets (PFDs), harnesses, helmets, safety boots |
| **Tier 2 – Vessel** | Onboard systems | Fire extinguishers, life buoys, life rafts, bilge pumps |
| **Tier 3 – Communication** | External signalling | VHF radio, EPIRB, flares, AIS |
| **Tier 4 – Procedural** | Behavioural standards | Watchkeeping, drills, log keeping, weather briefings |
| **Tier 5 – Regulatory** | Statutory compliance | SOLAS, MMD rules, fishing vessel licensing |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Minimum Personal Flotation Device (PFD) Buoyancy:** **75 N (Newtons)** for adult commercial use, **50 N** for recreational use.
> - **Standard Fire Extinguisher Charge Life:** **3 to 5 years** (periodic hydrostatic test every **12 years** for stored pressure types).
> - **VHF Channel 16:** **156.8 MHz** – International distress, safety, and calling channel.
> - **EPIRB Frequency:** **406 MHz** (with 121.5 MHz homing signal).
> - **Statutory Life Jacket Colour:** **International Orange** for high visibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Sea Safety (Framework)

The IMO consolidates sea safety into **five functional pillars** that any KTU student of traditional boat building must internalize:

1. **Pillar I – Personal Survival (Survival Craft & PFDs)**
2. **Pillar II – Fire Prevention & Firefighting**
3. **Pillar III – Emergency Communication (GMDSS)**
4. **Pillar IV – Navigation & Weather Safety**
5. **Pillar V – First Aid & Medical Assistance**

## 2.2 Pillar I – Personal Survival Equipment

### 2.2.1 Life Jackets (Personal Flotation Devices)

A life jacket works on **Archimedes' Principle**: it displaces a volume of water whose weight equals or exceeds the wearer's body weight.

$$F_b = \rho_{water} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$$

Where:
- $F_b$ = Buoyant force (N)
- $\rho_{water}$ = Density of seawater ≈ **1025 kg/m³**
- $V_{displaced}$ = Displaced volume (m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

For a body of mass $m$ in still seawater, the required displaced volume is:

$$V_{displaced} = \frac{m \cdot g}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} = \frac{m}{\rho_{water}}$$

Adding a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** (to account for wave action, clothing weight, and unconscious wearers), the minimum PFD buoyancy is:

$$F_{PFD} = 1.5 \cdot m \cdot g$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Quick Fact:** A 75 kg fisherman needs a PFD of at least **75 × 9.81 × 1.5 ≈ 1103 N ≈ 112.5 kgf** buoyant force. This is why commercial-grade life jackets provide **150 N to 275 N** – a comfortable safety margin.

### 2.2.2 Types of Life Jackets

| Type | Buoyancy | Use Case | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I – Offshore Life Jacket** | 275 N (≈ 28 kgf) | Open ocean, rough seas | Best for deep-sea traditional fishing |
| **Class II – Near-Shore Buoyant Vest** | 150 N (≈ 15 kgf) | Coastal waters | Suitable for *vallams* in backwaters |
| **Class III – Flotation Aid** | 75 N (≈ 7.6 kgf) | Calm inland waters | Minimum tier – rarely recommended |
| **Inflatable PFDs** | 150–275 N (after inflation) | Trained crew, sports | NOT allowed for non-swimmers; requires periodic re-arming |

## 2.3 Pillar II – Fire Prevention & Firefighting

### 2.3.1 The Fire Tetrahedron (Modern Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> The traditional **fire triangle** (Fuel + Oxygen + Heat) has been extended to a **fire tetrahedron** (Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Uninhibited Chain Reaction). A fire extinguisher works by removing at least one of these four elements.

### 2.3.2 Classification of Marine Fires & Extinguishers

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Agent | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Wood, cloth, paper | Water, foam | Sail canvas, wooden hull |
| **Class B** | Oil, petrol, diesel | Foam, CO₂, dry powder | Engine room, fuel tank |
| **Class C** | Electrical (live) | CO₂, dry powder | Wiring, navigation lights |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Special dry powder | Rare in traditional craft |
| **Class K** | Cooking oils | Wet chemical | Galley fires |

### 2.3.3 Selection Logic for Traditional Boats

For a *vallam* or *dhoni*, the recommended extinguisher load-out is:

- **1 × 6 kg ABC Dry Powder** (covers A, B, C – universal)
- **1 × 2 kg CO₂** (engine compartment, electrical panels)
- **1 × 9-litre AFFF Foam** (deck and fuel-spill area)

## 2.4 Pillar III – GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GMDSS** is the IMO-mandated communication framework that uses **satellite and terrestrial** systems to ensure a distress alert is received *anywhere in the world*.

### 2.4.1 GMDSS Functional Blocks

- **Block 1 – Distress Alerting:** EPIRB, DSC, SART
- **Block 2 – Search and Rescue Coordination:** RCC (Rescue Coordination Centre), MRCC
- **Block 3 – Maritime Safety Information (MSI):** NAVTEX, weather broadcasts
- **Block 4 – General Communication:** VHF RT, NBDP (telex over radio)
- **Block 5 – Bridge-to-Bridge:** Ship-to-ship safety comms

### 2.4.2 Distress Frequencies (Memorize for KTU)

| System | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **VHF Ch 16** | 156.8 MHz | Voice distress, calling |
| **VHF DSC Ch 70** | 156.525 MHz | Digital selective calling |
| **EPIRB** | 406 MHz | Satellite-tracked distress beacon |
| **SART** | 9 GHz (X-band radar) | Radar transponder, homing |
| **NAVTEX** | 518 kHz | MSI broadcasts |

## 2.5 Pillar IV – Navigation & Weather Safety

### 2.5.1 The Stability Triangle (Simplified)

For a traditional vessel, basic transverse stability is governed by the **metacentric height (GM)**:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

Where:
- $KB$ = Height of centre of buoyancy above keel
- $BM$ = Metacentric radius (transverse) $= \dfrac{I_T}{V_{displaced}}$
- $KG$ = Height of centre of gravity above keel
- $I_T$ = Second moment of waterplane area (m⁴)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for traditional boat builders:** Loading heavy equipment (anchors, ice boxes, catch) high in the boat raises $KG$, *reducing* $GM$. A boat with **GM < 0** is unstable and will capsize. The safe range for a small fishing vessel is **GM = 0.35 m to 0.60 m**.

### 2.5.2 Weather Warning Levels (India – IMD)

| Colour Code | Warning Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Green** | All clear | Normal operations |
| **Yellow** | Be aware | Check forecasts every 3 hours |
| **Orange** | Be prepared | Secure loose items, postpone launch |
| **Red** | Take action | Return to harbour, suspend operations |

## 2.6 Pillar V – First Aid & Medical Kits

For traditional fishing vessels under Indian MMD rules, the mandatory **Type C Medical Kit** must include:

- Bandages, gauze, antiseptic
- Pain relievers, anti-nausea tablets
- Thermal emergency blanket
- Manual resuscitation bag
- Eye-wash bottle
- Burns dressings
- Instruction card in local language

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Symbol | Value / Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buoyant force | $F_b = \rho V g$ | Life jacket design | N |
| Required PFD force | $F_{PFD} = 1.5 \cdot m \cdot g$ | Crew survival | N |
| Metacentric height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Vessel stability | m |
| Metacentric radius | $BM = I_T / V$ | Hull form stability | m |
| Wave height limit (rule of thumb) | $H_{max} \approx 0.6 \cdot L_{WL}$ | Small craft safety | m |
| Beaufort scale – small craft advisory | Force ≥ 5 | Wind 17–21 kt | knots |
| Fire tetrahedron sides | 4 | Extinguisher logic | – |
| SOLAS life jacket colour | – | International Orange | – |
| Distress VHF channel | Ch 16 | 156.8 MHz | MHz |
| EPIRB frequency | – | 406 | MHz |
| SART frequency | – | 9 | GHz |
| Mayday call format | MAYDAY × 3 | Distress voice call | – |
| Pan-Pan call | Pan-Pan × 3 | Urgency | – |
| Sécurité call | Sécurité × 3 | Safety information | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every traditional boat builder should be able to:
> - Estimate required life jacket buoyancy for a given crew size.
> - Calculate the metacentric height of a *vallam* before adding new deck equipment.
> - Select the correct fire extinguisher class for the engine bay of a *dhoni*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Worked Solutions

## 3.1 Procedure A – Determining Minimum PFD Buoyancy for a Crew

**Problem Statement:** A traditional *vallam* is to be operated by a crew of **6 fishermen**, with an average body mass of **70 kg**. The vessel operates in coastal backwaters (saline water, density 1025 kg/m³). Determine the minimum total PFD buoyancy required, and select the correct Class of life jacket.

### Step 1 – Identify Inputs
- Crew size, $n = 6$
- Average mass per person, $m = 70$ kg
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Safety factor, $SF = 1.5$ (IMO minimum for conscious wearers in calm water)

### Step 2 – Calculate Per-Person Required Buoyancy
Apply the safety-factored buoyancy equation:

$$F_{person} = SF \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$F_{person} = 1.5 \cdot 70 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{person} = 1030.05 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 – Calculate Total Crew Buoyancy Requirement
Multiply by the number of crew members:

$$F_{total} = n \cdot F_{person}$$

$$F_{total} = 6 \cdot 1030.05$$

$$F_{total} = 6180.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 – Convert to kgf for Practical Selection
Since life jacket ratings are often given in Newtons (N) directly:

$$F_{total} \approx 6180 \text{ N} \quad (\approx 630 \text{ kgf})$$

### Step 5 – Select the Appropriate PFD Class
- A Class II PFD provides **150 N** per person.
- For 6 crew, that is $6 \times 150 = 900$ N, which is **insufficient** for our 6180 N requirement.
- A Class I (Offshore) PFD provides **275 N** per person.
- For 6 crew, that is $6 \times 275 = 1650$ N, which is also **insufficient** if calculated using 1.5×SF, BUT the IMO and MMD accept **minimum statutory 150 N per person** in many coastal cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Interpretation:** The 1.5×SF is an *engineering* conservative calculation. The *statutory* requirement for Indian coastal fishing vessels (under MMD Notification 21 of 2008 and subsequent circulars) is that **each person be issued one PFD of at least 150 N buoyancy**, and that the total stock of life jackets on board must equal or exceed the total head count.

### Step 6 – Final Recommendation
- Issue **6 Class II PFDs (150 N each)** to satisfy MMD minimum.
- For enhanced safety, consider **6 Class I PFDs (275 N each)**.
- Mark each PFD with the wearer's name and issue date.
- **Inspection cycle:** every 6 months (or per manufacturer).

---

## 3.2 Procedure B – Quick Stability Check (Metacentric Height)

**Problem Statement:** A small country craft has:
- $KB$ = 0.45 m
- $BM$ = 0.92 m
- $KG$ = 1.05 m (after loading 200 kg of ice on the deck)

Determine if the vessel is stable.

### Step 1 – Write the GM Formula

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

### Step 2 – Substitute Values

$$GM = 0.45 + 0.92 - 1.05$$

### Step 3 – Compute

$$GM = 1.37 - 1.05 = 0.32 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 – Compare to the Safety Range
- Acceptable range: $GM = 0.35$ m to $0.60$ m
- Computed: $GM = 0.32$ m
- **Conclusion:** The vessel is **marginally below the safety threshold**. The vessel is technically stable (GM > 0) but stiff and prone to a **stiff roll** (rapid, uncomfortable rolling), and any further high loading (e.g., additional crew on deck) can drop GM to **zero or below**, causing capsize.

### Step 5 – Corrective Action
- Relocate the 200 kg ice load **below deck** (to lower KG).
- Re-compute: if KG becomes 0.85 m, then $GM = 0.45 + 0.92 - 0.85 = 0.52$ m → **safe**.

---

## 3.3 Procedure C – Man Overboard (MOB) Response Sequence

This is the **gold-standard 5-step sequence** drilled in every maritime academy:

| Step | Action | Time Target | Person Responsible |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 – Shout** | "MAN OVERBOARD! PORT/STARBOARD!" | 0 sec | Spotter |
| **2 – Throw** | Throw life buoy with light & line | 10 sec | Nearest crew |
| **3 – Mark** | Press MOB button on GPS; note position | 30 sec | Skipper |
| **4 – Helm** | Williamson Turn (or Scharnow Turn) | 60 sec | Helmsman |
| **5 – Communicate** | Mayday on VHF Ch 16 if recovery fails | 2 min | Comms officer |

### Step 3.3.1 – The Williamson Turn (Recovery Maneuver)

> [!NOTE]
> The Williamson Turn is a 60° course alteration followed by a hard turn back. It returns the vessel to approximately the MOB position.

$$Turn_1: \text{Steer } 60° \text{ toward the side MOB was sighted}$$

$$Turn_2: \text{After steadying on new heading, hard-over to opposite side}$$

$$Recovery: \text{On the reciprocal course, MOB should be on beam}$$

**Why it works:** It uses the vessel's turn diameter to *cancel out* the drift caused by current and wind during the recovery.

---

## 3.4 Procedure D – Fire Extinguisher Selection Logic

Use this 4-question decision tree:

> **Q1: Is the fire electrical?**
> - YES → CO₂ extinguisher (Class C, leaves no residue).
> - NO → proceed to Q2.

> **Q2: Is the fire liquid (oil, fuel)?**
> - YES → AFFF Foam or ABC Dry Powder (Class B).
> - NO → proceed to Q3.

> **Q3: Is the fire wood/cloth/paper?**
> - YES → Water or ABC Dry Powder (Class A).
> - NO → proceed to Q4.

> **Q4: Is the fire in cooking oil?**
> - YES → Wet chemical (Class K).
> - NO → ABC Dry Powder as universal fallback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students select **water** for *all* fires. **Never use water on fuel or electrical fires** – it spreads the fuel and conducts electricity, leading to mass casualties.

---

## 3.5 Procedure E – Mayday (Distress) Call Format

The IMO-mandated format on VHF Ch 16:

```
MAYDAY, MAYDAY, MAYDAY

THIS IS [Vessel Name × 3]
[MMSI Number]
CALL SIGN

MAYDAY [Vessel Name]
POSITION: [Latitude / Longitude]
NATURE OF DISTRESS: [Fire / Sinking / MOB / etc.]
ASSISTANCE REQUIRED: [Type – Medical / Tow / Pump]
NUMBER OF PERSONS ON BOARD: [Integer]
DESCRIPTION OF VESSEL: [Length, colour, hull type]
OTHER INFORMATION: [Weather, drift, life raft deployed]

OVER
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Saying "MAYDAY" in non-life-threatening situations. **Mayday is reserved for grave and imminent danger requiring immediate assistance.** For lesser urgency, use **Pan-Pan (×3)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 – Sea Safety Functional Architecture

This block diagram maps the five pillars of sea safety, their equipment, and the regulatory body governing each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Basic Safety at Sea] --> node2[Pillar 1: Personal Survival]
    node1 --> node3[Pillar 2: Fire Safety]
    node1 --> node4[Pillar 3: Communication]
    node1 --> node5[Pillar 4: Navigation and Weather]
    node1 --> node6[Pillar 5: First Aid]

    node2 --> node2a[Class I II III PFDs]
    node2 --> node2b[Life Buoys]
    node2 --> node2c[Life Rafts]
    node2 --> node2d[Survival Suits]

    node3 --> node3a[ABC Dry Powder]
    node3 --> node3b[CO2 Extinguisher]
    node3 --> node3c[AFFF Foam]
    node3 --> node3d[Fire Blanket]

    node4 --> node4a[VHF Radio Ch 16]
    node4 --> node4b[EPIRB 406 MHz]
    node4 --> node4c[SART 9 GHz]
    node4 --> node4d[Flares and Smoke]

    node5 --> node5a[GPS and Compass]
    node5 --> node5b[NAVTEX]
    node5 --> node5c[IMD Weather Bulletins]
    node5 --> node5d[Stability Calculations]

    node6 --> node6a[Type C Medical Kit]
    node6 --> node6b[First Aid Manual]
    node6 --> node6c[Resuscitation Bag]
    node6 --> node6d[Stretcher]

    node2a --> reg1[SOLAS and MMD]
    node3a --> reg1
    node4a --> reg1
    node5a --> reg1
    node6a --> reg1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 – Man Overboard (MOB) Response Flow

This flowchart depicts the **5-step MOB procedure** introduced in Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[MOB Sighted] --> nodeB{Shout MOB PORT STARBOARD}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Throw Life Buoy with Light]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Mark Position on GPS MOB Key]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Vessel Type}
    nodeE -->|Powered| nodeF[Execute Williamson Turn]
    nodeE -->|Sail| nodeG[Quick Stop or Figure Eight]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Approach MOB on Lee Side]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI{MOB Recovered}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Administer First Aid]
    nodeI -->|No| nodeK[Transmit MAYDAY on Ch 16]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Activate EPIRB]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Deploy Flares]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Log Incident and Inform Coast Guard]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 – Fire Extinguisher Selection Decision Tree

This block-flow implements the 4-question decision logic from Section 3.4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1[Fire Detected] --> nodeQ2{Fire Type Electrical}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3[Use CO2 Extinguisher]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ4{Fire Type Liquid Fuel}
    nodeQ4 -->|Yes| nodeQ5[Use AFFF Foam or ABC Powder]
    nodeQ4 -->|No| nodeQ6{Fire Type Wood Cloth Paper}
    nodeQ6 -->|Yes| nodeQ7[Use Water or ABC Powder]
    nodeQ6 -->|No| nodeQ8{Fire Type Cooking Oil}
    nodeQ8 -->|Yes| nodeQ9[Use Wet Chemical Class K]
    nodeQ8 -->|No| nodeQ10[Use ABC Dry Powder Universal]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeR1[Stand Down and Log]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeR1
    nodeQ7 --> nodeR1
    nodeQ9 --> nodeR1
    nodeQ10 --> nodeR1
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram 4 – GMDSS Communication Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Distressed Vessel] -->|EPIRB 406 MHz| nodeS2[GOES Satellite]
    nodeS1 -->|VHF DSC Ch 70| nodeS3[Coast Station]
    nodeS1 -->|MAYDAY Ch 16| nodeS3
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4[LUT Local User Terminal]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[MCC Mission Control Centre]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS6[MRCC Maritime Rescue Coord Centre]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Rescue Craft Dispatched]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS8[Aircraft SAR Deployed]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9[Distressed Vessel Located]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> *Define "Basic Safety at Sea" in the context of traditional boat building. List any four personal survival equipment items required under SOLAS for a traditional fishing vessel operating in coastal waters.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks distribution: 1 + 2):**

Basic Safety at Sea refers to the **minimum statutory set of preventive measures, emergency equipment, and procedural protocols** designed to protect the lives of crew and passengers, preserve the vessel, and minimize environmental damage during marine operations. It is governed primarily by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS, 1974)** and in India, by the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** and the **Indian Fishing Vessels Rules** administered by the DGS.

**Four personal survival equipment items (0.5 each):**
1. **Life jacket (PFD)** – Minimum 150 N buoyancy, international orange.
2. **Life buoy (Ring Buoy)** – With 30 m of buoyant line and self-igniting light.
3. **Whistle and signal mirror** – For attracting attention.
4. **Survival instructions card** – In English and the local language.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> *Explain the difference between "Mayday" and "Pan-Pan" distress calls. State the VHF channel and frequency used for these calls.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks distribution: 1.5 + 0.75 + 0.75):**

| Term | Meaning | Condition | Channel | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mayday** | Grave and imminent danger requiring immediate assistance (e.g., sinking, fire, MOB) | Life-threatening | **VHF Channel 16** | **156.8 MHz** |
| **Pan-Pan** | Urgency – safety of vessel/person is at stake but not in immediate grave danger (e.g., engine failure, medical issue) | Urgent but not life-critical | VHF Channel 16 | 156.8 MHz |

**Procedure:** Both calls are repeated **three times** for clarity, followed by vessel identification, position, nature of distress, and assistance required.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam Pattern 2024 Scheme – Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** *(7 Marks)*
Explain the **five functional pillars of sea safety** as recognized by the International Maritime Organization. Describe in detail the components of the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)** and state the operating frequencies of its key subsystems.

**Q. (b)** *(7 Marks)*
A traditional fishing *vallam* has a crew of **8 fishermen** with an average body mass of **72 kg**. Using Archimedes' principle, calculate the minimum total buoyant force required for the life jackets, applying a safety factor of **1.5**. Identify the appropriate Class of life jacket to be issued and justify your selection based on the operating environment.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 – The Five Pillars (2 Marks):**
The IMO consolidates sea safety into five functional pillars:

1. **Pillar I – Personal Survival:** PFDs, life rafts, immersion suits, life buoys.
2. **Pillar II – Fire Prevention & Firefighting:** Extinguishers, fire detection systems, fire blankets, fixed installations.
3. **Pillar III – Emergency Communication:** GMDSS, VHF, EPIRB, SART, NAVTEX.
4. **Pillar IV – Navigation & Weather Safety:** GPS, compass, radar, weather routing, IMD bulletins.
5. **Pillar V – First Aid & Medical Assistance:** Medical kits, stretcher, telemedicine, trained first-aiders.

**Step 2 – GMDSS Components (3 Marks):**

| Component | Function | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **EPIRB** | Satellite-tracked beacon | **406 MHz** |
| **SART** | Radar transponder for homing | **9 GHz** (X-band) |
| **VHF DSC** | Digital selective calling | **156.525 MHz** (Ch 70) |
| **VHF RT** | Voice distress & calling | **156.8 MHz** (Ch 16) |
| **NAVTEX** | Automatic MSI broadcasts | **518 kHz** |
| **Inmarsat** | Satellite communication (voice/data) | L-band (1.5–1.6 GHz) |

**Step 3 – Justification of GMDSS (2 Marks):**
The GMDSS ensures that a distress alert is **automatically transmitted, routed to the nearest Rescue Coordination Centre (RCC/MRCC)**, and acknowledged irrespective of the vessel's location. It eliminates the dependence on passing ships to relay distress.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 – Given Data (0.5 Marks):**
- Crew size, $n = 8$
- Average mass, $m = 72$ kg
- Safety factor, $SF = 1.5$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2 – Per-Person Buoyant Force (2 Marks):**

$$F_{person} = SF \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$F_{person} = 1.5 \cdot 72 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{person} = 1059.48 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 – Total Crew Buoyant Force (2 Marks):**

$$F_{total} = n \cdot F_{person} = 8 \cdot 1059.48 = 8475.84 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 – Conversion and Selection (2 Marks):**
- $F_{total} \approx 8476$ N (≈ 864 kgf).
- **Class II PFD (150 N)** per person = 1200 N total → **insufficient** vs. the 1.5×SF figure, but **meets the statutory minimum** for Indian coastal vessels.
- **Class I PFD (275 N)** per person = 2200 N total → **recommended** for backwater/coastal use, especially at night.
- **Recommendation:** Issue **8 Class I PFDs (275 N)** for better headroom in case of cold water, fatigue, or unconscious wearer.

**Step 5 – Justification (0.5 Marks):**
The *vallam* operates in coastal saline waters with high tidal currents. The higher 275 N Class I rating provides safety margin against clothing saturation and wave slap.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**Q. (a)** *(7 Marks)*
Describe the **Man Overboard (MOB)** procedure in detail, with a labelled sketch of the **Williamson Turn**. State the responsibilities of (i) the spotter, (ii) the helmsman, and (iii) the communications officer during an MOB emergency.

**Q. (b)** *(7 Marks)*
A traditional country craft has the following stability parameters: $KB = 0.50$ m, $BM = 0.85$ m, and $KG = 1.20$ m. The skipper plans to load an additional **300 kg of ice on the deck** (decentred 1.5 m above the existing KG). Determine:
1. The current metacentric height.
2. The new KG after loading.
3. Whether the vessel remains stable, with a justification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Q.B(b), students often **forget to recalculate KG** after the additional load. The mass of ice, combined with the existing vessel displacement, must be used to compute a **weighted average** for the new KG.
> - In Q.A(b), students frequently **omit the safety factor** or use the wrong formula. Always show: $F = SF \cdot m \cdot g$.
> - In any MOB question, **timing matters**: examiners award marks for the order of actions (Shout → Throw → Mark → Helm → Communicate).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 – The 5-Step MOB Procedure (3 Marks):**

1. **Shout:** "MAN OVERBOARD! PORT/STARBOARD SIDE!" – alerts the entire crew instantly.
2. **Throw:** Heave the life buoy with attached light and line to the MOB.
3. **Mark:** Press the MOB button on the GPS; record the lat/long. This marks the position even if visibility is lost.
4. **Helm:** Execute the Williamson Turn (60° toward MOB, then hard-over to opposite side, ending on reciprocal course).
5. **Communicate:** If not recovered, transmit MAYDAY on VHF Ch 16.

**Step 2 – Williamson Turn Diagram (2 Marks) – Verbal Description:**
> The vessel continues on the original heading for a short distance, alters **60° toward the MOB side**, steadies briefly, then hard-rudders to the **opposite extreme** until on the **reciprocal course**. The turn diameter approximately equals the distance the vessel drifted during the maneuver, bringing it back to the MOB's position.

**Step 3 – Crew Responsibilities (2 Marks):**

| Crew Member | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Spotter** | Continuously point at MOB with arm outstretched; never lose visual contact. |
| **Helmsman** | Execute the Williamson Turn; keep the spotter's bearing in mind. |
| **Comms Officer** | Inform the skipper, sound the alarm, prepare MAYDAY, activate EPIRB. |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) – 7 Marks**

**Step 1 – Current Metacentric Height (2 Marks):**

$$GM_{current} = KB + BM - KG$$

$$GM_{current} = 0.50 + 0.85 - 1.20 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

> The current vessel is **stable** but **marginal** (below the safe 0.35–0.60 m range).

**Step 2 – New KG After Loading Ice (3 Marks):**

Let:
- $W$ = existing vessel displacement (assumed) = **1500 kg** (typical small craft)
- $w$ = ice load = 300 kg
- $h$ = deck height above existing KG = 1.5 m

Using the weighted-average formula for the new centre of gravity:

$$KG_{new} = \frac{W \cdot KG + w \cdot h}{W + w}$$

$$KG_{new} = \frac{1500 \cdot 1.20 + 300 \cdot 1.50}{1500 + 300}$$

$$KG_{new} = \frac{1800 + 450}{1800} = \frac{2250}{1800} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 – New Metacentric Height (1 Mark):**

$$GM_{new} = KB + BM - KG_{new}$$

$$GM_{new} = 0.50 + 0.85 - 1.25 = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 – Stability Verdict (1 Mark):**
- $GM_{new} = 0.10$ m > 0 → still technically stable, but **well below** the safe threshold of 0.35 m.
- **Conclusion:** **Not safe for sea**. The skipper should either:
  1. Relocate the 300 kg ice to the **bilge** (lowering KG), or
  2. **Reduce non-essential deck weight** before departure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme – Basic Safety at Sea**

- ✅ **SOLAS 1974** is the international governing convention; **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** is the statutory law.
- ✅ **Five Pillars of Sea Safety:** Personal Survival, Fire, Communication, Navigation/Weather, First Aid.
- ✅ **Mayday (×3)** = grave & imminent danger; **Pan-Pan (×3)** = urgency; **Sécurité (×3)** = safety info.
- ✅ **VHF Channel 16 = 156.8 MHz** – remember this!
- ✅ **EPIRB = 406 MHz**, **SART = 9 GHz**.
- ✅ **PFD minimum buoyancy = 150 N (Class II)** for coastal Indian traditional vessels; 275 N (Class I) preferred.
- ✅ **Buoyancy equation:** $F_b = \rho V g$; **PFD required force:** $F = 1.5 \cdot m \cdot g$.
- ✅ **Metacentric height equation:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$.
- ✅ **Safe GM range for small craft:** 0.35 m to 0.60 m.
- ✅ **Fire extinguisher classes:** A (wood/cloth), B (oil/fuel), C (electrical), D (metals), K (cooking oil).
- ✅ **Never use water on fuel or electrical fires.**
- ✅ **MOB procedure:** Shout → Throw → Mark → Helm (Williamson Turn) → Communicate (MAYDAY).
- ✅ **Williamson Turn:** 60° toward MOB → hard-over opposite → reciprocal course.
- ✅ **IMD weather colour codes:** Green (clear) → Yellow (be aware) → Orange (be prepared) → Red (take action).
- ✅ **International Orange** is the statutory colour for life jackets and life buoys.
- ✅ **Type C Medical Kit** is the minimum statutory first-aid kit for Indian fishing vessels.
- ✅ **GMDSS** stands for **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System** – a satellite + terrestrial integrated alerting network.
- ✅ Always conduct a **GM check** before adding high loads (ice, catch, fuel) to a traditional vessel.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skills Required and Sub-Contracting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Skills Required and Sub-Contracting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Skills Required" in Traditional Boat Building

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> *Skills Required* in traditional boat building refer to the **comprehensive set of cognitive, technical, manual, and interpersonal competencies** that a master boatbuilder (or a member of a yard team) must possess to design, construct, repair, and maintain wooden/watercraft vessels using time-honoured, non-industrial methods. These skills span **carpentry, lofting, joinery, caulking, rigging, finishing, and supervisory capabilities**, and they form the human-capital backbone of any traditional yard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum** for *PENST864*, Module 3 treats "Skills Required" as the *human-input* dimension of the boat-building value chain, while "Sub-Contracting" is treated as the *organisational-input* dimension. The two are inseparable: a yard that lacks an in-house skill must either **train, hire, or sub-contract** to fill the gap.

## 1.2 Defining "Sub-Contracting" in the Boat-Building Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> *Sub-Contracting* in traditional boat building is a **project-execution strategy** in which the principal boatyard (the *contractor*) outsources a specific, well-defined portion of the construction, repair, or finishing work to a **specialised third-party vendor** (the *sub-contractor*) under a **secondary contractual agreement**, while the principal yard retains overall accountability for the final vessel.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Grand Kitchen" Model

Imagine a Michelin-star restaurant kitchen:

- The **Head Chef (Master Boatbuilder)** is responsible for the *final dish* (the boat).
- They cannot personally chop every vegetable, bake every bread roll, *and* plate every dish — that would be inefficient.
- Instead, they **in-source** the skills they do best (e.g., sauce-making) and **sub-contract** specialised tasks (e.g., bread from an artisan bakery, ice-cream from a local churner) to **trusted artisans**.
- The Head Chef's *skill set* dictates what stays in-house; the *absence* of a skill triggers sub-contracting.

A traditional boatyard operates identically: **the matrix of in-house skills determines the sub-contracting footprint.**

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 3 rests on **two inter-locking pillars**:
> 1. **Pillar A — Human Capital:** What skills must a boatbuilder hold, and how are they acquired, assessed, and certified?
> 2. **Pillar B — Organisational Architecture:** When should a yard retain a skill in-house versus sub-contract it, and what are the legal, quality, and financial implications of that decision?

| Dimension | Skills Required | Sub-Contracting |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | *People* (knowledge, ability, dexterity) | *Process* (allocation of work, contracts) |
| **Time horizon** | Long-term (lifelong craft) | Project-based (per-vessel) |
| **Owner of asset** | The craftsman (portable skill) | The yard (process & liability) |
| **Failure mode** | Skill gap → poor craftsmanship | Sub-contractor default → schedule slippage |
| **KTU Bloom Level** | *Understand, Apply* | *Analyse, Evaluate* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Skills Required in Traditional Boat Building

> [!NOTE]
> The skill set is best understood as a **five-layer pyramid**, with broad literacy at the base and rarified mastery at the apex.

### Layer 1 — Foundational Skills (Everyone in the yard)
- **Literacy & Numeracy:** Reading drawings, calculating plank lengths, scaling offsets.
- **Health & Safety Awareness:** PPE usage, fire safety around solvents, lifting techniques.
- **Material Identification:** Distinguishing *teak, mahogany, cedar, oak, deodar, rosewood* by grain, weight, smell.

### Layer 2 — Core Carpentry Skills (Every shipwright)
- **Measuring & Marking:** Use of *furlong, spirit level, bevel square, marking gauge*.
- **Cutting & Shaping:** Hand-saw, rip-saw, chisel, plane, adze.
- **Joinery:** *Mortise-and-tenon, scarph, rabbet, dovetail, hood-end* joints.

### Layer 3 — Specialist Boat-Building Skills
- **Lofting:** Full-scale translation of design lines onto the loft floor.
- **Planking & Steam Bending:** Cold moulding, hot-bending frames over steam chests.
- **Caulking & Cottoning:** Driving cotton/oakum into seams with a caulking iron and mallet.
- **Wood Preservation:** Application of *creosote, copper-based, or traditional oils* to prevent rot and borer attack.

### Layer 4 — Auxiliary Craft Skills
- **Rigging:** Knowledge of knots, splicing, deadeases, lanyards, shrouds, stays.
- **Sail-making:** (in sailing vessels) — cutting, stitching, boltrope attachment.
- **Blacksmithing & Metalwork:** Forged iron knees, floor-clamps, rudder pintles.
- **Upholstery & Joinery Finishing:** Cabin furnishing, varnishing, French polishing.

### Layer 5 — Managerial & Interpersonal Skills
- **Project Planning:** Sequencing frame erection → planking → caulking → launching.
- **Client Communication:** Translating technical jargon into lay terms.
- **Quality Assurance:** Inspection, defect rectification, ISO/QCI standards.
- **Sub-Contractor Coordination:** Scope definition, milestone tracking, payment gating.

## 2.2 The Skill-Acquisition Curve

The journey from novice to master is governed by the classic **Dreyfus Model of Skill Acquisition**, adapted for KTU's NEP 2020 *experiential-learning* framework:

$$\text{Competence} = f(\text{Knowledge}, \text{Experience}, \text{Deliberate Practice}, \text{Mentorship})$$

For boat-building specifically, the empirical rule of thumb is:

$$\text{Years to Mastery} \approx 7 \text{ to } 10 \text{ years of full-time craft practice}$$

## 2.3 Sub-Contracting: Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 The Make-or-Buy Decision Matrix

The yard's central question is: **"Do we make this in-house, or do we sub-contract it?"** The decision rests on four criteria:

1. **Strategic Importance** — Is the activity *core* to our yard's identity?
2. **Competence Gap** — Do we possess the skill in-house?
3. **Cost Efficiency** — Is sub-contracting cheaper than training/hiring?
4. **Capacity Utilisation** — Will in-house execution idle our core team?

### 2.3.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet**

| # | Concept | Key Principle | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Skill Pyramid** | 5 layers from foundational → managerial | *Understand* |
| 2 | **Dreyfus Stages** | Novice → Advanced Beginner → Competent → Proficient → Expert | *Apply* |
| 3 | **Mastery Horizon** | 7 to 10 years of deliberate practice | *Remember* |
| 4 | **Make-or-Buy Rule** | Sub-contract when *cost < in-house unit cost* AND *strategic risk < threshold* | *Evaluate* |
| 5 | **Core vs Non-Core** | Sub-contract *non-core*; retain *core* | *Analyse* |
| 6 | **Sub-Contractor Tier** | Tier 1 (strategic partner) to Tier 3 (transactional) | *Understand* |
| 7 | **Liability Ladder** | Yard remains liable regardless of sub-contract tier | *Apply* |
| 8 | **Skill Multiplication Factor** | 1 master can mentor 3 to 5 apprentices | *Apply* |
| 9 | **Quality Gate (Q-Gate)** | Stage-wise inspection before next sub-contractor payment | *Evaluate* |
| 10 | **Documentation Triad** | *Scope + Schedule + Specification* (the 3-S sub-contracting contract) | *Create* |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Boat-Building Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Note**
> In heritage-yards across **Kerala (Cochin, Beypore, Vizhinjam)**, **Goa**, and **Sunderbans (West Bengal)**, the make-or-buy calculus is acute. Many yards *retain* the iconic skills (planking, caulking) but *sub-contract* modern necessities (marine diesel engine installation, electrical wiring, navigation electronics, GRP sheathing for waterproofing). The *skill inventory* thus becomes a **strategic asset** that defines the yard's brand identity.

## 2.5 The Sub-Contracting Risk Equation (Conceptual)

While the topic is non-mathematical, the conceptual *risk weighting* can be expressed as:

$$R_{sc} = (P_{default} \times I_{impact}) + (P_{quality} \times I_{rework}) + (P_{delay} \times I_{schedule})$$

where:
- $P_{default}$ = Probability of sub-contractor default
- $I_{impact}$ = Impact severity
- (and so on for quality and delay)

> The yard's *risk appetite* (RA) determines the threshold: **sub-contract only if $R_{sc} < RA$.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Worked Example — Skill Inventory Audit for a Traditional Fishing Vessel

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU Sample Case Study):**
> *Cochin Marine Crafts Pvt. Ltd.* is a heritage yard building a 35-foot traditional fishing trawler. The yard manager must conduct a **Skill Inventory Audit** and produce a **Make-or-Buy Recommendation Table**.

### Step 1 — Decompose the Vessel into Work Packages (WPs)

The vessel is decomposed into **10 standard work packages** used in KTU Module 3:

1. Hull design & lofting
2. Frame & timber sourcing
3. Keel & stem fabrication
4. Planking (cold-moulded)
5. Decking & beams
6. Caulking & sealing
7. Engine installation
8. Electrical & navigation
9. Rigging & sail (if applicable)
10. Painting, varnishing & finishing

### Step 2 — Score Each WP on the Four Decision Criteria

**Scoring Scale:** 1 (Low) to 5 (High)

| WP | Strategic Importance | In-House Skill | Cost-Efficiency Gap | Capacity Strain | **Sub-Contract?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Lofting | 5 | 4 | 1 | 2 | **No** (core) |
| 2. Timber Sourcing | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | **No** (core) |
| 3. Keel & Stem | 5 | 5 | 1 | 2 | **No** (core) |
| 4. Planking | 5 | 5 | 1 | 3 | **No** (core) |
| 5. Decking | 4 | 4 | 1 | 2 | **No** (core) |
| 6. Caulking | 5 | 5 | 1 | 2 | **No** (core) |
| 7. Engine | 3 | 1 | 5 | 1 | **YES** |
| 8. Electrical | 2 | 1 | 5 | 1 | **YES** |
| 9. Rigging | 3 | 2 | 4 | 2 | **YES** |
| 10. Finishing | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | **MAYBE** |

### Step 3 — Decision Logic

For each WP, the yard applies the **"CORE-FIT Rule"**:

$$\text{Sub-Contract Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } (\text{Skill} \leq 2) \text{ AND } (\text{Cost-Gap} \geq 4) \\ \text{No} & \text{if } (\text{Skill} \geq 4) \text{ AND } (\text{Strategic} \geq 4) \\ \text{Maybe} & \text{Otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Recommendation Matrix (Final)

| WP | Final Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Lofting, Frames, Planking, Caulking, Keel, Decking | **In-House** | Core heritage skills; brand identity |
| Engine, Electrical | **Sub-Contract** | Specialised, capital-intensive, non-core |
| Rigging | **Sub-Contract** | Sporadic need; specialist sail-lofts available |
| Finishing | **In-House (with outsourced materials)** | Brand-signature craftsmanship |

## 3.2 Skill-Gap Mitigation Strategies (When Sub-Contracting is NOT an Option)

If a skill is **strategically critical** but **in-house competence is low**, the yard has three non-sub-contracting pathways:

### Pathway 1 — Train (Build In-House Skill)
$$\text{Apprenticeship Yield} = \frac{\text{Apprentices Retained After 3 Yrs}}{\text{Apprentices Hired}} \times 100\%$$
Target industry benchmark: **$\geq 60\%$** retention after 3 years.

### Pathway 2 — Hire (Recruit Lateral Talent)
Cost of lateral hire vs cost of apprenticeship must be weighed:

$$C_{lateral} = \text{CTC} + \text{Recruitment Fee} + \text{Onboarding Cost}$$
$$C_{apprentice} = \text{Stipend} \times 3 + \text{Training Cost} + \text{Mentor Hours}$$

### Pathway 3 — Partner (Knowledge Sub-Contracting)
Engage a *retired master* on a **retainership** model — a soft form of sub-contracting that preserves in-house control.

## 3.3 Sub-Contractor Selection: A 5-Filter Model

When the yard decides to sub-contract, the *selection process* itself is a skill. Apply these **5 sequential filters**:

1. **Capability Filter** — Does the sub-contractor have the *technical skill* and *certification* (e.g., marine-grade electrical license)?
2. **Capacity Filter** — Can they handle the yard's *volume* and *timeline*?
3. **Cost Filter** — Is the quote within the yard's *bill-of-materials* envelope?
4. **Compliance Filter** — Do they follow *safety, environmental, and labour laws*?
5. **Chemistry Filter** — Will the yard's *foreman and the sub-contractor's team* collaborate harmoniously?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A sub-contractor who clears Filters 1 to 4 but fails Filter 5 is the **most common cause of project failure** in traditional boat yards.

## 3.4 The 3-S Sub-Contracting Contract

Every sub-contract in a KTU-aligned traditional boat yard should reduce to a **3-S document**:

$$\text{Sub-Contract} = \{\text{Scope}, \text{Schedule}, \text{Specification}\}$$

- **Scope** — *What* is being done (deliverables, exclusions)
- **Schedule** — *When* (milestones, penalties, hand-over dates)
- **Specification** — *How well* (quality standards, material grade, defect liability period)

A yard that **omits any one of the 3-S** is statistically exposed to **scope-creep, schedule-slip, and quality-defect** disputes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom Levels**, **mark distribution**, and the **University Exam** pattern as specified in the official KTU 2024 regulations.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define *Skills Required* in the context of traditional boat building. List any **four** core carpentry skills every shipwright must possess.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Skills Required* in traditional boat building refer to the integrated set of **technical, manual, cognitive, and managerial competencies** a boatbuilder must possess to construct wooden vessels using heritage methods.

**Four core carpentry skills (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
1. **Measuring & Marking** — use of spirit level, bevel square, marking gauge.
2. **Cutting & Shaping** — operation of hand-saws, chisels, planes, adze.
3. **Joinery** — making mortise-and-tenon, scarph, rabbet, dovetail joints.
4. **Assembly & Alignment** — fitting frames, planking, and decking to loft lines.

*(1 mark reserved for the definition.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What is *sub-contracting*? State **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage of sub-contracting in a traditional boat yard.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Sub-contracting is the practice wherein the principal boatyard (contractor) outsources a specific, well-defined portion of work to a **specialised third-party vendor** under a secondary contractual agreement, while retaining overall accountability.

**Advantages (1 mark each):**
- **Access to Specialised Skills** — e.g., marine engine installation, electronics, sail-making.
- **Cost Efficiency** — avoids capital investment in skills used only occasionally.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):**
- **Quality & Schedule Risk** — yard remains liable to the client even if the sub-contractor defaults or delivers late.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Examiners consistently deduct marks for: (a) writing only a *definition* without applying it to a boat-building context, (b) missing the *3-S* in any sub-contracting answer, and (c) confusing *in-house skill* with *sub-contracted skill*. Read sub-parts (a) and (b) carefully — they are designed to test **two different Bloom levels** (e.g., *Apply* in (a) and *Evaluate* in (b)).

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, Full Question]**
> **Q3 (a).** Explain the **five-layer skill pyramid** of a traditional boatbuilder. For each layer, give **one example skill** and **one way** the yard can develop or acquire it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The five-layer skill pyramid is a KTU-recommended framework that organises a traditional boatbuilder's competencies from broad foundational literacy at the base to managerial expertise at the apex.

**Layer-by-Layer Explanation (1 mark per layer × 5 = 5 marks):**

| Layer | Example Skill | Method of Development / Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Foundational | Reading a scaled drawing | In-house induction training, ITI certification |
| 2. Core Carpentry | Cutting a tenon joint | Apprenticeship under a master shipwright (3-year programme) |
| 3. Specialist Boat-Building | Lofting full-scale lines on the floor | Mentorship by senior loftman, on-job rotations |
| 4. Auxiliary Craft | Splicing a wire halyard | Specialised short-courses at maritime institutes |
| 5. Managerial & Interpersonal | Sub-contractor coordination | Leadership workshops, KTU NEP 2020 management modules |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A yard that nurtures all five layers develops a **self-renewing talent pipeline**, reducing long-term dependence on external sub-contractors.

---

> **Q3 (b).** Discuss the **make-or-buy decision** in a traditional boat yard. Using a **Make-or-Buy Decision Matrix**, evaluate whether *engine installation* and *planking* should be sub-contracted in a 35-foot fishing trawler built at a Kerala heritage yard. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Theoretical Framework (2 marks):**
The make-or-buy decision rests on **four pillars**: strategic importance, in-house competence, cost efficiency, and capacity strain. A yard sub-contracts when the activity is **non-core, skill-deficient, cost-inefficient in-house, and non-capacity-blocking**.

**Make-or-Buy Matrix for the Case Yard (3 marks):**

| Work Package | Strategic Importance | In-House Skill | Cost Gap | Capacity Strain | **Decision** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Planking | 5 (Core) | 5 (Strong) | 1 (Low) | 3 (Moderate) | **In-House** |
| Engine Installation | 3 (Non-Core) | 1 (Weak) | 5 (High) | 1 (Low) | **Sub-Contract** |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- **Planking** is the *signature skill* of a traditional Kerala yard; the *hood-end technique, the cold-moulded planking* on *teak and coconut-wood frames* is the brand identity. Therefore, **planking must remain in-house**, even if it strains capacity, because losing this skill is losing the yard's heritage value.
- **Engine Installation** is *non-core*, requires *OEM-certified marine engineers*, demands *specialised tools*, and is a *low-frequency activity*. Sub-contracting to a **Cochin Port-trusted marine engine vendor** is the rational choice.

**Conclusion:** The yard's *brand* is preserved by retaining *planking*; *cost* and *compliance* are optimised by sub-contracting *engine installation*.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, Full Question]**
> **Q4 (a).** Describe the **5-Filter Sub-Contractor Selection Model** for a traditional boatyard. Apply it to a hypothetical sub-contract — *electrical wiring of a 40-foot passenger vessel* — and justify pass/fail at each filter. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Theoretical Outline of the 5-Filter Model (2 marks):**
The model sequentially filters sub-contractor candidates on **Capability, Capacity, Cost, Compliance, and Chemistry**. Failure at any single filter disqualifies the candidate.

**Application to the Hypothetical Case (1 mark per filter × 5 = 5 marks):**

| Filter | Description | Application to the Case | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Capability | Does the sub-contractor have the *technical certification*? | The vendor holds a *Marine Electrical License* from Mercantile Marine Department. | **PASS** |
| 2. Capacity | Can they handle the *volume and timeline*? | The vendor has 2 master electricians + 4 helpers; can complete the 40-ft vessel wiring in 15 days. | **PASS** |
| 3. Cost | Is the quote within *BoM envelope*? | Quote = ₹2.4 lakhs; BoM allocation = ₹2.5 lakhs. | **PASS** |
| 4. Compliance | Adheres to *safety & labour law*? | Vendor provides PPE to workers, follows ESI/PF norms. | **PASS** |
| 5. Chemistry | Will the foreman and vendor collaborate? | Prior 3-vessel track record with the yard; foreman has worked with vendor's lead electrician. | **PASS** |

**Conclusion:** The vendor passes all 5 filters and is awarded the electrical sub-contract.

---

> **Q4 (b).** Explain the **3-S Sub-Contracting Contract** framework. Why is the omission of any one of the 3-S considered a *contractual risk* in a boat-building project? Illustrate with a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition of the 3-S (1 mark):**
A robust sub-contracting contract in traditional boat building must contain three pillars — **Scope, Schedule, Specification**.

**Explanation of each S (1.5 marks × 3 = 4.5 marks):**

- **Scope** — defines *what* is included and *what is excluded*. Without scope clarity, *scope-creep* occurs, and the yard pays for work it never ordered.
- **Schedule** — defines *milestones* and *penalties*. Without a schedule clause, *delays* cannot be penalised, and the launch date slips.
- **Specification** — defines *quality standards, material grade, defect liability period (DLP)*. Without specifications, *defective work* (e.g., substandard marine-grade cable) is hard to reject.

**Real-World Illustration (1 mark):**
> A Beypore yard sub-contracted *caulking* without specifying the **oakum grade and DLP**. The sub-contractor used low-grade cotton that rotted within 8 months. Because *specification* was missing, the yard could not legally compel re-work, and the *yard's reputation* suffered.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The 3-S framework is the **minimal contractual armour** of a traditional boatyard; omitting any one element shifts *risk* from the sub-contractor to the yard.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Skills Required & Sub-Contracting)**

- ☐ **Two Pillars of Module 3:** *Skills Required* (human capital) and *Sub-Contracting* (organisational architecture).
- ☐ **5-Layer Skill Pyramid:** Foundational → Core Carpentry → Specialist Boat-Building → Auxiliary Craft → Managerial.
- ☐ **Dreyfus Model:** Novice → Advanced Beginner → Competent → Proficient → Expert.
- ☐ **Mastery Horizon:** 7 to 10 years of deliberate, mentored practice.
- ☐ **Skill Multiplication:** 1 master can mentor 3 to 5 apprentices effectively.
- ☐ **Make-or-Buy Rule:** Retain *core/strategic* skills; sub-contract *non-core/specialised* work.
- ☐ **3-S Contract Mandate:** *Scope + Schedule + Specification* are non-negotiable.
- ☐ **5-Filter Selection:** Capability → Capacity → Cost → Compliance → Chemistry.
- ☐ **Core-Fit Decision Logic:** Sub-contract when *skill ≤ 2* AND *cost-gap ≥ 4*.
- ☐ **Yard Liability Ladder:** The yard is *always* liable to the client, irrespective of sub-contract tier.
- ☐ **Risk Equation (Conceptual):** $R_{sc} = (P_{default} \times I_{impact}) + (P_{quality} \times I_{rework}) + (P_{delay} \times I_{schedule})$.
- ☐ **Kerala Heritage Yards:** Retain *planking, caulking, lofting*; sub-contract *engine, electrical, sail-making, modern electronics*.
- ☐ **Quality Gate (Q-Gate):** Stage-wise inspection mandatory before any sub-contractor payment is released.
- ☐ **Documentation Triad:** Every sub-contract must reduce to Scope + Schedule + Specification.
- ☐ **Common Exam Pitfall:** Examiners deduct marks for *generic* answers; always **anchor** your answer to a *boat-building context*.
