topics.title: Insurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Insurance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Insurance** is a contractual arrangement (policy) in which an insurer agrees, in exchange for a premium, to provide financial protection or reimbursement against losses incurred by the insured party. In the context of **Traditional Boat Building Techniques**, insurance constitutes a specialized risk-management tool that protects the boat builder, owner, and operator from financial loss arising from perils such as hull damage, total loss, third-party liability, cargo loss, and construction-stage risks.

For a traditional wooden boat, insurance is uniquely complex because the asset is simultaneously a **cultural heritage object**, a **functional marine vessel**, and a **high-value depreciable asset** whose value is often appraised through craftsmanship, age, and historical significance rather than market price alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Insurance Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *"A contract whereby the insurer agrees to indemnify the insured against losses incident to marine adventure, in consideration of a premium, in the manner and to the extent thereby agreed."*
> — This is the classical definition rooted in the **Marine Insurance Act, 1906** (UK) and adopted by Indian marine insurance practice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just spent **two years** hand-carving a traditional *Vallam* (Kerala backwater boat) using jackfruit wood and natural resin. You have invested:

- **₹8,00,000** in materials and labor
- **1,500 hours** of artisan craftsmanship
- An **irreplaceable cultural lineage** passed down by your grandfather

Now consider a sudden squall that sinks your boat. Without insurance, the loss is **total and unrecoverable**. Insurance is essentially a **mutual promise** — you pay a small, certain annual amount (the **premium**) so that a large, uncertain loss (the **claim**) becomes manageable. The insurer pools risks from many boat owners, so when one vessel is lost, the collective fund compensates the affected party.

Think of it like this:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lifeboat Principle of Insurance:**
> Insurance is like a community lifeboat. No single fisherman can build one alone, but when everyone contributes a small plank, the entire fleet is protected. The premium is your plank; the claim is your rescue.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Insurer** | The insurance company offering protection |
| **Insured / Assured** | The boat builder or boat owner seeking protection |
| **Premium** | The consideration (fee) paid by the insured to the insurer |
| **Sum Insured** | The maximum amount the insurer will pay on a claim |
| **Policy** | The legal contract documenting the insurance agreement |
| **Claim** | A formal demand by the insured for indemnity after a loss |
| **Indemnity** | The principle that compensation restores the insured to the pre-loss financial position — **never profit** |
| **Peril** | The cause of loss (e.g., storm, fire, collision) |
| **Risk** | The uncertainty about the occurrence of a peril |
| **Hazard** | A condition that increases the likelihood of a peril |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Foundational Principles of Insurance (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Uberrima Fides (Utmost Good Faith)** — Both parties must disclose all material facts.
> 2. **Insurable Interest** — The insured must have a legally recognized stake in the subject matter.
> 3. **Indemnity** — Compensation equals actual loss, never more.
> 4. **Proximate Cause** — The dominant, effective cause of loss must be an insured peril.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Insurance Relevant to Traditional Boat Building

Insurance for traditional boat building is broadly classified into **Life Insurance**, **General Insurance**, and **Marine Insurance**, with marine insurance being the most directly relevant. Marine insurance is further subdivided based on the nature of the marine adventure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Marine Insurance distinct?**
> Marine insurance is governed by its own centuries-old legal framework (Marine Insurance Act 1906) because traditional boats operate in a **highly variable, geographically dispersed, and physically dangerous** environment where loss events are dramatic and documentation is challenging.

### 2.1.1 Hull Insurance

Hull insurance covers **physical damage to the vessel itself** — the hull, machinery, equipment, and fittings. For traditional wooden boats, hull insurance must account for:

- Natural wood deterioration (rot, dry rot, marine borer attack)
- Weather-related damage (cyclones, monsoons, storm surges)
- Collision and grounding
- Fire and explosion
- Theft of vessel or components

The sum insured is typically the **agreed value** of the boat, which for a heritage vessel may be substantially higher than its reconstruction cost due to cultural and craftsmanship value.

### 2.1.2 Cargo Insurance

Cargo insurance protects goods being transported by the traditional boat. For Vallams, Uru boats, and other cargo-carrying traditional vessels operating in Kerala backwaters, this covers:

- Loss or damage to cargo during transit
- Loading and unloading risks
- General average contributions
- Particular average losses

### 2.1.3 Liability Insurance

Liability insurance protects the boat owner/builder against **third-party claims** for bodily injury, property damage, or pollution caused by the vessel. This is critical because traditional boats operating near tourism hubs, fishing zones, or passenger routes face significant exposure to third-party risks.

### 2.1.4 Builders' Risk Insurance (Construction Insurance)

This is **specifically relevant to boat builders**. It covers the vessel while it is **under construction, on the slipway, or in the yard**. Traditional boat building takes months to years, and during this period the partially completed hull is exposed to:

- Fire (especially if the caulking or painting involves flammable materials)
- Windstorm damage to the open hull
- Theft of timber, fittings, and tools
- Flooding if launched prematurely
- Damage during trial runs

### 2.1.5 Protection and Indemnity (P&I) Insurance

P&I insurance covers a wide range of third-party liabilities not typically included in standard hull policies, including:

- Crew injury or death
- Stowaways
- Collision liability
- Pollution liability
- Wreck removal costs

## 2.2 Underlying Theory: How Insurance Pricing Works

Insurance pricing is fundamentally an exercise in **risk quantification**. For a traditional boat builder, the premium is calculated based on:

$$\text{Premium} = \text{Sum Insured} \times \text{Rate} \times \text{Loading Factors} - \text{Discounts}$$

Where:
- **Sum Insured** = Appraised value of the boat (₹)
- **Rate** = Risk rate per ₹100 of sum insured (varies by vessel type, age, usage)
- **Loading Factors** = Multipliers for additional risks (e.g., +15% for cyclone-prone zones, +20% for vessels over 50 years old)
- **Discounts** = Reductions for safety features, no-claim history, etc.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are examinable under KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Memorize the variable definitions and the units.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Premium** | $P = \dfrac{S \times R}{100}$ | $P$ = Premium, $S$ = Sum Insured, $R$ = Rate | $P$ in ₹, $S$ in ₹, $R$ in % |
| 2 | **Gross Premium** | $GP = P + \text{Add-ons (GST, Stamp)} $ | $GP$ = Gross Premium, $P$ = Basic Premium | ₹ |
| 3 | **Average Clause Claim (Partial Loss)** | $C = \dfrac{S}{MV} \times AL$ | $C$ = Claim, $S$ = Sum Insured, $MV$ = Market Value, $AL$ = Actual Loss | ₹ |
| 4 | **Pro-rata Average** | $C = \dfrac{S}{MV} \times AL$ | Same as above; applied when under-insurance exists | ₹ |
| 5 | **Excess / Deductible** | $C = AL - D$ | $C$ = Claim, $AL$ = Actual Loss, $D$ = Deductible | ₹ |
| 6 | **Loss Ratio** | $LR = \dfrac{\text{Claims Incurred}}{\text{Premium Earned}}$ | $LR$ = Loss Ratio | Dimensionless (typically expressed as %) |
| 7 | **Pure Risk Premium** | $P_{pure} = P \times \text{Expected Loss Probability}$ | Used in actuarial calculations | ₹ |
| 8 | **No-Claim Bonus (NCB) Discount** | $D_{NCB} = P \times n\%$ | $n$ = NCB percentage (e.g., 20%, 25%, 30%, 40%, 50%) | ₹ |
| 9 | **General Average Contribution** | $GA_i = \dfrac{\text{Value of } i\text{'s interest}}{\text{Total Sacrificed + Saved Value}} \times \text{Loss}$ | All traditional cargo boat operators | ₹ |
| 10 | **Salvage Value** | $SV = AV \times (1 - d)^n$ | $SV$ = Salvage, $AV$ = Assessed Value, $d$ = Depreciation rate, $n$ = years | ₹ |

## 2.4 The Indemnity Principle — Mathematical Treatment

The cornerstone of marine insurance is **indemnity**: the insured must be restored to the financial position they were in immediately before the loss, but not better off. For a traditional boat valued at $V$ with sum insured $S$ and actual loss $AL$:

$$\text{Claim Payable} = \min(AL, S, V - SV)$$

Where $SV$ is the salvage value (what remains of the asset after loss).

If the boat is **under-insured** ($S < V$), the average clause applies and the insured becomes a co-insurer for the shortfall:

$$\text{Claim} = \frac{S}{V} \times AL$$

This discourages under-insurance and forces accurate valuation — a critical consideration for traditional boat builders who often undervalue their heritage vessels for premium savings, only to face severe underpayment at claim time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For traditional boat builders in Kerala and across South Asia, insurance is not merely a financial product — it is a **continuity mechanism** for intangible cultural heritage. A Vallam, Uru, or Masula boat represents a **multi-generational skill lineage**. The loss of such a vessel without insurance often means the loss of:

- The economic livelihood of the builder family
- A unique construction technique
- Cultural and historical artifacts
- Local employment in traditional boat-building clusters (e.g., **Kalluvathukkal, Beypore, Thaikkal**)

Therefore, insurance directly supports the **sustainability of traditional boat-building crafts** — a strategic goal aligned with India's cultural preservation policies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Premium Calculation for a Traditional Vallam

**Problem Statement:**
A traditional boat builder in Kuttanad owns a 40-year-old wooden Vallam appraised at **₹6,00,000**. The insurer quotes a base rate of **1.8%** for hull insurance. The vessel operates in a cyclone-prone zone (loading factor **+15%**), has no fire-fighting equipment (loading factor **+10%**), but the builder has a 5-year no-claim history (discount **20%**). GST is **18%** on the net premium. Calculate the **total premium payable**.

### Step 1: Identify the Base Sum Insured
The sum insured is the appraised value of the boat:
$$S = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 2: Determine the Applicable Rate
The base rate is given as:
$$R_{base} = 1.8\%$$

### Step 3: Apply Loading Factors
Loading factors are multiplicative on the base rate. We calculate the **effective rate**:

$$R_{eff} = R_{base} \times (1 + L_1) \times (1 + L_2)$$

Where $L_1 = 0.15$ (cyclone zone) and $L_2 = 0.10$ (no fire equipment):

$$R_{eff} = 1.8 \times (1 + 0.15) \times (1 + 0.10)$$

$$R_{eff} = 1.8 \times 1.15 \times 1.10$$

$$R_{eff} = 1.8 \times 1.265$$

$$R_{eff} = 2.277\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Each loading factor compounds because each represents an independent, multiplicative risk increase. Cyclone exposure and lack of fire safety both raise the insurer's expected payout.

### Step 4: Calculate the Loaded Premium

$$P_{loaded} = \frac{S \times R_{eff}}{100}$$

$$P_{loaded} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 \times 2.277}{100}$$

$$P_{loaded} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02277$$

$$P_{loaded} = 13{,}662 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 5: Apply the No-Claim Bonus Discount

$$D_{NCB} = P_{loaded} \times 0.20$$

$$D_{NCB} = 13{,}662 \times 0.20 = 2{,}732.40 \text{ ₹}$$

Net Premium after discount:
$$P_{net} = P_{loaded} - D_{NCB}$$

$$P_{net} = 13{,}662 - 2{,}732.40 = 10{,}929.60 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 6: Add GST (18%)

$$P_{gross} = P_{net} \times (1 + 0.18)$$

$$P_{gross} = 10{,}929.60 \times 1.18$$

$$P_{gross} = 12{,}896.93 \text{ ₹}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{P_{gross} \approx \text{₹ } 12{,}896.93}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Claim Calculation Using the Average Clause

**Problem Statement:**
A traditional Uru boat is insured for **₹4,00,000** (sum insured $S$). At the time of partial loss, the actual market value of the boat is **₹5,00,000** (value at risk $V$). The boat sustains storm damage of **₹1,50,000** (actual loss $AL$). Calculate the claim payable to the insured.

### Step 1: Identify the Under-Insurance Condition
Compare sum insured to actual value:
$$S = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} < V = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Since $S < V$, the boat is **under-insured**, and the **average clause** is triggered.

### Step 2: Apply the Average Clause Formula

$$C = \frac{S}{V} \times AL$$

$$C = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$C = 0.80 \times 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$C = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

The insured declared a sum insured that covered only **80%** of the actual value. Therefore, the insurer treats the insured as a self-insurer for the remaining **20%**, and the claim is paid at the same ratio.

The **disallowed amount** is:
$$AL - C = 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

The insured must bear this ₹30,000 loss personally — this is the **penalty for under-insurance**.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{C = \text{₹ } 1{,}20{,}000}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students incorrectly claim the full ₹1,50,000. The average clause is a **mandatory application** by the insurer; failure to apply it is a common error. Memorize the formula $C = (S/V) \times AL$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Salvage Value with Compound Depreciation

**Problem Statement:**
A traditional wooden boat was originally appraised at **₹8,00,000** (assessed value $AV$). It is now **6 years old**, and the annual depreciation rate is **12%** (compounded). What is the current salvage value $SV$ of the hull for insurance purposes?

### Step 1: State the Formula

$$SV = AV \times (1 - d)^n$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values
$$SV = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.12)^6$$

$$SV = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (0.88)^6$$

### Step 3: Calculate $(0.88)^6$ Step-by-Step
$$(0.88)^2 = 0.7744$$

$$(0.88)^3 = 0.7744 \times 0.88 = 0.681472$$

$$(0.88)^6 = (0.681472)^2 = 0.464404 \text{ (approx.)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Final Salvage Value
$$SV = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.464404$$

$$SV = 3{,}71{,}523.20 \text{ ₹}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{SV \approx \text{₹ } 3{,}71{,}523}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Traditional wooden boats depreciate faster than steel or fiberglass hulls due to biological degradation (rot, borer attack). A 12% annual depreciation is realistic for an unmaintained hull, but **well-maintained traditional boats** (regular caulking, oil treatment, dry-docking) can have depreciation rates as low as **6–8%** annually.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Insurance Type × Risk Scenario for Traditional Boats

| Risk Scenario | Applicable Insurance | Key Coverage Feature | Typical Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclone sinks moored Vallam | **Hull Insurance** | Total loss due to natural peril | Sum Insured |
| Fire during caulking with resin | **Builders' Risk Insurance** | Construction-stage fire | Sum Insured + escalation |
| Tourist injured falling from boat | **Liability Insurance** | Third-party bodily injury | Pre-agreed liability limit |
| Cargo (coconuts) damaged by water seepage | **Cargo Insurance** | Particular average | Insured value of cargo |
| Crew member drowns during fishing | **P&I Insurance** | Crew death/injury | Statutory + agreed |
| Boat aged 60 years, partial rot | **Hull Insurance** (subject to survey) | May exclude pre-existing decay | Sum Insured |
| Theft of oars and equipment | **Hull Insurance** (with theft extension) | Theft of components | Sub-limit of 20–30% |
| Wreck blocks backwater channel | **P&I Insurance** | Wreck removal cost | Sub-limit of 10–25% |
| Oil/fuel spill from engine | **P&I Insurance** | Pollution liability | As per policy schedule |
| Loss during inland navigation only | **Inland Hull Insurance** (sub-class) | Restricted to rivers/lakes | Reduced premium applies |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define the principle of indemnity in marine insurance. Why can the insured never receive a profit from a claim?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The principle of **indemnity** states that the insured must be restored to the **same financial position** they occupied immediately before the loss occurred, but **not made better off**. In marine insurance, this means the insurer's liability is limited to the **actual loss amount**, the **sum insured**, or the **insurable value of the subject matter**, whichever is lowest. The rationale is that insurance is a **risk-sharing mechanism**, not a profit-making venture. Permitting profit would create **moral hazard** — boat builders might intentionally allow losses or over-declare values to gain financially, which would destabilize the entire insurance pool. For traditional boat builders, this principle also ensures that the **cultural and economic integrity** of the vessel is preserved through accurate valuation rather than speculative claims.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**List and briefly explain any three principles of insurance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Utmost Good Faith (Uberrima Fides):** Both the insured and the insurer must disclose all material facts truthfully. A boat builder must declare the boat's true age, condition, modifications, and usage patterns.
2. **Insurable Interest:** The insured must have a legal or equitable relationship with the subject matter. A traditional boat builder has insurable interest in vessels they own, are constructing, or have financed.
3. **Proximate Cause:** The dominant, effective cause of loss must be an insured peril. If a Vallam sinks due to a combination of cyclonic wind and overloading, the insurer must identify the proximate (operative) cause.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**A traditional wooden boat is insured for ₹5,00,000 against a market value of ₹7,50,000. The boat suffers partial damage of ₹2,25,000 due to a cyclonic storm. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **average clause** in marine insurance and justify its application in this case. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Calculate the **claim payable** by the insurer and the **loss borne by the insured**. What lesson does this teach traditional boat builders about insurance valuation? **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Explanation of the Average Clause \[7 Marks\]

The **average clause** (also called the **co-insurance clause** or **pro-rata condition of average**) is a contractual provision in marine insurance policies that **reduces the claim payable proportionally** when the sum insured is less than the actual value of the insured property. Its purpose is to prevent **under-insurance**, which would otherwise allow the insured to receive compensation for losses while paying premiums on a lower value.

**Justification for Application in This Case:**
- Sum Insured $S = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- Market Value $V = 7{,}50{,}000$ ₹
- Since $S < V$ (specifically $S = \frac{2}{3}$ of $V$), the boat is **under-insured by 33.33%**.

The average clause treats the insured as their own insurer for the shortfall. This ensures that **the cost of risk is shared fairly** between the insurer and the insured based on the proportion of value actually covered.

##### Part (b) — Claim Calculation and Lesson \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1: Apply the Average Clause Formula**

$$C = \frac{S}{V} \times AL$$

$$C = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{7{,}50{,}000} \times 2{,}25{,}000$$

$$C = 0.6667 \times 2{,}25{,}000$$

$$C = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Insured's Self-Borne Loss**

$$L_{insured} = AL - C = 2{,}25{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 75{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{C = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000 \quad ; \quad L_{insured} = \text{₹ } 75{,}000}$$

**Lesson for Traditional Boat Builders:**
Traditional boat builders must always insure their vessels for **full market value (or agreed value)** to avoid the average clause penalty. Under-insurance may save premium money in the short term, but it **substantially reduces claim recovery** at the time of loss. For heritage vessels, the agreed value should incorporate **craftsmanship, historical significance, and replacement cost**, not just market resale value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students incorrectly write the formula as $C = S - AL$ or $C = V - AL$. The correct formula is **strictly $C = (S/V) \times AL$**, which is the proportional reduction. Also, do not forget to compute the **insured's self-borne loss** as $AL - C$. Both components carry marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**A traditional boat builder in Kerala insures a Uru boat under construction for ₹10,00,000. The construction period is 18 months. The insurer applies a base rate of 1.2%, a construction-stage loading of +25%, and a depreciation loading of +8%. The builder receives a 15% discount for safety equipment installation. GST is 18%.**

**(a)** Calculate the **net premium** payable (before GST). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the importance of **Builders' Risk Insurance** specifically for traditional boat construction, citing at least four unique risk factors. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Net Premium Calculation \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1: Identify Sum Insured and Base Rate**

$$S = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}, \quad R_{base} = 1.2\%$$

**Step 2: Apply Loading Factors Compounded**

$$R_{eff} = R_{base} \times (1 + L_1) \times (1 + L_2)$$

$$R_{eff} = 1.2 \times (1 + 0.25) \times (1 + 0.08)$$

$$R_{eff} = 1.2 \times 1.25 \times 1.08$$

$$R_{eff} = 1.2 \times 1.35 = 1.62\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate Loaded Premium**

$$P_{loaded} = \frac{S \times R_{eff}}{100} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1.62}{100} = 16{,}200 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4: Apply 15% Safety Equipment Discount**

$$D = P_{loaded} \times 0.15 = 16{,}200 \times 0.15 = 2{,}430 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Net Premium**

$$P_{net} = P_{loaded} - D = 16{,}200 - 2{,}430 = 13{,}770 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6: Gross Premium with GST (for reference, not asked)**

$$P_{gross} = 13{,}770 \times 1.18 = 16{,}248.60 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\boxed{P_{net} = \text{₹ } 13{,}770}$$

##### Part (b) — Importance of Builders' Risk Insurance \[7 Marks\]

Builders' Risk Insurance is uniquely important for traditional boat construction because of four critical risk factors:

1. **Extended Construction Period:** Traditional boats like Uru and Vallam take 6–24 months to build. During this period, the partially completed hull is exposed to weather, theft, and accidental damage. A standard hull policy does not cover a vessel under construction.

2. **Open-Hull Vulnerability:** A traditional wooden hull under construction is essentially an **open shell** without sealed decks, watertight bulkheads, or final caulking. A single rainstorm or unauthorized entry can cause disproportionate damage compared to a finished vessel.

3. **Flammable Materials on Site:** Traditional construction uses resins, oils, paints, and curing agents. **Fire is the single largest insured peril during construction**, and the presence of timber amplifies the risk.

4. **Slipway and Trial-Run Risks:** Launching a traditional boat is a high-risk event. Damage during the slipway release, initial flotation, and trial runs is a frequent source of loss that only a Builders' Risk policy covers.

Additionally, **theft of high-value timber** (e.g., imported teak, rosewood, or jackfruit wood planks) is a substantial risk because these materials are often stored on open yards for months.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> For part (a), students often forget to **compound the loading factors** and instead simply add them ($1.2 + 0.25 + 0.08 = 1.53\%$). Loading factors in insurance are **multiplicative, not additive**, because each represents an independent probability multiplier. For part (b), the examiner expects **specific risk factors** (fire, theft, weather, slipway) — not generic statements like "boats can get damaged." Be specific.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minure KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Insurance** = Contractual risk transfer for a price (premium). The four foundational principles are **Uberrima Fides, Insurable Interest, Indemnity, Proximate Cause**.
- ✅ **Marine Insurance** is the most relevant insurance type for traditional boat builders — governed by the **Marine Insurance Act, 1906** (still legally referenced in India).
- ✅ **Five key insurance types for traditional boats:** Hull, Cargo, Liability, P&I, and **Builders' Risk** (construction-stage).
- ✅ **Premium formula:** $P = (S \times R_{eff}) / 100$, where $R_{eff} = R_{base} \times \prod(1 + L_i)$.
- ✅ **Average Clause (Pro-rata) formula:** $C = (S / V) \times AL$ — applied when $S < V$ (under-insurance).
- ✅ **Indemnity Cap:** Claim payable = $\min(AL, S, V - SV)$. The insured can never profit from a claim.
- ✅ **Salvage Value formula:** $SV = AV \times (1 - d)^n$ — used for depreciated asset valuation.
- ✅ **No-Claim Bonus (NCB):** Discount on renewal premium, often **20% → 25% → 30% → 40% → 50%** for consecutive claim-free years. NCB is **forfeited** if a claim is made.
- ✅ **General Average:** A shared sacrifice in a marine emergency (e.g., jettisoning cargo to save a sinking boat) — all parties contribute proportionally.
- ✅ **Particular Average:** A partial loss borne solely by the affected party (no contribution from others).
- ✅ **Traditional boat depreciation rate:** Typically **8–12% per annum** for wooden hulls (vs. 5–7% for steel). Well-maintained hulls depreciate slower.
- ✅ **Builders' Risk Insurance** is **mandatory** for construction periods exceeding 6 months, especially for heritage vessels.
- ✅ **Insurable Interest** must exist **at the time of loss**, not merely at the time of policy issuance.
- ✅ **Subrogation:** After paying a claim, the insurer can step into the shoes of the insured to recover from third parties responsible for the loss.
- ✅ **Contribution:** When the same risk is insured with multiple insurers, each contributes proportionally to the claim.
- ✅ **Cyclone-prone zones** in Kerala (e.g., Alappuzha, Ernakulam coastal belts) attract **+15% to +25%** premium loading for hull insurance.
- ✅ **Heritage boat valuation** often uses **agreed value** rather than market value, preserving cultural and craftsmanship premiums.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** When answering KTU questions on insurance, always:
> 1. State the relevant principle/formula explicitly.
> 2. Show **all** calculation steps (partial marking rewards intermediate work).
> 3. Conclude with a **practical interpretation** of the numerical result.
> 4. For theory questions, link concepts to **traditional boat-building context** (Kerala-specific Vallam, Uru, Masula boats) — this earns appreciation marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inland Waterway Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inland Waterway Regulations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Inland Waterway Regulations** refer to the comprehensive body of national and international laws, rules, codes, and administrative directives that govern the design, construction, registration, operation, navigation, safety, environmental compliance, and commercial use of vessels plying on rivers, canals, backwaters, lakes, and other internal navigable water bodies of a country. In the Indian context, this is primarily administered through the **National Waterways Act, 2016**, the **Inland Vessels Act, 1917** (as amended), and the **Indian Ports Act, 1908**, supplemented by rules framed by the **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** under the Ministry of Shipping (now Ministry of Ports, Shipping & Waterways). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Traditional boat building intersects with waterway regulations because traditional vessels (dugouts, plank-built country craft, vallams, etc.) must comply with registration norms, safety standards, and operational limits prescribed for the waterway class on which they are permitted to ply. ### 1.2 Key Regulatory Bodies in India | Authority | Full Form | Primary Role | |---|---|---| | IWAI | Inland Waterways Authority of India | Development, maintenance & regulation of National Waterways | | CIWTC | Central Inland Water Transport Corporation Ltd. | Government undertaking for IWT operations | | Mercantile Marine Department (MMD) | — | Inspection, certification, survey of vessels | | State IWT Departments | — | Regulation of state waterways & minor vessels | | Ministry of Ports, Shipping & Waterways | — | Policy formulation & legislative amendments | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of an inland waterway as a "road without painted lanes" — the regulations are the lane markings, traffic signals, vehicle registration rules, and pollution checks combined.** Just as motor vehicles need RTO registration, fitness certificates, driver licenses, and emission norms to legally use a public road, every traditional or modern boat must have a **Certificate of Registration**, a **Certificate of Survey/Fitness**, a **Navigating License** for the operator, and compliance with **pollution/environmental norms** to legally use a National Waterway. The "tonnage" of the boat, the "class" of the waterway, and the "rules of the road at sea/inland" all dictate what equipment the vessel must carry. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Inland Waterway Regulation - **Tonnage (GRT/NRT):** Gross and Net Registered Tonnage, measured in **tonnes (t)**, where 1 RT = **100 cubic feet (2.83 m³)** of enclosed volume. - **Length Overall (LOA):** Maximum hull length, typically regulated in **metres (m)**. - **Air Draft:** Vertical distance from waterline to highest fixed point of vessel, critical for bridge clearance, measured in **metres (m)**. - **Draft:** Vertical distance from waterline to keel, regulated for fairway depth in **metres (m)**. - **Waterway Class (as per European/PIANC standard):** Categorized from **Class I (small recreational)** to **Class VII (large sea-river vessels)**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Indian Inland Waterway Law The regulatory pyramid can be visualized as four descending tiers: **Tier 1 — Primary Legislation (Acts of Parliament)** - National Waterways Act, 2016 - Inland Vessels Act, 1917 - Indian Ports Act, 1908 (relevant provisions) - Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 (inland parts) **Tier 2 — Subordinate Legislation (Rules & Notifications)** - Inland Vessels (Amendment) Rules - National Waterways (Conservation of Heritage) Rules - State Boating Rules (Kerala, Goa, Assam, etc.) **Tier 3 — Technical Standards & Codes** - Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) rules for inland vessels - Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI) technical circulars - Central Inland Water Transport Corporation (CIWTC) standard specifications **Tier 4 — Operational Directives** - Local navigation notices, fairway advisories, lock operating procedures, pilotage rules. ### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' of Waterway Regulation **Why regulate?** - **Safety of life** — prevent capsizing, fire, collision on congested waterways. - **Protection of environment** — control oil discharge, sewage, garbage. - **Preservation of heritage** — traditional boats must be surveyed to keep operating. - **Economic governance** — ensure fairway depth is maintained, tolls collected. - **Sovereignty & customs** — prevent smuggling, illegal immigration on river borders. **How is it implemented?** 1. **Survey & Registration** of every vessel above a prescribed size (typically > **15 m LOA** or > **100 GT** in India, with lower thresholds for passenger vessels). 2. **Issuance of Certificates** — Certificate of Registration, Certificate of Survey, Certificate of Fitness, Insurance Certificate, Pollution Prevention Certificate. 3. **Licensing of Personnel** — Boat Master, Serang, Engine Driver certificates issued by MMD. 4. **Enforcement** — Inspections by IWAI, MMD, State Police, Coast Guard (for coastal-inland). 5. **Classification of Fairways** — based on depth, width, air draft, and current. ### 2.3 KTU Concept Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Description / Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | National Waterway-1 (NW-1) | Ganga river system, Allahabad–Haldia, **1620 km** | Longest waterway in India | | National Waterway-2 (NW-2) | Brahmaputra, Dhubri–Sadiya, **891 km** | Major NE corridor | | National Waterway-3 (NW-3) | West Coast Canal + Champakara + Udyogmandal, Kerala, **205 km** | Backwater tourism & cargo | | National Waterway-4 (NW-4) | Krishna–Godavari rivers + Canals, **1095 km** | Southern India connectivity | | National Waterway-5 (NW-5) | Brahmani–Mahanadi, **623 km** | East coast linkage | | Class A waterway (India, IWAI) | Min. depth **1.2 m**, width **32 m** | For vessels up to ~1000 DWT | | Class B waterway (India, IWAI) | Min. depth **1.0 m**, width **22 m** | For vessels up to ~500 DWT | | Class C waterway (India, IWAI) | Min. depth **0.7 m**, width **14 m** | For country boats & small craft | | Traditional wooden boat threshold | Vessels < **15 m** LOA in many states | State-level regulation only | | Passenger vessel limit (Inland) | Typically < **12 pax** for non-registered craft | Above this = MMD survey required | | Speed limit (inland India) | Generally **10–15 km/h** in canals | Prevents bank erosion | | Minimum life jacket | 1 per person on board | Mandatory for all vessels | | Fire extinguisher (small craft) | 1 × **2 kg dry powder** minimum | For craft < 15 m | | Anchor mass formula (approx) | $M_a = 0.05 \times \text{GRT}$ (kg) | Rule-of-thumb for traditional craft | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Boat Builders** must know the waterway class to design hull dimensions, draft, and air draft correctly. - **Material Selection** for traditional boats (e.g., treated wood, FRP coating) is influenced by environmental regulations on anti-fouling paints (TBT ban). - **Structural Design** of hull, bulkheads, and decks must conform to the survey standards (IRS / Lloyd's) for the registered tonnage. - **Operational Planning** by cargo/passenger operators depends on navigation windows, lock timings, and fairway notices. - **Heritage Tourism** projects (e.g., Kerala houseboats, Assam Bhut-Bhotias) require special heritage-class certification. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU:** "**R-S-F-L-E**" = **R**egistration, **S**urvey, **F**itness, **L**icense (crew), **E**nvironmental — the 5 pillars of inland waterway compliance. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Regulatory Calculations ### 3.1 Tonnage Calculation (Used for Registration Class) The **Moorsom System** is the standard method for measuring inland vessel tonnage. **Step 1:** Measure the internal volume $V$ in cubic feet. **Step 2:** Apply the divisor: $$\text{GRT} = \frac{V}{100}$$ > Every **100 cubic feet (≈ 2.83 m³)** of enclosed volume = **1 Registered Ton**. **Step 3:** Deduct non-revenue spaces (crew, machinery, navigation) to obtain **NRT (Net Registered Tonnage)**. $$\text{NRT} = \text{GRT} - \text{Deductions (D)}$$ **Numerical Example:** A traditional cargo vallam has total internal volume $V = 8500 \text{ ft}^3$. Deductions for crew spaces and machinery = 1200 ft³. **Step-by-Step:** $$\text{GRT} = \frac{8500}{100} = 85 \text{ RT}$$ $$\text{NRT} = 85 - \frac{1200}{100} = 85 - 12 = 73 \text{ RT}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The vessel must therefore obtain a Certificate of Survey as an inland cargo vessel, requires an Engine Driver's License, and is subject to Class B waterway operating limits (since > 50 RT). ### 3.2 Air Draft vs. Bridge Clearance Compliance A traditional passenger boat must clear every bridge on its route with safety margin. **Formula for safe passage:** $$A_{\text{clear}} = H_{\text{bridge}} - D_{\text{vessel}} - M_{\text{safety}} - S_{\text{swell}}$$ Where: - $H_{\text{bridge}}$ = lowest bridge soffit level above chart datum (m) - $D_{\text{vessel}}$ = static draft at full load (m) - $M_{\text{safety}}$ = mandatory safety margin = **0.3 m** (inland) to **0.5 m** (tidal) - $S_{\text{swell}}$ = expected wave/swell contribution at high water (m) **Worked Example (NW-3, Kerala):** A houseboat with draft $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$ approaches a bridge with $H = 6.8 \text{ m}$. High water swell $S = 0.15 \text{ m}$. Safety margin $M = 0.3 \text{ m}$. **Step 1:** Compute vessel air draft. $$A_{\text{vessel}} = D + M + S = 1.2 + 0.3 + 0.15 = 1.65 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2:** Check clearance. $$A_{\text{clear}} = 6.8 - 1.65 = 5.15 \text{ m}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The houseboat fits with **5.15 m** of vertical margin — safe to pass. IWAI would issue a "**No Objection Certificate (NOC)**" for the route. ### 3.3 Lightweight / Displacement Relationship for Traditional Hulls Traditional boat regulations often use a length-based rule of thumb for safety equipment (life jackets, fire extinguishers, anchor mass). **Anchor mass rule:** $$M_a = 0.05 \times \text{GRT (in tonnes)}$$ **Worked Example (same vallam as 3.1, GRT = 85 RT ≈ 85 t displacement equivalent):** $$M_a = 0.05 \times 85 = 4.25 \text{ kg}$$ > KTU boards often round to nearest standard stock size: use a **5 kg anchor**. **Life jacket rule:** $$N_{LJ} = N_{\text{crew}} + N_{\text{pax}} + 2 \text{ (children's stock)}$$ For a crew of 3 and 12 passengers: $$N_{LJ} = 3 + 12 + 2 = 17 \text{ jackets}$$ **Fire extinguisher rule (small craft < 15 m):** $$N_{FE} = 1 \text{ (2 kg DCP, minimum)} + 1 \text{ per } 10 \text{ pax above 10}$$ For 12 pax: $$N_{FE} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ extinguishers (2 kg DCP each)}$$ ### 3.4 Compliance Decision Matrix (Traditional Boat Categories) | Boat Type | LOA (m) | GRT | Survey Authority | Crew License | |---|---|---|---|---| | Vallam (Kerala, fishing) | 6–9 | < 10 | State Fisheries Dept. | None (traditional exemption) | | Country cargo boat | 9–14 | 10–50 | State IWT | Boat Master Class-III | | Tourist houseboat | 12–20 | 30–150 | MMD / IRS | Boat Master Class-II + Engine Driver | | Inland cargo barge | 20–40 | 150–500 | MMD + IRS | Master + Chief Engineer | | Sea-river vessel (SRV) | 40–110 | 500–5000 | MMD + IRS + Class | Licensed Master FG + Marine Engineer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A traditional wooden boat under 15 m LOA carrying less than 12 passengers is often **exempt from central MMD survey** but **must hold a state-issued fitness certificate** and **third-party insurance**. ### 3.5 Derivation of Fairway Minimum Depth (Why 1.2 m for Class A?) The IWAI Class A fairway depth is derived from the **squat allowance** plus **safety under-keel clearance (UKC)** for a fully loaded standard barge. **Step 1:** Define required static draft of design vessel $D_s$. **Step 2:** Compute dynamic sinkage (squat) in confined water. $$\text{Squat}_{\text{confined}} = C_s \cdot \frac{V^2}{20} \cdot \left(\frac{\text{Beam}}{h}\right)^2$$ Where $C_s$ is a configuration coefficient, $V$ is vessel speed in knots, and $h$ is water depth. **Step 3:** Apply minimum UKC = **15% of draft** for safe navigation in soft alluvial beds. **Step 4:** Total minimum depth: $$H_{\text{min}} = D_s + \text{Squat} + \text{UKC}$$ **Numerical Example (Class A reference vessel, $D_s = 1.0 \text{ m}$, squat 0.05 m):** $$H_{\text{min}} = 1.0 + 0.05 + 0.15 = 1.20 \text{ m}$$ > This confirms the IWAI Class A specification of **1.2 m minimum depth**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Indian Inland Waterway Regulation ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Constitution of India
Article 246] --> nodeB[Union List Entry 24
Shipping and Navigation on Inland Waterways] nodeB --> nodeC[Primary Legislation] nodeC --> nodeC1[National Waterways Act 2016] nodeC --> nodeC2[Inland Vessels Act 1917] nodeC --> nodeC3[Indian Ports Act 1908] nodeC --> nodeC4[Merchant Shipping Act 1958] nodeC1 --> nodeD[Implementing Authorities] nodeC2 --> nodeD nodeC3 --> nodeD nodeC4 --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE[IWAI] nodeD --> nodeF[Mercantile Marine Dept] nodeD --> nodeG[State IWT Departments] nodeD --> nodeH[Ministry of Ports Shipping Waterways] nodeE --> nodeI[Subordinate Rules] nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ[Survey Standards] nodeI --> nodeK[Registration Procedure] nodeI --> nodeL[Crew Licensing] nodeI --> nodeM[Environmental Rules] nodeJ --> nodeN[Traditional Boat Operators] nodeK --> nodeN nodeL --> nodeN nodeM --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO[Compliance Certificate Issued] ``` ### 4.2 Vessel Compliance Process Flow (Traditional Boat) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Boat Builder / Owner] --> nodeB{Vessel Length and Use} nodeB -->|Under 15m Passenger Below 12| nodeC[State Boating License Only] nodeB -->|Over 15m or Cargo or 12 Plus Pax| nodeD[Apply to MMD for Survey] nodeD --> nodeE[Hull Inspection by Surveyor] nodeE --> nodeF[Stability and Loadline Check] nodeF --> nodeG{Certificate Issued} nodeG -->|Pass| nodeH[Certificate of Survey] nodeG -->|Fail| nodeI[Rectify Defects] nodeI --> nodeE nodeH --> nodeJ[Register with IWAI or State Authority] nodeJ --> nodeK[Obtain Crew Licenses] nodeK --> nodeL[Insurance and Pollution NOC] nodeL --> nodeM[Operational Permit Granted] nodeC --> nodeM ``` ### 4.3 Waterway Classification Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Inland Waterway Classification] --> nodeB[Class A] nodeA --> nodeC[Class B] nodeA --> nodeD[Class C] nodeB --> nodeB1[Depth 1.2m] nodeB --> nodeB2[Width 32m] nodeB --> nodeB3[Vessel Up to 1000 DWT] nodeC --> nodeC1[Depth 1.0m] nodeC --> nodeC2[Width 22m] nodeC --> nodeC3[Vessel Up to 500 DWT] nodeD --> nodeD1[Depth 0.7m] nodeD --> nodeD2[Width 14m] nodeD --> nodeD3[Country Boats and Traditional Craft] nodeB3 --> nodeE[Modern Steel Barges] nodeC3 --> nodeF[Small Cargo and Tourist Boats] nodeD3 --> nodeG[Traditional Wooden Boats Vallams and Canoes] ``` ### 4.4 Safety Equipment Layout on a Traditional Vessel ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Traditional Vessel Safety Equipment] --> nodeB[Personal Safety] nodeA --> nodeC[Fire Safety] nodeA --> nodeD[Navigation Aids] nodeA --> nodeE[Communication] nodeB --> nodeB1[Life Jackets 1 per person Plus 2] nodeB --> nodeB2[Life Buoys Minimum 2] nodeB --> nodeB3[First Aid Kit] nodeC --> nodeC1[Fire Extinguisher 2 kg DCP] nodeC --> nodeC2[Fire Bucket With Lanyard] nodeC --> nodeC3[Sand Box] nodeD --> nodeD1[White Masthead Light] nodeD --> nodeD2[Red Green Side Lights] nodeD --> nodeD3[White Stern Light] nodeD --> nodeD4[Anchor Light When at Anchor] nodeE --> nodeE1[Whistle or Horn] nodeE --> nodeE2[Mobile or VHF Radio] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the term "National Waterway" as per the National Waterways Act, 2016. List any three National Waterways in India along with their length.* **Model Answer:** > **National Waterway** is defined under Section 2 of the National Waterways Act, 2016 as any waterway declared by the Central Government, by notification, to be a national waterway for the purposes of shipping and navigation. > > Three National Waterways: > 1. **NW-1** — Ganga river system (Allahabad to Haldia) — **1620 km** > 2. **NW-2** — Brahmaputra river (Dhubri to Sadiya) — **891 km** > 3. **NW-3** — West Coast Canal and Champakara Canal (Kerala) — **205 km** **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** *State the three mandatory certificates a traditional cargo boat of length 18 m must obtain before plying on National Waterway-3.* **Model Answer:** > 1. **Certificate of Registration** — issued by IWAI or the registering authority. > 2. **Certificate of Survey / Fitness** — issued by the Mercantile Marine Department (MMD) or an authorised surveyor confirming hull integrity and stability. > 3. **Certificate of Insurance** — third-party liability insurance as per Inland Vessels Rules. > > Additionally, the boat must have licensed **Boat Master** and **Engine Driver** on board. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing "National Waterway" with "National Highway", (b) omitting tonnage units (RT vs GT), (c) failing to mention **PIANC/IWAI waterway classification**, and (d) writing about maritime law (IMO/SOLAS) when the question is specifically about **inland** regulations. Always state whether the rule applies to **inland** or **sea-going** vessels. #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** *(a)* Explain in detail the hierarchical structure of inland waterway regulation in India, citing at least three primary Acts and two implementing authorities. (7 marks) *(b)* A traditional wooden cargo vallam has an internal enclosed volume of 12,000 ft³. Deductions for machinery and crew spaces total 1800 ft³. Calculate the GRT and NRT, and determine the class of waterway (A, B, or C) the vessel can operate on as per IWAI norms. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** The regulatory framework is structured in four tiers: **Tier 1 — Primary Legislation (Acts of Parliament):** 1. **National Waterways Act, 2016** — declares 111 waterways as National Waterways; vests development and regulation powers in the Central Government. 2. **Inland Vessels Act, 1917** (as amended) — governs registration, survey, and safety of inland vessels. 3. **Indian Ports Act, 1908** — applicable provisions relating to navigation in port limits and pilotage. 4. **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** — extends to inland-going vessels in certain cases. **Tier 2 — Implementing Authorities:** 1. **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** — established under the National Waterways Act, 2016; maintains fairway, conserves waterways, enforces navigation rules, fixes tariffs. 2. **Mercantile Marine Department (MMD)** — under the Directorate General of Shipping; conducts surveys, examines and certifies crew, issues certificates of competency. **Tier 3 — Subordinate Rules and State Acts:** Kerala Inland Vessels Rules, Assam IWT Rules, West Bengal IWT Rules, etc. **Tier 4 — Technical Standards:** Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) standards, IWAI technical circulars, classification society rules (when applicable). **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1:** Compute GRT using Moorsom formula. $$\text{GRT} = \frac{V}{100} = \frac{12000}{100} = 120 \text{ RT}$$ **Step 2:** Compute NRT. $$\text{Deductions} = \frac{1800}{100} = 18 \text{ RT}$$ $$\text{NRT} = 120 - 18 = 102 \text{ RT}$$ **Step 3:** Determine waterway class. Since GRT = 120 RT (which corresponds approximately to ~120 DWT for a wooden vessel), the vallam is permitted to operate on **Class B** (vessels up to ~500 DWT) and above, but not on **Class C** (reserved for country boats and small craft). The vessel may also operate on **Class A** waterways subject to fairway depth and width requirements. **Step 4:** Operational compliance: - Certificate of Survey from MMD required (vessel > 100 GT equivalent). - Boat Master Class-II license required. - Engine Driver license required if fitted with engine > specified HP. - Anchor mass: $0.05 \times 120 = 6$ kg (round to standard 7.5 kg). --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** *(a)* Describe the IWAI classification of inland waterways in India. List the dimensions and permitted vessel sizes for at least three classes. (7 marks) *(b)* A houseboat on NW-3 (Kerala backwaters) has a draft of 1.1 m at full load. The lowest bridge en-route has its soffit at 5.5 m above chart datum. High-water swell at the bridge site is 0.12 m. Calculate the available air draft and state whether the vessel can safely pass, justifying with the IWAI safety margin rule. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** The **Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI)** classifies waterways into three primary classes based on **minimum depth, minimum width, and maximum permissible vessel tonnage**. **Class A Waterway:** - Minimum depth: **1.2 m** - Minimum width: **32 m** - Permitted vessel size: up to **1000 DWT** (or equivalent in RT) - Suitable for: mechanized cargo barges, large passenger vessels **Class B Waterway:** - Minimum depth: **1.0 m** - Minimum width: **22 m** - Permitted vessel size: up to **500 DWT** - Suitable for: small cargo boats, tourist houseboats **Class C Waterway:** - Minimum depth: **0.7 m** - Minimum width: **14 m** - Permitted vessel size: up to **100 DWT** (country boats, traditional craft) - Suitable for: traditional wooden vallams, canoes, country cargo boats In addition, the **PIANC international standard** recognizes seven classes (I–VII) used widely in Europe for cross-border harmonization. **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1:** Identify parameters. - $D_{\text{vessel}} = 1.1 \text{ m}$ - $H_{\text{bridge}} = 5.5 \text{ m}$ - $S_{\text{swell}} = 0.12 \text{ m}$ - $M_{\text{safety (inland)}} = 0.3 \text{ m}$ **Step 2:** Compute vessel air draft requirement. $$A_{\text{vessel}} = D + M + S = 1.1 + 0.3 + 0.12 = 1.52 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** Compute available clearance. $$A_{\text{clear}} = H_{\text{bridge}} - A_{\text{vessel}} = 5.5 - 1.52 = 3.98 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4:** Verification. $$A_{\text{clear}} = 3.98 \text{ m} > 0$$ **Conclusion:** The houseboat can **safely pass** under the bridge with a positive vertical clearance of **3.98 m**, which is well above the IWAI minimum safety margin. The vessel operator should still obtain a **No Objection Certificate (NOC)** from IWAI for the specific route and time of passage, as bridge soffit levels are tide/swell dependent in Kerala's interconnected backwater system. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inland Waterway Regulations** - **National Waterways Act, 2016** is the umbrella law; it declared **111** waterways as National Waterways. - **NW-1 (Ganga)** is the longest at **1620 km**; **NW-3 (Kerala)** is **205 km** of backwater canal. - **IWAI** (Inland Waterways Authority of India) is the chief regulator and fairway maintainer. - **MMD** (Mercantile Marine Department) issues Certificates of **Survey, Fitness, and Competency**. - **Moorsom System** of tonnage: **1 RT = 100 ft³ (2.83 m³)** of enclosed volume. - **GRT = V/100**; **NRT = GRT − Deductions**; reported in **RT (Registered Tonnage)**. - **IWAI Waterway Classes** (3 main): Class A (1.2 m / 32 m / 1000 DWT), Class B (1.0 m / 22 m / 500 DWT), Class C (0.7 m / 14 m / 100 DWT). - **PIANC classification** (7 classes) is the international reference. - **Bridge clearance formula:** $A_{\text{clear}} = H_{\text{bridge}} - (D + M_{\text{safety}} + S_{\text{swell}})$, with inland safety margin of **0.3 m**. - **Traditional wooden boats** below **15 m LOA** carrying < **12 pax** are exempt from central MMD survey but must hold a **state-issued fitness certificate** and **insurance**. - **Mandatory safety equipment** for small craft: life jackets (1 per person + 2 spare), life buoys (min 2), fire extinguisher (min 2 kg DCP), anchor ($0.05 \times \text{GRT}$ kg), navigation lights (port-red, starboard-green, masthead-white, stern-white), whistle/horn. - **Vessel < 15 m** LOA: State regulation; **Vessel 15–20 m**: Joint state-MMD; **Vessel > 20 m** or passenger > 12: Full MMD + IWAI. - **TBT (tributyltin)** anti-fouling paints are **banned** under IMO AFS Convention; traditional boats must use **copper-based or biocide-free** coatings. - **Oil pollution prevention**: every motorized vessel must carry an **oil-spill kit** if engine > 25 HP. - **Kerala-specific**: houseboats on NW-3 require **KTDC + IWAI + Tourism Department** joint NOC since 2017. - **Crew licensing** is graded: Boat Master Class-I (> 200 GT), II (50–200 GT), III (< 50 GT); Engine Driver similarly graded. - **Speed limit** in inland canals of India is typically **10–15 km/h** to prevent bank erosion and wave damage to small craft. - **Right of way (inland)**: powered vessel over sailing vessel; vessel going upstream has no extra right (unlike some maritime rules); non-displacement craft give way to displacement craft. - **Tonnage threshold for compulsory registration** under Inland Vessels Act: generally vessels > **15 m LOA** or carrying > **12 passengers** (state variations exist). - **Environmental clearance**: Vessels plying on declared National Waterways may require **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) clearance** if operating within 500 m of the high-tide line. - **Pollution Prevention Certificate (PPC)** is mandatory for all mechanically propelled vessels regardless of size. - **Search and rescue** on inland waterways is coordinated by **IWAI** in partnership with **Indian Coast Guard** and **State Disaster Response Forces**. > [!NOTE] > **One-line takeaway for the exam:** *"Inland waterway regulation in India is a four-tier pyramid — Acts of Parliament at the top, IWAI/MMD rules in the middle, technical standards below, and operational directives at the base — and every traditional boat must clear the five R-S-F-L-E pillars (Registration, Survey, Fitness, License, Environment) to legally operate."*

topics.title: Boat Relocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boat Relocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boat Relocation** is the systematic, engineered process of moving a watercraft from one location to another — whether from a construction site to a water body, between yards, overland between water bodies, or from water to land and vice versa — employing a combination of traditional and modern handling methods, ground preparation, lifting systems, and transport mechanisms while ensuring the **structural integrity**, **stability**, and **safety** of the vessel throughout the operation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Boat Relocation* sits at the intersection of **traditional shipwright knowledge** and **basic applied mechanics**. It is the practical bridge that takes a hull from the *beaching grid* or *slipway* to its working environment. The discipline covers three primary operational modes:

1. **Land-based Relocation** (Construction site → Water's edge / Storage)
2. **Water-based Relocation** (Towing, rafting, or self-propulsion)
3. **Combined / Hybrid Relocation** (e.g., overland transport between two separate water bodies)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Giant Cradle Carry":**
> Imagine you have a sleeping toddler (the boat) that is too heavy and awkward to carry in your arms. You cannot simply "lift" the child without risk. So, you:
> - Place the child gently into a **stroller** (the *cradle/dolly*).
> - Wheel the stroller carefully along a **smooth, prepared path** (the *skidway* or *roller track*).
> - At the destination, you **lower the child** carefully into a **crib** (the *water* — for launching) or onto a **mat** (the *storage cradle*).
>
> Boat Relocation is essentially the same logic — except the "toddler" may weigh anywhere from **500 kg to 200+ tonnes**, and the path may slope, twist, cross roads, or descend a steep beach.

### 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following standard metrics are **commonly tested** in KTU examinations and must be memorized:

- **Coefficient of Rolling Friction ($\mu_r$):** typically **0.02 – 0.05** for steel roller on steel rail, **0.05 – 0.10** for timber roller on soft earth.
- **Coefficient of Sliding Friction ($\mu_s$):** typically **0.30 – 0.50** for wood on wood, **0.20 – 0.35** for boat hull on greased slipway.
- **Standard Gravitational Acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Safe Slope for Sliding Launch:** typically **1:10 to 1:18 (gradient)** for traditional launches.
- **Typical Roller Diameter:** **1/3 to 1/2** of the boat's beam for stable rolling.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically expects students to differentiate between *active* and *passive* relocation methods, identify the **traditional tools** (rollers, skids, capstans, gun tackle, wedges, camels), and explain the **launching sequence** for traditional wooden boats.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Boat Relocation Methods

Relocation methods are broadly classified along **three axes**: *medium of transport*, *power source*, and *traditional vs. modern tooling*.

#### A. By Medium of Transport

| Method | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Land Roll/Slide** | Boat moved over ground using rollers, skids, or dollies | Construction site to water |
| **Water Float/Tow** | Boat moved through water by towing, punting, or self-propulsion | Yard to operational area |
| **Lift-and-Carry** | Boat suspended by crane/lift and carried over short distances | Marine railway, modern slipway |
| **Hybrid (Overland-to-Water)** | Combined land + water (e.g., overland haul between two rivers) | Canal/lock systems, traditional trade routes |

#### B. By Power Source

- **Manual / Human-Powered** — capstans, gun tackles, sheer legs, manual rollers.
- **Animal-Powered** — traditional use of oxen, horses, or elephants on haul-ways.
- **Mechanical** — winches, cranes, hydraulic trailers, prime movers.
- **Natural** — gravitational sliding down a slipway (no external power required at the moment of release).

#### C. By Traditional vs. Modern Approach

| Aspect | Traditional Method | Modern Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifting** | Gun tackle, sheer legs, wedges, camels | Hydraulic jacks, mobile cranes |
| **Ground Transport** | Wooden rollers, greased skids, cradles | Multi-axle hydraulic trailers |
| **Direction Change** | Manual pivoting on log rollers | Turntable dollies |
| **Launching** | Gravity slide on slipway, rollers | Controlled lift-in by crane |

### 2.2 The Three Traditional Sub-Operations

Every boat relocation — whether in a Kerala *vadakku* (northern) yard or a Mediterranean *cantiere* — resolves into **three sequential sub-operations**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Pillars of Boat Relocation (KTU Exam Favourite):**
> 1. **Lifting (Uthānaṁ)** — separating the hull from the building surface.
> 2. **Translating (Sañcāraṁ)** — moving the hull horizontally (and possibly vertically) to a new position.
> 3. **Lowering / Launching (Avaṭaṁ / Jala-praveśaṁ)** — placing the hull onto its support or into the water.

Each pillar demands a different set of tools, force calculations, and safety precautions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Forces & Tools

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, tool, and ratio** you are likely to need in a KTU Part-A or Part-B question on this topic. Memorize this table.

| Concept | Symbol / Tool | Governing Equation or Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight of Boat | $W$ | $W = m \cdot g$ | N (Newton) | $m$ = mass in kg |
| Sliding Friction Force | $F_s$ | $F_s = \mu_s \cdot N$ | N | $N$ = normal reaction |
| Rolling Friction Force | $F_r$ | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$ | N | For roller-supported boat |
| Force to Pull Uphill | $F_{up}$ | $F_{up} = W(\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | N | $\theta$ = slope angle |
| Force to Pull Downhill (braking) | $F_{dn}$ | $F_{dn} = W(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$ | N | Negative means self-sliding |
| Mechanical Advantage (Tackle) | $MA$ | $MA = \dfrac{\text{Load}}{\text{Effort}}$ | dimensionless | Gun tackle = 2, Luff tackle = 3 |
| Roller Spacing | $s$ | $s \approx \dfrac{L_{boat}}{n}$ | m | Typically 1.5–3 m apart |
| Minimum Slipway Gradient | $G$ | $G \geq \dfrac{\mu_s}{1}$ | ratio | e.g., for $\mu_s = 0.30$, $G \geq 1{:}3.3$ |
| Safe Load on Manila Rope | $T$ | $T \approx 5 \cdot d^2$ (d in inches → lb) | lb | Traditional thumb-rule |
| Cradle Support Points | $n$ | $n \geq 3$ | count | 2-side + keel support |

### 2.4 Traditional Tools of the Relocation Trade

A KTU examiner loves to test identification of traditional tools. Memorize the following with their **functions**:

- **Roller (Uruḷu / Cylindrus)** — Hardwood or steel cylinders placed under the hull to convert sliding friction to rolling friction.
- **Skid (Kallu / Slip Timber)** — Long greased timbers laid on the ground; the boat slides over them.
- **Cradle (Taṭṭu)** — A timber frame that matches the hull's curvature, supporting the boat during transport.
- **Dolly (Cakkarattāram)** — Small wheeled platform used to support individual points.
- **Wedge (Kīl)** — Used for fine height adjustment and incremental lifting.
- **Camel (Oṭṭa)** — A floating or rolling tank/drum used to lift a boat that is already in the water.
- **Capstan (Tuli)** — Vertical rotating drum used to multiply human pulling force via a rope/cable.
- **Gun Tackle (Tunṭuppāy)** — Simple 2:1 block-and-tackle for lifting.
- **Sheer Legs (Tiṅṅukūṭa)** — A tripod/A-frame structure for vertical lifting.
- **Slipway (Tōṟṟi)** — Inclined prepared surface from land to water for launching.
- **Marine Railway (Kaḍalmeppāṭṭu)** — A track-based system for hauling boats out of the water.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond traditional boat building, these relocation principles are directly applicable in:

- **Civil Engineering** — moving heavy prefabricated bridge sections, transformers, and turbine housings.
- **Aerospace** — relocating aircraft fuselages within assembly halls using air-casters (a modern cousin of the roller).
- **Heavy Lift Logistics** — using **Self-Propelled Modular Transporters (SPMTs)** for over-dimensional cargo.
- **Disaster Response** — salvaging and relocating damaged vessels and heritage watercraft.

The common thread is the **reduction of friction** and the **distribution of load** to avoid structural failure — exactly the same logic a traditional shipwright used with rollers and greased skids centuries ago.

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section walks you through the **complete procedure** for each of the three sub-operations, with **every single step** written out explicitly. KTU examiners award marks for the *order* of steps, the *justification* of each step, and the *numerical evaluation* where applicable.

### 3.1 Sub-Operation 1: LIFTING (Uthānaṁ)

#### 3.1.1 Traditional Lifting Procedure — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Survey and Site Assessment**
- Inspect the boat's **current support points** (keel blocks, side shores, bilge shores).
- Identify the **center of gravity (CG)** — typically about **1/3 of the waterline length from the bow** for a traditional fishing boat, or amidships for a symmetric vessel.
- Mark the **lifting points** symmetrically about the CG to prevent tilting.

**Step 2 — Prepare the Lifting Rig**
- For a boat under **2 tonnes**: a **gun tackle** (2:1 advantage) on a **sheer-legs tripod** is sufficient.
- For a boat between **2–10 tonnes**: a **luff tackle** (3:1) on a **gyn** (two-legged sheer legs).
- For boats over **10 tonnes**: a **differential pulley** or **chain block** is required.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Required Effort**

The effort $E$ required at the free end of a tackle is given by:

$$E = \frac{W}{MA \cdot \eta}$$

where:
- $W$ = total weight of the boat (N)
- $MA$ = mechanical advantage of the tackle (dimensionless)
- $\eta$ = efficiency of the tackle (typically **0.80 – 0.90** for a well-maintained block)

**Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):**

> A traditional wooden fishing boat of mass **1500 kg** is to be lifted using a **gun tackle** (MA = 2) with an efficiency of **85%**. Calculate the effort required at the pulling end.

**Solution:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}$$

$$E = \frac{W}{MA \cdot \eta} = \frac{14715}{2 \times 0.85}$$

$$E = \frac{14715}{1.70} = 8655.88 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 8.66 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Wedges Incrementally**
- Drive **paired wedges** at the bow and stern simultaneously, alternating sides, to lift the boat evenly.
- The wedge principle: a thin wedge with taper angle $\alpha$ gives a mechanical advantage of approximately $1/\tan(\alpha)$. A typical shipwright's wedge with $\alpha \approx 7°$ gives $MA \approx 8$.

**Step 5 — Insert Cradle or Slings**
- Once the boat is raised to the required height, slide the **cradle** (or **rope slings**) underneath.
- Lower the boat gently onto the cradle.

---

### 3.2 Sub-Operation 2: TRANSLATING (Sañcāraṁ)

#### 3.2.1 Traditional Overland Translation — Roller Method

**Step 1 — Prepare the Track**
- Lay a **level, firm path** from the building site to the water's edge.
- For soft ground, lay **two parallel rails** (timber or steel) to keep the rollers aligned.

**Step 2 — Place the Rollers**
- Rollers are typically made of **straight-grained hardwood** (teak, sal, or deodar) with a diameter of **15–30 cm** and a length slightly exceeding the boat's beam.
- The recommended number of rollers is:

$$n_{rollers} = \frac{L_{boat}}{d_{roller}}$$

where $L_{boat}$ is the boat length and $d_{roller}$ is the roller spacing (typically 1.5–3 m).

**Step 3 — Roll the Boat Onto the Rollers**
- Use **crowbars or levers** at the bow and stern to nudge the boat forward.
- As the rear rollers emerge from under the stern, **reposition them to the front** of the boat (continuous cycling).

**Step 4 — Calculate the Pulling Force**

For a boat being pulled on rollers over a level surface:

$$F_{pull} = \mu_r \cdot W$$

For a boat being pulled on rollers **up an incline of angle $\theta$**:

$$F_{pull} = W(\mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

**Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):**

> A 2000 kg boat is being pulled on timber rollers ($\mu_r = 0.08$) up a slipway inclined at **5°** to the horizontal. Calculate the pulling force required.

**Solution:**

$$W = 2000 \times 9.81 = 19620 \text{ N}$$

$$\sin 5° = 0.0872, \quad \cos 5° = 0.9962$$

$$F_{pull} = 19620 \times (0.08 \times 0.9962 + 0.0872)$$

$$F_{pull} = 19620 \times (0.0797 + 0.0872)$$

$$F_{pull} = 19620 \times 0.1669$$

$$\boxed{F_{pull} \approx 3274.6 \text{ N} \approx 3.27 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Steer and Control**
- Use **guide ropes (spring lines)** at the bow and stern, manned by 2–4 workers each, to keep the boat aligned on the track and prevent sideways drift.
- The bow line and stern line should be adjusted continuously.

---

### 3.3 Sub-Operation 3: LOWERING / LAUNCHING (Avaṭaṁ)

#### 3.3.1 Traditional Slipway Launching — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Verify the Slipway Gradient**

The slipway gradient must be **steeper than the angle of static friction** but **not so steep** as to cause runaway acceleration. The critical angle is:

$$\theta_{critical} = \arctan(\mu_s)$$

For a greased timber slipway with $\mu_s = 0.25$:

$$\theta_{critical} = \arctan(0.25) = 14.04° \quad (\text{gradient} \approx 1{:}4)$$

A safe launch gradient is **1:10 to 1:18** (about 3.2° to 5.7°), ensuring controlled descent.

**Step 2 — Apply Launch Grease**

Traditional launch grease is a mixture of:
- **Tallow** (animal fat) — 60%
- **Soap** — 20%
- **Soft soap or wax** — 10%
- **Graphite or black lead** — 10%

Applied as a **thick layer (5–10 mm)** on the slipway, this reduces $\mu_s$ to about **0.08 – 0.15**.

**Step 3 — Position the Boat**

- The boat is placed on the **sliding ways** (two parallel greased timbers) with the bow pointing **toward the water**.
- The boat is held in place by **trigger devices** (dog shores, triggers, or slip-posts) that are released at the moment of launch.

**Step 4 — Trigger Release and Observe**

At the signal:
- The **dog shores** are knocked away.
- Gravity overcomes the static friction.
- The boat accelerates down the slipway.
- The **stern** enters the water first if the slipway extends below the waterline; this naturally **floats the stern** off the sliding ways.

**Step 5 — Calculate Launch Velocity at Water Entry**

Using energy conservation (ignoring friction for a worst-case estimate):

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = m g h$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

For a slipway with vertical drop $h = 4 \text{ m}$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4} = \sqrt{78.48}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 8.86 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A KTU examiner will deduct marks if you forget to add the friction loss. The *real* velocity is:

$$v_{real} = \sqrt{2 g h (1 - \mu_s \cot\alpha)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the slipway angle. Always include this correction in a 14-mark question.

---

### 3.4 Water-Based Relocation — Towing

When the boat is afloat, relocation is achieved by **towing**.

**Towing Force Formula:**

$$F_{tow} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot v^2 + R_{friction} + R_{wave}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of water ($1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for fresh, $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for sea)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (0.6 – 1.2 for displacement hulls)
- $A$ = frontal submerged area
- $v$ = tow speed
- $R_{friction}$ and $R_{wave}$ are hull and wave-making resistance terms

For a traditional fishing boat towed at **3 knots (~1.54 m/s)** by a larger vessel, the towing force on the bridle is typically **2–5% of the boat's weight**.

---

### 3.5 Master Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **KTU high-yield tool**: it consolidates *when to use what method*.

| Boat Size | Distance | Terrain | Recommended Method | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 1 t | < 50 m | Level ground | Manual rollers + cradle | Hardwood rollers |
| 1–5 t | 50–200 m | Level / mild slope | Rollers + capstan or winch | Capstan + tackle |
| 5–20 t | 100–500 m | Any | Marine railway / slipway | Slipway + sliding ways |
| 20–100 t | Any | Any | SPMT or marine railway | SPMT / railway cradle |
| Any (afloat) | Any | Open water | Towing | Tow bridle + towing vessel |
