## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **Boat Relocation** and list the **three traditional sub-operations** involved in relocating a wooden boat from the construction site to the water.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Boat Relocation** is the systematic process of moving a boat from one location to another — typically from a construction site to a water body — using a combination of traditional and modern methods, while preserving the vessel's structural integrity and ensuring safety.
>
> The three traditional sub-operations are:
> 1. **Lifting (Uthānaṁ)** — separating the hull from the building blocks using tackles, wedges, or jacks.
> 2. **Translating (Sañcāraṁ)** — moving the hull horizontally over land using rollers, skids, cradles, or a marine railway.
> 3. **Lowering / Launching (Avaṭaṁ / Jala-praveśaṁ)** — placing the boat onto the water via a slipway, crane lift, or direct float-off.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for naming the three sub-operations, 0.5 mark for brief description.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
Explain the **difference between sliding friction and rolling friction** in the context of boat relocation on rollers. Why are rollers preferred over direct sliding?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> In boat relocation:
> - **Sliding friction** acts when the hull moves directly over a surface. The coefficient of sliding friction $\mu_s$ for wood on greased timber is **0.20–0.35**, requiring a large pulling force.
> - **Rolling friction** acts when the boat rests on cylindrical rollers. The coefficient of rolling friction $\mu_r$ for timber rollers is only **0.05–0.10**, which is roughly **3 to 5 times lower** than sliding friction.
>
> **Rollers are preferred** because they:
> 1. Drastically reduce the pulling force required ($F_{pull} = \mu_r W$ vs. $F_{pull} = \mu_s W$).
> 2. Allow the boat to be moved over long distances with limited manpower.
> 3. Distribute the hull's load more evenly, protecting the planking.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark for the coefficient values, 1 mark for the three reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **traditional roller method** of translating a wooden boat overland from a construction site to the water's edge. Include a labeled sketch of the arrangement and explain the **role of the capstan** in multiplying human effort. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional fishing boat of mass **2500 kg** is to be pulled on **hardwood rollers** ($\mu_r = 0.07$) up a slipway inclined at **4°** to the horizontal. Calculate the **minimum pulling force** required. If a **capstan** with a mechanical advantage of **5** and efficiency **80%** is used, what is the **effort** a worker must apply at the capstan bar? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Roller Method (7 Marks):**

1. **Track Preparation (1.5 marks):** Two parallel **guide rails** of timber or steel are laid on a level, firm path from the building site to the water. The rails keep the rollers aligned and prevent the boat from drifting sideways.

2. **Roller Placement (1.5 marks):** Hardwood rollers (typically teak or sal) of **diameter 15–30 cm** and length exceeding the boat's beam are placed perpendicular to the direction of motion, spaced **1.5–3 m apart** along the boat's length.

3. **Boat Transfer onto Rollers (1 mark):** The boat, resting on its cradle, is gently jacked up, and the rollers are slid underneath. The cradle may remain in place to support the hull.

4. **Pulling Operation (1.5 marks):** Workers pull on ropes attached to the bow and stern, OR a capstan-driven cable is used for heavier boats. As the boat advances, the **rear rollers emerge** from under the stern and are manually carried to the front (a process called "recycling rollers").

5. **Role of the Capstan (1.5 marks):** The capstan is a vertical rotating drum. A rope is wound around the drum 2–5 times. As workers push on horizontal capstan bars, the drum rotates, winding the rope and pulling the boat. The mechanical advantage of the capstan depends on the **diameter ratio** of the bar's circle of push to the drum radius. With a MA of 5, a single worker can generate a pulling force of about **1.5–2 kN** comfortably.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 2500 \text{ kg}$
- Coefficient of rolling friction $\mu_r = 0.07$
- Slope angle $\theta = 4°$
- Capstan MA = 5, efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Weight of the boat:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 2500 \times 9.81 = 24525 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Pulling force on rollers up the slope:**

$$F_{pull} = W(\mu_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

$$\cos 4° = 0.9976, \quad \sin 4° = 0.0698$$

$$F_{pull} = 24525 \times (0.07 \times 0.9976 + 0.0698)$$

$$F_{pull} = 24525 \times (0.0698 + 0.0698)$$

$$F_{pull} = 24525 \times 0.1396$$

$$\boxed{F_{pull} = 3423.7 \text{ N} \approx 3.42 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Effort at the capstan bar:**

$$E = \frac{F_{pull}}{MA \cdot \eta} = \frac{3423.7}{5 \times 0.80} = \frac{3423.7}{4.0}$$

$$\boxed{E = 855.9 \text{ N} \approx 0.86 \text{ kN}}$$

**Conclusion:** A single worker applying a push of about **86 kg-force** at the capstan bar is sufficient to pull a **2.5-tonne boat** up a 4° slope on rollers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Retest Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **traditional slipway launching method** for a wooden boat. Describe the **composition of traditional launch grease**, the function of **dog shores**, and the **sequence of events** from trigger release to free-floating. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional boat slides down a slipway of length **25 m** inclined at **6°** to the horizontal. The coefficient of sliding friction with the greased slipway is **0.12**. If the boat starts from rest, calculate the **velocity of the boat as it enters the water** using energy methods. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Slipway Launching (7 Marks):**

1. **Slipway Construction (1 mark):** A slipway is an inclined prepared surface extending from the shore into the water. It carries two **sliding ways** (parallel greased timbers) that match the boat's cradle.

2. **Launch Grease Composition (1.5 marks):** Traditional launch grease is composed of **tallow (60%)**, **soap (20%)**, **soft wax (10%)**, and **graphite (10%)**. This mixture reduces the coefficient of friction from ~0.35 (dry wood) to ~0.10–0.15 (greased).

3. **Dog Shores — Function (1.5 marks):** **Dog shores** are heavy timber braces that hold the boat stationary on the slipway until the moment of launch. They are designed to be **knocked away simultaneously** on signal, releasing the boat instantly.

4. **Launch Sequence (3 marks):**
   - **Signal given** by the launching commander.
   - **Dog shores released** (struck with mallets or cut with axes).
   - **Static friction overcome** by gravity; boat begins to slide.
   - **Acceleration phase** down the slipway (typically 5–15 seconds for a medium boat).
   - **Stern contacts water first** (the slipway extends below the waterline).
   - **Buoyancy lifts the stern** off the sliding ways, rotating the boat about the bow.
   - **Boat floats free** and is taken under tow by a waiting vessel.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Length of slipway $L = 25 \text{ m}$
- Slope angle $\theta = 6°$
- Coefficient of sliding friction $\mu_s = 0.12$
- Initial velocity $u = 0$

**Step 1 — Vertical drop:**

$$h = L \sin\theta = 25 \times \sin 6° = 25 \times 0.1045 = 2.613 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance with friction:**

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = m g h - \mu_s m g L \cos\theta$$

$$v^2 = 2 g (h - \mu_s L \cos\theta)$$

$$\cos 6° = 0.9945$$

$$v^2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times (2.613 - 0.12 \times 25 \times 0.9945)$$

$$v^2 = 19.62 \times (2.613 - 2.9835)$$

$$v^2 = 19.62 \times (-0.3705)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative result** — this means the boat **will not slide** on its own! The friction is too high for the given slope. The boat must be **pushed** or the slipway must be **steeper** or **re-greased**.

**Step 3 — Corrected: Re-calculate with the correct formula:**

The correct energy equation is:

$$v^2 = 2 g L (\sin\theta - \mu_s \cos\theta)$$

$$v^2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times 25 \times (0.1045 - 0.12 \times 0.9945)$$

$$v^2 = 490.5 \times (0.1045 - 0.1193)$$

$$v^2 = 490.5 \times (-0.0148)$$

$$\boxed{v^2 < 0 \implies \text{Boat will NOT slide}}$$

**Conclusion:** With $\mu_s = 0.12$ and $\theta = 6°$, the slipway is **too gentle** to overcome static friction. The minimum slope angle required is:

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan(\mu_s) = \arctan(0.12) = 6.84°$$

The slipway must be steepened to at least **6.84°** (gradient ≈ 1:8.3) for the boat to launch under gravity alone. Otherwise, external pushing is required.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Boat Relocation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency ($\eta$) of the tackle.** A gun tackle alone gives MA = 2, but in practice, friction in the sheaves reduces this. KTU examiners expect $\eta = 0.80–0.90$ to be included.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\mu_r$ (rolling) with $\mu_s$ (sliding).** Using 0.30 instead of 0.07 in a roller-based problem will give a 4× wrong answer.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the slope component.** A common error is to compute $F = \mu W$ without adding the $W \sin\theta$ uphill component. Both terms **must** appear.
>
> 4. **Wrong slipway energy balance sign.** When the friction loss exceeds the gravitational gain, the velocity is imaginary — students often write a fictitious number instead of **concluding that the boat will not slide**, which is the correct engineering answer.
>
> 5. **Omitting the unit.** KTU strictly deducts **0.5 mark** for missing units in numerical answers.
>
> 6. **Confusing a "cradle" with a "skid."** A cradle **supports** the boat (curved to hull shape); a skid is a **track** the cradle or boat slides on.
>
> 7. **Not identifying the CG before lifting.** Lifting off-center causes the boat to tilt, stressing the hull. The CG must be **explicitly mentioned** in a 7-mark lifting question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Boat Relocation (Module 4):**
>
> - **Definition:** Boat relocation = lift + translate + lower/launch.
> - **Three sub-operations:** Lifting → Translating → Launching.
> - **Key friction coefficients:** $\mu_s$ (sliding) ≈ 0.20–0.50; $\mu_r$ (rolling) ≈ 0.02–0.10. Always use $\mu_r$ for rollers.
> - **Slope formulas:** Uphill pull = $W(\mu \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$; Downhill self-slide only if $\tan\theta > \mu_s$.
> - **Mechanical advantage:** Gun tackle = 2; Luff tackle = 3; Capstan = 2–8 depending on geometry. Always divide by efficiency $\eta \approx 0.85$.
> - **Traditional tools (must know):** Rollers, Skids, Cradle, Dolly, Wedge, Camel, Capstan, Gun tackle, Sheer legs, Slipway, Marine railway, Dog shores.
> - **Launch grease composition:** Tallow 60% + Soap 20% + Wax 10% + Graphite 10%.
> - **Slipway gradient:** Safe range 1:10 to 1:18; Minimum for gravity launch: $\theta \geq \arctan(\mu_s)$.
> - **Launching sequence:** Signal → Release dog shores → Gravity slide → Stern enters water → Buoyancy lifts stern → Free float → Tow to berth.
> - **Towing force:** Approximately **2–5% of boat weight** at low speeds (3 knots).
> - **Critical safety checks:** CG identification before lifting; even wedging; continuous guide-rope tension; clearance of slipway path before trigger release.
> - **Modern parallel:** SPMT (Self-Propelled Modular Transporter) = motorized descendant of the traditional roller-and-cradle system.
> - **KTU's favourite test points:** (i) roller vs. sliding friction comparison, (ii) pulling force with slope, (iii) tackle MA with efficiency, (iv) launching sequence, (v) tools identification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Launching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Launching** is the controlled, engineered process of transferring a newly constructed or repaired vessel from its construction site (land-based slipway, building cradle, or beach) into the water (sea, river, or backwater) for the first time, achieving positive buoyancy and free-floating stability. In the context of **Traditional Boat Building Techniques (PENST864)**, launching represents the critical transition phase where the vessel's structural integrity, hydrostatic balance, and watertight integrity are physically validated against the actual hydrodynamics of the operating water body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Launching is **NOT** merely putting a boat into the water. It is a meticulously planned engineering event that combines **gravitational mechanics**, **hydrostatic principles**, **structural load management**, and in the Kerala context, deeply rooted **cultural rituals** that predate modern engineering documentation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have spent **six months** crafting a large wooden sculpture in your bedroom. The sculpture weighs nearly **200 kg** and must be moved downstairs through a narrow staircase without breaking it. You would not simply push it down the stairs — you would plan a controlled descent path, lubricate the path, position the sculpture on a wheeled cart, calculate the angle of descent, and ensure someone guides it from below. **Boat launching is exactly this process, but on an industrial scale, with a vessel weighing anywhere from 500 kg (small vallam) to over 50,000 kg (large uru or odam).**

The "stairs" are replaced by a **slipway** (inclined ramp), the "cart" is replaced by a **cradle** (wooden support structure), and the "lubricant" is typically **tallow, grease, or launching oil** applied to the slipway surface.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Launching

The following physical constants and standard metrics govern any launching event:

- **Launching Weight (W):** Total mass of vessel + cradle + temporary supports, typically ranging from **1,000 kg** (small vallam) to **80,000 kg** (large odam).
- **Slipway Gradient (θ):** Inclination angle of the launching ramp, traditionally measured in **degrees** or as a **slope ratio (1:N)**.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** Static friction between cradle runners and slipway surface, reduced by lubrication to values between **0.05 and 0.15**.
- **Freeboard at Launch:** Vertical distance between waterline and the lowest deck edge at the moment of flotation, ideally not less than **300 mm** for traditional wooden vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering theory questions on launching, students frequently forget to mention the **transition moment** — the exact instant when the vessel's weight is fully supported by water buoyancy instead of the slipway cradle. This transition point is the most critical engineering moment of the entire process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Launching Process

The launching of a traditional Kerala boat can be broken down into **six sequential engineering phases**:

### Phase 1 — Pre-Launch Inspection
- **Hull Integrity Check:** All planks, seams, and fastenings (treenails, copper rivets, or coir stitching) are inspected.
- **Caulking Verification:** Cotton, coir, or pitch-based caulking is tested for water-tightness.
- **Drain Plug Test:** Temporary drain plugs at the lowest hull point are sealed.
- **Buoyancy Prediction:** Weight is estimated and compared with predicted displacement.

### Phase 2 — Cradle & Slipway Preparation
- The **cradle** (locally called *pattika* or *uzhi*) is a timber frame that supports the hull during construction and slides during launch.
- The **slipway** (*thazhukal*) is an inclined surface, typically with greased wooden runners called **ways** (*thara*).
- Lubricants traditionally used: **tallow (animal fat), coconut oil, or neem oil.**

### Phase 3 — Trigger Mechanism Setup
- **Damsel blocks** or **trigger wedges** are positioned at the stern to hold the vessel stationary.
- A **shuttering plate** or **packing piece** prevents premature sliding.
- In Kerala tradition, a consecrated **coconut** (*pooja thengu*) is placed under the trigger.

### Phase 4 — The Launch Event
- On signal, the trigger is released.
- Gravitational force component along the slipway: **F = W × sin(θ)**
- Frictional resistance: **F_f = μ × W × cos(θ)**
- Net accelerating force: **F_net = W × (sin θ − μ cos θ)**

### Phase 5 — Flotation Transition
- The vessel's stern enters the water first (in **stern-first launching**).
- Buoyancy progressively supports the hull.
- The **metacenter (M)** rises above the **center of gravity (G)**, providing stability.

### Phase 6 — Post-Launch Retrieval
- The cradle remains on the slipway.
- The vessel is towed to a mooring buoy or shallow dock.
- Initial **shakedown cruise** is performed to check for leaks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Component of weight along slipway | $F_\parallel$ | $W \sin\theta$ | N or kgf | Driving force |
| Normal reaction on slipway | $N$ | $W \cos\theta$ | N or kgf | Perpendicular load |
| Frictional resistance | $F_f$ | $\mu W \cos\theta$ | N or kgf | Opposes motion |
| Net launching force | $F_{net}$ | $W(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)$ | N or kgf | Must be positive |
| Minimum slipway angle | $\theta_{min}$ | $\tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | degrees | Critical threshold |
| Displacement at flotation | $\Delta$ | $\rho \cdot V_{submerged} \cdot g$ | N | Equals W at flotation |
| Metacentric height (GM) | $\overline{GM}$ | $I/V - \overline{BG}$ | m | Must be positive |
| Block coefficient (traditional hull) | $C_b$ | $\nabla / (L \times B \times T)$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.55 to 0.75 |
| Launching velocity | $v$ | $\sqrt{2 \cdot a \cdot s}$ | m/s | At water entry |
| Cradle sliding distance | $s$ | $L_{cradle} / \cos\theta$ | m | Full slipway travel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** For a launch to be self-starting (i.e., begin moving under gravity alone), the condition $\tan\theta > \mu$ **must** be satisfied. If the slipway is too shallow or the friction too high, external force (winches, capstans) must be applied.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The principles studied in traditional boat launching directly translate to modern marine engineering applications:

- **Shipbuilding Yards (Cochin Shipyard, HSL):** Modern vessels use the same **gravitational slipway** principle, scaled up to **150,000+ DWT** vessels.
- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft are moved from assembly buildings to launch pads on **mobile launchers** using the same cradle-and-way concept.
- **Civil Engineering:** Heavy structural members (bridges, dams) are launched using **incremental launching methods** with identical force-balance equations.
- **Disaster Response:** Temporary bridges in flood zones use launching principles derived from boat-building.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Self-Starting Condition for Traditional Slipway Launch

The most fundamental analytical problem in launching is determining whether a vessel will slide on its own once released, or if external force is required. Let us derive the **self-starting condition** rigorously.

### Step 1 — Identify All Forces Acting on the Vessel-Cradle System

Consider a vessel of total weight **W** (including cradle and fittings) resting on a slipway inclined at angle **θ** to the horizontal. Three forces act on the system:

1. **Weight W** acting vertically downward through the center of gravity.
2. **Normal reaction N** acting perpendicular to the slipway surface.
3. **Frictional force F_f** acting parallel to the slipway surface, opposing motion.

### Step 2 — Resolve the Weight into Components

Resolve W into two components:

- One **parallel** to the slipway (along the direction of intended motion).
- One **perpendicular** to the slipway (pressing into the surface).

Using trigonometric resolution:

$$
F_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta
$$

$$
F_{\perp} = W \cos\theta
$$

The perpendicular component equals the normal reaction: **N = W cos θ**

### Step 3 — Express the Frictional Force

The maximum static friction force is given by Coulomb's law of friction:

$$
F_f = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W \cos\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Condition for Self-Starting

For the vessel to begin sliding on its own, the gravitational component along the slipway must **exceed** the maximum static friction:

$$
F_{\parallel} > F_f
$$

Substituting:

$$
W \sin\theta > \mu W \cos\theta
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify and Solve

Dividing both sides by W cos θ (assuming W > 0 and cos θ > 0):

$$
\tan\theta > \mu
$$

Therefore, the **minimum slipway angle** for self-starting is:

$$
\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(\mu)
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example for Traditional Kerala Slipway

A typical Kerala teak slipway has a coefficient of friction **μ = 0.08** after tallow lubrication.

$$
\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.08) = 4.57^{\circ}
$$

This means a **slope of 1:12.5** (1 unit rise per 12.5 units horizontal run) is sufficient. Most traditional Kerala slipways are built at slopes between **1:8 and 1:15**, comfortably satisfying the self-starting condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Exams:** The self-starting condition is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in PENST864. Examiners specifically look for: (1) correct free-body diagram identification, (2) proper trigonometric resolution, (3) clear final inequality, and (4) a **physical interpretation** of the result.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Launching Methods

| Launching Method | Vessel Size Range | Required Infrastructure | Traditional Use in Kerala | Key Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **End Launching** (stern-first or bow-first) | 1 to 30 tonnes | Simple slipway, greased ways | Most common for vallam, toni, chundan vallom | Bow slamming, longitudinal stress |
| **Side Launching** (broadside roll) | 1 to 10 tonnes | Flat beach, log rollers | Used for small fishing boats on Alleppey beach | Capsizing if not balanced |
| **Davits / Crane Lift** | 0.5 to 5 tonnes | Wooden or steel crane | Used for repairs of small vessels | Sling damage to hull |
| **Floating Dock (Syncrolift)** | 10 to 100 tonnes | Mechanical lifting platform | Modern shipyards only | High capital cost |
| **Dry Dock Flooding** | 50 to 10,000 tonnes | Permanent dock structure | Not traditional | Infrastructure dependence |
| **Beach Haul (Manual)** | 0.2 to 2 tonnes | None (manual labour) | Used by traditional fishermen | Labour-intensive, seasonal |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Net Launching Force Calculation

**Problem:** A traditional odam (large cargo boat) of total weight **W = 20,000 kgf** is to be launched on a slipway inclined at **θ = 8°**. The coefficient of friction after tallow application is **μ = 0.06**. Determine the net launching force.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the gravitational component along the slipway.

$$
F_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta = 20000 \times \sin(8^{\circ}) = 20000 \times 0.1392 = 2784 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the frictional resistance.

$$
F_f = \mu W \cos\theta = 0.06 \times 20000 \times \cos(8^{\circ}) = 0.06 \times 20000 \times 0.9903 = 1188.4 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the net launching force.

$$
F_{net} = F_{\parallel} - F_f = 2784 - 1188.4 = 1595.6 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify the self-starting condition.

$$
\tan\theta = \tan(8^{\circ}) = 0.1405
$$

$$
\mu = 0.06
$$

Since **0.1405 > 0.06**, the condition is satisfied and the vessel will self-start. The net force of **1595.6 kgf** is substantial and will impart a notable velocity by the time the vessel reaches the water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A net force of ~1600 kgf on a 20-tonne vessel produces an acceleration of approximately **0.78 m/s²**. Over a typical 30-metre slipway, this yields a launching velocity of **~6.8 m/s** (about 12 knots) — fast enough that traditional launching crews must be positioned safely upslope, never downslope.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram: Traditional Boat Launching Sequence

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete engineering and ceremonial workflow for a traditional Kerala boat launching, from pre-launch preparation through to post-launch shakedown.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["A: HULL CONSTRUCTION COMPLETE"] --> nodeB["B: PRE-LAUNCH INSPECTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["B1: Check planks and caulking"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["B2: Seal drain plug"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["B3: Verify fastenings"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC["C: CRADLE ALIGNMENT CHECK"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC
    nodeB3 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD["D: SLIPWAY LUBRICATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["D1: Apply tallow or coconut oil"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["D2: Position wooden ways thara"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE["E: TRIGGER MECHANISM SETUP"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeE1["E1: Install damsel blocks at stern"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["E2: Position trigger wedge"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["E3: Place consecrated coconut"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF["F: CEREMONIAL RITUALS"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeF1["F1: Pooja by temple priest"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["F2: Breaking of coconut"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["F3: Astrologer signals muhurtham"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG["G: TRIGGER RELEASE"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["H: VESSEL SLIDES DOWN SLIPWAY"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["I: STERN ENTERS WATER"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["J: BUOYANCY TRANSITION"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["K: VESSACH ACHIEVES FLOTATION"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["L: TOW TO MOORING"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["M: SHAKEDOWN CRUISE"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["N: POST-LAUNCH INSPECTION"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["O: DELIVERY TO OWNER"]
```

## 4.2 Force Balance Architecture: Slipway Launching Mechanics

The following block diagram illustrates the **force interaction architecture** acting on a vessel during the critical moment of slipway release.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW["W: TOTAL WEIGHT OF VESSEL PLUS CRADLE"] -->|"Resolves into"| nodeF1["F parallel: W sin theta DRIVING FORCE"]
    nodeW -->|"Resolves into"| nodeF2["F perpendicular: W cos theta NORMAL LOAD"]
    nodeF2 -->|"Multiplied by mu"| nodeFf["Ff: Frictional Resistance"]
    nodeF1 -->|"Minus Ff"| nodeFnet["Fnet: Net Launching Force"]
    nodeFf -->|"Opposes"| nodeFnet
    nodeFnet -->|"Determines"| nodeAcc["Acceleration of vessel"]
    nodeFnet -->|"Determines"| nodeVel["Final velocity at water entry"]
    nodeAcc --> nodeVel
    nodeVel -->|"At water entry"| nodeBuoy["Buoyancy force takes over"]
    nodeBuoy --> nodeFloat["Vessel achieves positive flotation"]
```

## 4.3 Launching Method Selection Architecture

The following diagram maps the **decision logic** used by traditional boat builders in Kerala to select an appropriate launching method based on vessel size, infrastructure, and site conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["SELECT LAUNCHING METHOD"] --> nodeQ1{"Vessel weight less than 2 tonnes?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"YES"| nodeQ2{"Beach access available?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"NO"| nodeQ3{"Slipway available?"}
    nodeQ2 -->|"YES"| nodeMethodA["METHOD: SIDE LAUNCHING WITH LOG ROLLERS"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"NO"| nodeMethodB["METHOD: DAVIT OR CRANE LIFT"]
    nodeQ3 -->|"YES"| nodeQ4{"Slipway angle exceeds 5 degrees?"}
    nodeQ3 -->|"NO"| nodeMethodC["METHOD: FLOATING DRY DOCK REQUIRED"]
    nodeQ4 -->|"YES"| nodeMethodD["METHOD: STERN FIRST END LAUNCHING"]
    nodeQ4 -->|"NO"| nodeMethodE["METHOD: WINCH ASSISTED LAUNCHING"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In Mermaid syntax, every arrow represents a **causal or sequential relationship**. The `{}` diamond shapes represent **decision points** where the process branches based on a yes/no condition. The `[]` rectangles represent **process states or actions**. This Block-Level Functional Architecture approach is used here because launching is a **physical procedural workflow** rather than a circuit or network topology, making pure physical drawings less instructive than logical flow representations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "launching" in the context of traditional boat building. List any three pre-launching checks.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Launching is the controlled process of transferring a newly completed or repaired boat from its construction site on land into the water, using methods such as slipway sliding, side rolling, or crane lifting, while ensuring the vessel achieves safe flotation.

**Three Pre-Launching Checks:**
1. **Hull Integrity Inspection:** All plank joints, seams, and caulking must be checked for water-tightness. In traditional vessels using coir stitching, the tightness of each stitch is verified by hand pressure.
2. **Drain Plug and Bilge Check:** All temporary openings at the lowest point of the hull are sealed with wooden plugs coated in pitch or tar. The bilge is inspected for any debris or loose tools left inside.
3. **Fastening and Structural Check:** All treenails, copper rivets, or iron bolts are visually inspected for tightness. Loose fastenings are hammered or re-driven before launch.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a "cradle" in boat building? Explain its function during launching.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A cradle (locally called *pattika* or *uzhi* in Kerala) is a robust timber framework constructed beneath the hull of a boat during its building phase. It is shaped to match the hull's curvature and provides a stable platform on which the vessel rests both during construction and launching.

**Functions During Launching:**
1. **Load Distribution:** The cradle spreads the concentrated weight of the hull over a larger slipway area, preventing localised stress on any single hull plank.
2. **Sliding Interface:** The cradle's base incorporates wooden runners called "ways" (*thara*) that slide along greased slipway tracks, providing a low-friction launching surface.
3. **Hull Protection:** It prevents the hull bottom from direct contact with the ground or slipway, protecting the keel and planking from abrasion damage during the sliding motion.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** In the End Semester Examination, Module 4 carries a compulsory question of **14 marks** with internal choice between Part (a) and Part (b) of 7 marks each, OR between two full questions of 14 marks. Below, TWO complete alternative questions are provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different types of launching methods used in traditional boat building in Kerala. Mention the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the self-starting condition for a traditional slipway launching. A vessel weighing **15,000 kgf** is to be launched on a slipway inclined at **6 degrees**. The coefficient of friction is **0.05**. Determine whether the vessel will self-start. If yes, calculate the net launching force. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Traditional Launching Methods in Kerala

The following are the principal launching methods used in traditional Kerala boat building:

**1. End Launching (Stern-First):**
- The vessel slides stern-first down a greased slipway.
- The stern enters the water first, and the bow follows as the vessel pivots around its center of buoyancy.
- **Advantages:** Suitable for vessels up to 30 tonnes; requires minimal equipment; preserves the delicate bow ornamentation common in chundan vallom.
- **Limitations:** Risk of stern slamming damage; requires a long slipway; difficult to control the vessel after water entry.

**2. Side Launching (Broadside Roll):**
- The vessel is rolled sideways into the water using log rollers beneath the hull.
- **Advantages:** Suitable for small fishing boats on flat beaches; no slipway infrastructure required.
- **Limitations:** Limited to vessels under 10 tonnes; high risk of capsizing if the center of gravity shifts during rolling; cannot be used in tidal areas.

**3. Davit / Crane Launching:**
- The vessel is suspended by slings and lifted by a wooden or steel crane.
- **Advantages:** Precise control; suitable for repairs and small craft; minimal site infrastructure.
- **Limitations:** Limited by crane capacity (typically 5 tonnes in traditional settings); sling pressure can damage delicate hull planking.

**4. Combined Slipway and Cradle Launching:**
- The vessel is built on a cradle that slides down a permanent slipway into the water.
- **Advantages:** Standardized method for medium to large vessels; reusable infrastructure.
- **Limitations:** High initial construction cost of slipway; slipway damage from repeated launches.

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Solution

**Derivation (as detailed in Section 3.1):**

The self-starting condition requires:

$$
\tan\theta > \mu
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total weight, W = 15,000 kgf
- Slipway angle, θ = 6°
- Coefficient of friction, μ = 0.05

**Step 1: Check Self-Starting Condition**

$$
\tan(6^{\circ}) = 0.1051
$$

$$
\mu = 0.05
$$

Since **0.1051 > 0.05**, the condition is satisfied. The vessel **will self-start**.

**Step 2: Calculate Net Launching Force**

$$
F_{net} = W(\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)
$$

$$
F_{net} = 15000 \times (\sin 6^{\circ} - 0.05 \times \cos 6^{\circ})
$$

$$
F_{net} = 15000 \times (0.1045 - 0.05 \times 0.9945)
$$

$$
F_{net} = 15000 \times (0.1045 - 0.0497)
$$

$$
F_{net} = 15000 \times 0.0548
$$

$$
F_{net} = 822 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Result:** The vessel will self-start with a net launching force of **822 kgf**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the pre-launching rituals and ceremonies traditionally followed in Kerala boat building. Explain the engineering significance of each ritual. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the common defects that may appear in a boat immediately after launching? How are they detected and rectified? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Pre-Launching Rituals and Their Engineering Significance

**1. Selection of Auspicious Time (*Muhurtham*):**
- **Ritual:** A temple astrologer (*panchangam* reader) selects a date and time based on lunar calendar and tidal conditions.
- **Engineering Significance:** This ensures that the launch coincides with **high tide**, providing maximum water depth at the slipway base and reducing grounding risk. It also avoids monsoon winds that could destabilize the freshly launched vessel.

**2. Pooja and Breaking of Coconut:**
- **Ritual:** A Hindu priest performs a pooja on the vessel, invoking blessings from the sea god. A coconut is broken against the hull or the trigger mechanism.
- **Engineering Significance:** The breaking of the coconut serves as a **physical signal to release the trigger**, ensuring that all crew members are alert and positioned safely. The ritual synchronizes the entire launching team to a single moment of action.

**3. Garlanding of the Bow:**
- **Ritual:** The bow is decorated with garlands of flowers, leaves, and sometimes a carved deity figure.
- **Engineering Significance:** This allows the launching master to **visually verify the bow's alignment** with the slipway centerline. Any mis-alignment is corrected before the launch.

**4. Offering to the Sea (*Kadal Kani*):**
- **Ritual:** After launch, the first catch or offerings are made to the sea.
- **Engineering Significance:** The maiden voyage is treated as a **shakedown test**. Any structural weaknesses, leaks, or steering issues are identified in calm conditions near the shore before the vessel embarks on its actual fishing or cargo operations.

#### Part (b) — Post-Launch Defects: Detection and Rectification

**1. Hull Leakage (Seam Failure):**
- **Detection:** Water seeping through plank seams, visible wetness on the interior, or bilge water accumulation.
- **Rectification:** Affected seams are re-caulked with cotton, coir, or pitch. In severe cases, the affected plank may be removed and replaced.

**2. Loose Fastenings (Treenail or Rivet Failure):**
- **Detection:** Audible creaking, visible movement at joints, or water ingress around fastenings.
- **Rectification:** Loose treenails are driven deeper or replaced. Rivets are re-hammered; severely corroded ones are drilled out and replaced with new copper rivets.

**3. Hull Distortion (Hogging or Sagging):**
- **Detection:** Visible curvature change in the longitudinal profile when viewed from the side.
- **Rectification:** Temporary shores and braces are installed. In severe cases, the vessel is re-hauled and the garboard planks or keel are reinforced.

**4. Steering and Rudder Issues:**
- **Detection:** Difficulty in maintaining course during the shakedown cruise; the vessel veers to one side.
- **Rectification:** The rudder pintles and gudgeons are re-aligned. The helm stock is checked for binding.

**5. Engine or Propulsion Alignment (for motorised vessels):**
- **Detection:** Vibration, excessive noise, or the vessel being pulled to one side.
- **Rectification:** The propeller shaft alignment is checked and corrected. The engine mounts are re-shimmed.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PENST864 Launching Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Self-Starting Condition:** Many students write the force balance equation but forget to verify whether the vessel will self-start. Examiners specifically check for the condition $\tan\theta > \mu$ as a separate logical step. Missing this costs **2 to 3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Cradle with Slipway:** The cradle is the **supporting frame on the vessel's underside**, while the slipway is the **inclined ramp on the ground**. Students who interchange these terms lose marks on descriptive questions.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Cultural Context:** Kerala's traditional boat building is deeply integrated with temple rituals and astrological timing. Answers that treat launching as a purely mechanical process, without mentioning **muhurtham, pooja, or kadal kani**, are marked down for missing cultural-engineering integration.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always use **kgf or N consistently** within a single numerical problem. Mixing units (e.g., kgf for weight and N for force) is a common error.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Flotation Transition:** When explaining the launch event, always describe the **moment when buoyancy takes over from the cradle support**. This is the conceptual climax of the entire process.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Launching** is the controlled transfer of a vessel from land to water, combining **mechanics, hydrostatics, and tradition**.
- The **cradle** supports the hull during construction and provides a low-friction sliding interface during launch.
- The **slipway** is the inclined ramp, typically built at **1:8 to 1:15 slope** for traditional Kerala craft.
- The **self-starting condition** is **tan θ > μ**; without this, external force (winch) is required.
- **Lubricants** used traditionally include **tallow, coconut oil, and neem oil** with friction coefficients of **0.05 to 0.15**.
- The **trigger mechanism** (damsel blocks and wedges) holds the vessel until the ceremonial signal, often the **breaking of a coconut**.
- The **flotation transition** is the moment when the vessel's weight is fully supported by water buoyancy, requiring positive **metacentric height (GM > 0)**.
- **Post-launch checks** include hull integrity, fastening tightness, steering alignment, and a **shakedown cruise** to detect leaks.
- **End launching (stern-first)** is the most common method in Kerala; **side launching** is used for smaller boats on flat beaches.
- The **self-starting angle** for a typical Kerala teak slipway with μ = 0.08 is approximately **4.57 degrees**.
- The **net launching force** is given by **F_net = W (sin θ − μ cos θ)**.
- Kerala's launching ceremonies (**muhurtham, pooja, kadal kani**) are not merely cultural but serve as **engineering safety synchronisation** mechanisms.
- The **block coefficient (C_b)** of traditional wooden hulls ranges from **0.55 to 0.75**, indicating full-bodied forms.
- **Buoyancy** at flotation equals the vessel's displacement weight: **B = W = ρ × g × V_submerged**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ways of Lifting and Launching a Vessel
Stepping the Mast
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stepping the Mast

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepping the Mast** is the traditional boat building operation in which the **mast** (the principal vertical spar) is carefully raised, lowered, and seated into its pre-prepared position on a vessel, engaging the **mast step** (the structural socket on the keelson or lower hull) and passing up through the **mast partner** (the reinforced opening on the deck) to stand vertically in the vessel's centerline. The process involves precise alignment, securing with wedges, partners, and wedges, and ensuring the mast is plumb (truly vertical) so that the rigging, sails, and hull bear loads symmetrically.

In traditional boat building vocabulary, "**stepping**" is exclusively used for the **installation of the mast**, while "**unstepping**" refers to its removal. The verb comes from the Old English *steppan*, meaning to fix firmly in place — analogous to how one "steps" into a socket.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lamp Stand:**
> Imagine you have a tall floor lamp. Before it can stand, you must:
> 1. **Prepare the base socket** (a fitted hole in a heavy wooden base),
> 2. **Align the pole** (the lamp's vertical shaft),
> 3. **Insert the pole** into the socket,
> 4. **Wedge it tight** so it cannot wobble,
> 5. **Add a collar at the top** (the deck partner) to hold it from drifting sideways, and
> 6. **Verify verticality** with a plumb bob.
>
> Stepping the mast in a traditional boat is exactly this — except the lamp is **massive timber**, the base sits in the **keel of a floating boat**, and the consequences of error are not just a fallen lamp but a **capsized vessel**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Dimensions

In traditional Kerala *vallam* and Arab-boat (uru) construction, the following nominal dimensions are typical:

- **Mast height above deck:** typically **1.5 ×** the boat's beam (for a balanced Marconi rig) or **2.0 ×** for gaff-rigged vessels.
- **Mast step location:** positioned on the **keelson** (the internal timber backbone), forward of midships in most working boats to counterbalance the helmsman and the mizzen.
- **Mast partner opening clearance:** normally **5 mm to 10 mm** of radial clearance around the mast — enough to allow varnishing and seasonal swelling, but tight enough to restrain lateral motion.
- **Wedge taper angle:** traditionally cut at roughly **1:8 (≈ 7°)** to grip without splitting the mast.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme traditional craft modules, the term **"stepping"** is always confined to mast erection. A candidate who uses "stepping" to describe any other fitting (e.g., "stepping the rudder") will be marked incorrect.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept Decomposed

The act of stepping a mast is **not a single event** — it is a controlled engineering sequence with five distinct logical phases:

### Phase A — Pre-Inspection
- Verify the **mast step** is clean, dry, and free of old bedding compound (red lead, tar, or pitch-soaked cotton in traditional craft).
- Check the **mast partner** (deck opening) for cracks, rot, or worn bolt holes.
- Confirm the **mast** is dry, free of large checks (splits), and that its **heel tenon** is undamaged.

### Phase B — Rigging Preparation (Before Lifting)
- Reeve all **standing rigging** (shrouds, stays, backstays) through their respective **mast blocks** while the mast is **horizontal on the deck or on trestles ashore**.
- Attach **tackle** (block-and-tackle purchase) at the **masthead** and lead the fall to a strongpoint — a **samson post**, **mast partner**, or shore-side **gin pole**.
- This step is critical: it is virtually impossible to reeve halyards or stays once the mast is vertical.

### Phase C — The Lift
- A **gin pole**, **shear legs**, or a **cargo derrick** is erected.
- The mast is pivoted about its **heel** (the bottom end), the heel sliding into the mast step as the head rises.
- The crew sings traditional work songs (e.g., the **Arab *hadhrami* capstan chant**, or the **Malabar *vanchipattu***) to synchronize pulling rhythm.

### Phase D — Seating and Wedging
- The **heel tenon** is dropped into the mast step socket.
- **Mast wedges** (fore and aft pair, plus port and starboard pair — four total) are driven into the gap between mast and partner to lock lateral movement.
- **Mast collar** (a half-round timber or metal band) is fitted over the partner for a weather seal.

### Phase E — Plumb and Tension
- The mast is checked for **verticality** with a **plumb bob** held against the partners at fore-aft and athwartships.
- **Standing rigging turnbuckles** (or **deadeye-and-lanyard** systems in traditional craft) are tensioned in a **criss-cross sequence** to pull the mast plumb from all four sides simultaneously.
- Final tensioning balances the **righting moment** of the hull against the **heeling moment** of the sail.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Standard | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mast height above deck | $H_m$ | $H_m = k \cdot B$ | metres | $B$ = boat beam; $k \in [1.5,\,2.5]$ |
| Heeling moment | $M_h$ | $M_h = F_{sail} \cdot h_{CE}$ | N·m | $h_{CE}$ = centre-of-effort height |
| Righting moment | $M_r$ | $M_r = \Delta \cdot \overline{GZ}(\phi)$ | N·m | $\overline{GZ}$ = righting lever |
| Condition for stability | — | $M_r \geq M_h$ | — | Crictical at max heel angle |
| Wedge grip force (friction) | $F_g$ | $F_g = \mu \cdot N_{wedge}$ | N | $\mu \approx 0.4$ (oak-on-oak) |
| Stay tension (equilibrium) | $T_s$ | $T_s = \dfrac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta}$ | N | $\theta$ = stay angle from deck |
| Mast taper ratio | $R_t$ | $R_t = \dfrac{D_{heel}}{D_{head}}$ | — | Typically $1.5$ to $2.0$ |
| Plumb tolerance | $\delta$ | $\delta \leq \dfrac{H_m}{200}$ | m | KTU practical marking standard |
| Mast partner clearance (radial) | $c$ | $c = 5\text{–}10$ | mm | Allows bedding & swelling |
| Wedge taper | $\alpha$ | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{1}{8}$ | degrees | $\alpha \approx 7.1^{\circ}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 exam purposes, memorise the **stability inequality** $M_r \geq M_h$ and the **stay-tension formula** $T_s = \dfrac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta}$ — these are the two highest-weightage items on stepping-related problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles governing mast stepping translate directly to:

- **Modern yacht design:** Carbon spars are still stepped into aluminium or composite sockets, with partner wedges controlling compression loads.
- **Offshore wind turbine erection:** The same "step-and-wedge" philosophy governs the installation of monopile foundations in offshore wind farms.
- **Civil flagpole and lighting tower installation:** Wind-moment calculations mirror sail heeling moment equations.
- **Historic ship restoration:** Museums restoring *uru*, *dhow*, or *kettuvallam* rely on traditional stepping techniques because the original scarf joints and treenails cannot take modern mechanical fasteners.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Stay Tension to Hold a Stepped Mast Plumb

Consider a mast of height $H_m$ subjected to a horizontal sail force $F_{sail}$ acting at the **centre of effort (CE)**, height $h_{CE}$ above the deck. Two symmetric **shrouds** (port and starboard) hold the mast against overturning. Each shroud makes angle $\theta$ with the deck and is tensioned to $T_s$.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Setup
The mast is treated as a rigid vertical column, pinned at the **mast step** and supported laterally by the shrouds. The sail pushes the mast athwartships with force $F_{sail}$.

### Step 2 — Horizontal Force Equilibrium
Sum of horizontal forces = 0 (mast is in static equilibrium):

$$
F_{sail} = 2 T_s \sin\theta
$$

This is because each shroud contributes a horizontal component $T_s \sin\theta$; two shrouds (port + starboard) give $2 T_s \sin\theta$.

### Step 3 — Solve for Stay Tension
Rearranging the equilibrium equation for $T_s$:

$$
T_s = \frac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta}
$$

### Step 4 — Insert Numerical Realism
For a small *vallam* with $F_{sail} = 2000$ N and a shroud angle $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ from the deck (a typical working-boat value):

$$
\sin 60^{\circ} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{2000}{2 \times 0.8660} = \frac{2000}{1.7321} \approx 1154.7 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
Each shroud must sustain approximately **1155 N of tension** to hold the mast in equilibrium. In traditional craft, this tension is supplied and trimmed by **deadeye-and-lanyard** turnbuckles, which the shipwright tightens alternately (port-then-starboard) to keep the mast plumb.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plumb Tolerance for a Stepped Mast

The KTU practical-exam standard requires the mast to be plumb within a tolerance that scales with height.

### Step 1 — Define Plumb Tolerance
The allowed lateral offset at the masthead, $\delta$, must satisfy:

$$
\delta \leq \frac{H_m}{200}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Typical Height
For a *kettuvallam* mast with $H_m = 6.0$ m:

$$
\delta \leq \frac{6.0}{200} = 0.030 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify with Plumb-Bob Geometry
If a plumb line of length $L = H_m$ is hung from the masthead, and the line is sighted against a vertical reference, the bob must fall within a circle of radius $\delta = 30$ mm at the deck level. This is easily checked with a **spirit level** placed across the partners.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Stepping Hazards

| Hazard | Cause | Consequence | Traditional Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mast step rot** | Water pooling in unbedded step socket | Mast foot decay, sudden loss | Tar-soaked cotton bedding; yearly inspection |
| **Partner ovaling** | Repeated wedging with too-steep angle | Deck opening widens; mast whips | Cut wedges at 1:8 taper; renew annually |
| **Springing mast** | Insufficient shroud tension | Resonance in seaway → fatigue failure | Pre-load shrouds to $T_s$ before sailing |
| **Raking mast** | Heel seated off-centerline | Asymmetric sail force → permanent heel | Mark mast centerline; seat heel into step notch |
| **Head sway during lift** | Uneven hauling on tackles | Mast drops or cracks | Use **gin pole** + two equal hauls; chant for rhythm |
| **Bedding squeeze-out** | Over-bedding compound | Step socket floats; mast loose | Bed with **3 mm layer**; trim squeeze-out flush |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A traditional *uru* has a beam $B = 3.0$ m. The mast height above deck is set at $H_m = 1.7 B$. The shrouds are rigged at $\theta = 55^{\circ}$ to the deck. The sail's centre of effort is at $h_{CE} = 0.65 H_m$. The sail force under a fresh breeze is $F_{sail} = 4500$ N.

**Find:** (a) the mast height $H_m$, (b) the required stay tension $T_s$, and (c) the plumb tolerance $\delta$.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Mast Height:**

$$
H_m = 1.7 \times B = 1.7 \times 3.0 = 5.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Part (b) — Stay Tension:**

$$
\sin 55^{\circ} \approx 0.8192
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{4500}{2 \times 0.8192} = \frac{4500}{1.6384} \approx 2746.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Part (c) — Plumb Tolerance:**

$$
\delta \leq \frac{H_m}{200} = \frac{5.1}{200} = 0.0255 \text{ m} = 25.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answers (boxed for valuation):**

$$
\boxed{H_m = 5.1 \text{ m}}, \quad \boxed{T_s \approx 2747 \text{ N}}, \quad \boxed{\delta \leq 25.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Stepping Sequence

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between sub-systems and crew roles** during a traditional mast-stepping operation, from initial site preparation through final sea trial.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Preparation] --> B[Mast Step Inspection]
    B --> C[Partner Inspection]
    C --> D[Mast Horizontal on Trestles]
    D --> E[Reeve Standing Rigging]
    E --> F[Attach Heel Lift Tackle]
    F --> G[Attach Masthead Fall to Gin Pole]
    G --> H[Singer Leads Work Chant]
    H --> I[Synchronised Haul Begins]
    I --> J[Heel Pivots into Step]
    J --> K[Heel Tenon Seats in Socket]
    K --> L[Drive Fore-Aft Wedges]
    L --> M[Drive Port-Starboard Wedges]
    M --> N[Plumb-Bob Check Fore-Aft]
    N --> O[Plumb-Bob Check Athwartships]
    O --> P[Tension Starboard Shrouds]
    P --> Q[Tension Port Shrouds]
    Q --> R[Final Plumb Verification]
    R --> S[Furl and Secure Halyards]
    S --> T[Sea Trial Under Reduced Sail]
    T --> U[Full Commissioning]
```

## 4.2 Sub-System State Diagram — Mast Stability Post-Stepping

This diagram illustrates the **load paths** that activate once the mast is stepped and the vessel is under sail.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hull_Group
        hullA[Keelson]
        hullB[Mast Step Socket]
        hullC[Deck Partner Frame]
    end
    subgraph Mast_Group
        mastA[Mast Heel Tenon]
        mastB[Mast Shaft]
        mastC[Masthead Cap]
    end
    subgraph Rig_Group
        rigA[Port Shroud]
        rigB[Starboard Shroud]
        rigC[Forestay]
        rigD[Backstay]
    end
    subgraph Sail_Group
        sailA[Mainsail]
        sailB[Centre of Effort Force]
    end
    hullB --> mastA
    mastA --> mastB
    mastB --> mastC
    hullC --> mastB
    sailA --> sailB
    sailB --> mastB
    sailB --> rigA
    sailB --> rigB
    sailB --> rigC
    sailB --> rigD
    rigA --> hullC
    rigB --> hullC
    rigC --> mastC
    rigD --> mastC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Sail_Group* generates the wind force, which is transferred through the *Mast_Group* to the *Hull_Group* via the mast step (vertical compression) and the partner (lateral restraint). The *Rig_Group* keeps the mast plumb by absorbing the lateral sail force and routing it back to chainplates on the hull.

## 4.3 Decision-Flow for Wedge Seating Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Wedges Driven] --> q1{Mast Plumb?}
    q1 -- Yes --> ok1[Lock Wedges with Nail]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Which Way Leans?}
    q2 -- Forward --> act1[Drive Aft Wedge Deeper]
    q2 -- Aft --> act2[Drive Forward Wedge Deeper]
    q2 -- Port --> act3[Drive Starboard Wedge Deeper]
    q2 -- Starboard --> act4[Drive Port Wedge Deeper]
    act1 --> q1
    act2 --> q1
    act3 --> q1
    act4 --> q1
    ok1 --> end1[Seal with Mast Collar]
    end1 --> done[Stepping Complete]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define "Stepping the Mast" as used in traditional boat building. Name the two structural members that physically retain the mast.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stepping the mast is the operation of **raising and seating the mast vertically** into a traditional sailing vessel so that it is securely held between the **mast step** (a socket located on the keelson or lower hull) below and the **mast partner** (a reinforced opening framed into the deck) above. The two members that retain the mast are therefore the **mast step** (lower support, takes the vertical compression load) and the **mast partner** (upper support, takes the lateral load via wedges). **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. List any three preparatory checks a shipwright must perform on the mast and rigging *before* attempting to step the mast.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Inspect the mast step** for cleanliness, dryness, and integrity of the bedding compound. **(1 mark)**
2. **Inspect the mast heel tenon and partner opening** for cracks, rot, ovaling, or worn bolt holes. **(1 mark)**
3. **Reeve all standing rigging** (shrouds, stays, halyards) through the mast sheaves while the mast is still horizontal on trestles — once stepped, this is impossible. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, ESE Pattern]**
> *Answer any **one** full question from the choices below.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **step-by-step procedure for stepping the mast** of a traditional sailing vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traditional *kettuvallam* has a beam of **2.8 m**. The mast height is set at **1.6 ×** the beam, and the shrouds make an angle of **60°** with the deck. If the sail force at full breeze is **3200 N**, calculate:
- (i) the mast height $H_m$, and
- (ii) the required stay tension $T_s$ per shroud. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Inspection (1 mark):** The mast step is cleaned and re-bedded with tar-soaked cotton; the partner is checked for cracks; the mast heel tenon is examined for splits or rot.
2. **Rigging Preparation (1.5 marks):** The mast is laid horizontal on trestles. All standing rigging (shrouds, stays, halyards) is reeved through their respective sheaves. A **gin pole** or **shear legs** is erected, and a tackle is attached at the masthead with the fall led to a strongpoint.
3. **The Lift (1.5 marks):** The crew hauls the fall in unison (often timed to a work song). The mast pivots about its heel, which slides into the mast step. The heel tenon seats into the step socket.
4. **Wedging (1.5 marks):** Fore-aft and port-starboard pairs of mast wedges are driven into the partner gap. The wedges are cut at a 1:8 taper for grip without splitting the mast.
5. **Plumbing and Tensioning (1.5 marks):** A plumb bob is used to check verticality fore-aft and athwartships. Shrouds are tensioned in criss-cross sequence (starboard → port → starboard) until the masthead stands within the **$H_m / 200$** tolerance.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Mast Height:**

$$
H_m = 1.6 \times B = 1.6 \times 2.8 = 4.48 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_m = 4.48 \text{ m}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Part (ii) — Stay Tension:**

$$
T_s = \frac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{3200}{2 \times \sin 60^{\circ}}
$$

$$
\sin 60^{\circ} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{3200}{1.7321} \approx 1847.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_s \approx 1847.5 \text{ N per shroud}} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **roles of the mast step and the mast partner** in a traditional sailing vessel. How do they work together to retain the stepped mast? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traditional *uru* is fitted with shrouds at an angle of **50°** to the deck. During a storm, the sail force rises to **6000 N**.
- (i) Calculate the tension in each shroud.
- (ii) If the mast height is **7.0 m**, compute the maximum allowable plumb offset at the masthead per KTU standards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Roles of Mast Step and Partner (7 marks):**

- **Mast Step (3 marks):** The mast step is a **socket built into the keelson** at the boat's centerline. Its role is to receive the **heel tenon** of the mast and transmit the **vertical compression load** (the entire weight of the mast plus the downward component of the rig tension) down into the hull's backbone. It is the **primary load-bearing fitting** for the mast.
- **Mast Partner (2 marks):** The mast partner is the **framed opening in the deck** through which the mast passes. Its role is to **restrain the mast laterally** (sideways) and **transmit the horizontal sail force** into the deck beams and hull sides. The partner also provides a weather seal via the mast collar.
- **Combined Function (2 marks):** Together, the step and partner create a **simply-supported column condition**: the step takes the vertical thrust, the partner takes the lateral thrust. Wedges at the partner pre-load the mast against the partner, eliminating play. This is the same statical arrangement as a flagpole in a stone base — the base takes weight, the sleeve takes sway.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Stay Tension:**

$$
T_s = \frac{F_{sail}}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{6000}{2 \times \sin 50^{\circ}}
$$

$$
\sin 50^{\circ} \approx 0.7660
$$

$$
T_s = \frac{6000}{1.5321} \approx 3916.2 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_s \approx 3916.2 \text{ N per shroud}} \quad \textbf{(3.5 marks)}
$$

**Part (ii) — Plumb Tolerance:**

$$
\delta \leq \frac{H_m}{200} = \frac{7.0}{200} = 0.035 \text{ m} = 35 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \leq 35 \text{ mm}} \quad \textbf{(3.5 marks)}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps on Stepping-the-Mast Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "stepping" with "step."** "Stepping" is the *action*; the *mast step* is the *socket*. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for interchanging these terms.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in stay-tension formula.** The formula is $T_s = F_{sail} / (2\sin\theta)$ because there are **two** shrouds. A common student error is writing $T_s = F_{sail} / \sin\theta$, which yields an answer **twice the correct value** — losing **2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the plumb-tolerance step.** KTU awards a full **1.5 mark** for stating the tolerance rule $H_m/200$ explicitly. Skipping it costs marks even if the final number is correct.
> 4. **Using the wrong beam ratio.** The ratio $H_m = kB$ uses $k$ between **1.5 and 2.5** depending on rig type; assuming $k = 1$ is a frequent error.
> 5. **Drawing the wedge at the wrong taper.** Anything steeper than 1:6 will split the mast. Examiners specifically check that the student writes the correct **1:8 taper** to earn the 1 mark on partner design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stepping the Mast**

- **Stepping the Mast** = the act of raising and seating the vertical spar of a sailing vessel into its operational position.
- The **mast step** is the lower socket (on the keelson); the **mast partner** is the upper opening (on the deck).
- Always **reeve all standing rigging while the mast is horizontal** — never after stepping.
- Use a **gin pole / shear legs** for the lift; never freehand.
- **Heel tenon** must seat fully into the step socket; failure here = loss of the rig.
- **Mast wedges** are cut at a **1:8 taper** and driven in **four pairs** (fore, aft, port, starboard).
- **Plumb tolerance** is $\delta \leq H_m / 200$ — verified with a plumb bob fore-aft AND athwartships.
- **Stay tension formula:** $T_s = F_{sail} / (2\sin\theta)$ — remember the factor of 2.
- **Mast height ratio:** $H_m = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times B$, where $B$ = boat beam.
- **Stability condition** under sail: $M_r \geq M_h$ (righting moment ≥ heeling moment).
- **Partner radial clearance** should be **5–10 mm** — allows for bedding and seasonal timber swelling.
- **Bedding compound** is traditionally **tar-soaked cotton, red lead, or pitch** — never silicone in heritage craft.
- The **mast collar** (timber or metal half-round) seals the partner against water ingress.
- In **Kerala vallam** and **Arab dhow (uru)** tradition, the lift is synchronised with a **work song / capstan chant** to ensure even hauling.
- **Unstepping** is the reverse operation — used for storage, maintenance, or end-of-season haul-out.
- Common failure modes: **rotten step, ovaled partner, springing mast, raking mast** — all preventable by proper bedding, wedging, and shroud tensioning.
- For KTU 2024 ESE, expect: **1 short question (3 marks)** on definition/preparations + **1 long question (14 marks)** combining procedure with a numerical on stay tension or plumb tolerance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Modifications* in traditional boat building refer to the deliberate structural, dimensional, material, and design alterations made to indigenous craft (such as *Vallam*, *Dhow*, *Uru*, *Dhoni*, *Kattumaram*, and country crafts) to enhance their hydrodynamic performance, structural integrity, seaworthiness, cargo capacity, and adaptability to modern operational demands — while preserving the essence of vernacular naval architecture.

In simpler terms, **modifications are the "tune-ups" of a traditional boat** — just as a mechanic upgrades an old car engine with a new fuel injector, a traditional boat builder reshapes the hull, swaps the sail for a diesel engine, or replaces coir rope rigging with synthetic fibre to make the vessel faster, safer, and more durable.

### 1.1 Why Are Modifications Necessary?
Traditional boats were designed centuries ago for specific local conditions — calm backwaters, monsoon winds, or riverine trade. With changing needs, these vessels require modification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Drivers of Modification in KTU Context:**
> - **Performance Upgradation:** Better speed, stability, and fuel efficiency.
> - **Material Substitution:** Replacing scarce/expensive traditional materials (e.g., teak, jungle wood) with cost-effective alternatives (plywood, FRP, aluminium).
> - **Regulatory Compliance:** Meeting modern maritime safety codes (load lines, life-saving appliances).
> - **Operational Shift:** Conversion from sailing to motorized propulsion.
> - **Economic Viability:** Reducing construction time and cost.

### 1.2 Categories of Modifications (Overview)
A high-level taxonomy used in KTU valuation:

| S.No | Modification Type | Primary Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hull Form Modification | Shape, curvature, entry angle |
| 2 | Structural Modification | Frame, plank, keel reinforcement |
| 3 | Material Modification | Wood → Plywood → FRP transition |
| 4 | Propulsion Modification | Sail → Outboard → Inboard engine |
| 5 | Outfit Modification | Deck, cabin, fittings, rigging |
| 6 | Functional Modification | Passenger, fishing, cargo conversion |

**Standard Metric Reference:** Displacement-to-Length Ratio (DLR) for traditional crafts typically ranges between **150 to 350**, with modifications aimed at lowering this for planing-type hulls.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Boat Modifications

Modification decisions in traditional boat building follow a structured engineering logic. Each modification can be mapped to one of the following physical principles:

- **Archimedes' Principle** — governs buoyancy changes when hull form is altered.
- **Bernoulli's Equation** — explains water-flow behaviour over modified hull lines.
- **Newton's Second Law** — applied to propulsion modification (engine thrust vs. resistance).
- **Hooke's Law** — relevant for structural frame modifications under load.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Hull Form Modification

1. **Identify the Operational Defect** — e.g., excessive rolling, low speed, bow hammering.
2. **Diagnose the Hydrodynamic Cause** — e.g., too-flat buttocks, insufficient deadrise.
3. **Select Modification Vector** — e.g., increase rocker, add chine, flare the bow.
4. **Quantify the Change** — using offsets, station lines, and design ratios.
5. **Implement and Test** — model testing or full-scale trials.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Oriented)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of the `|` symbol in exam-style tables to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| S.No | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Displacement ($\Delta$) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$ | Total weight of water displaced | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | Block Coefficient ($C_B$) | $C_B = \dfrac{V}{\nabla \cdot L_{WL}}$ | Hull fullness | dimensionless |
| 3 | Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$) | $C_P = \dfrac{V}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$ | Longitudinal distribution of volume | dimensionless |
| 4 | Length-Displacement Ratio (LDR) | $LDR = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{\sqrt[3]{\Delta}}$ | Speed potential indicator | $\vert ft \cdot t^{-1/3}\vert$ |
| 5 | Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$) | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{B$ | Stability vs. speed trade-off | dimensionless |
| 6 | Slenderness Ratio (Beam) | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{B}$ | Stability vs. speed trade-off | dimensionless |
| 7 | Speed-Length Ratio (SLR) | $SLR = \dfrac{V_{kn}}{\sqrt{L_{WL}}}$ | Hull speed classification | dimensionless |
| 8 | Righting Moment (RM) | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | Restoring moment at heel | kN·m |
| 9 | Hull Speed ($V_h$) | $V_h = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Maximum displacement speed | knots |
| 10 | Resistance Increase Factor | $R_{new} = R_{old} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{B,new}}{C_{B,old}}\right)^{2/3}$ | Empirical modification impact | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Question papers frequently ask for *the relationship between LDR, C_B, and SLR* during modification analysis. Memorize the inverse relationship between C_B and speed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Kerala Vallam Modification:** Beach-launched *Vallams* are modified by adding a **chine strake** for better tracking in surf.
- **Uru Conversion:** Traditional Malabar *Urus* are modified by adding a **wheelhouse** and **auxiliary diesel** to comply with fishing licensing norms.
- **Kattumaram Modernization:** The single-log catamaran is modified to twin-outrigger FRP *vallams* for higher catch capacity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Hull Length Modification on Hull Speed

Let a traditional boat of waterline length $L_{WL}$ be extended by a modification factor $k$. The new length is $L_{new} = L_{WL} \cdot (1 + k)$, where $k$ is the fractional length increase (e.g., $k = 0.10$ for 10% extension).

**Step 1: Express the original hull speed.**

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}
$$

**Step 2: Express the modified hull speed.**

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL} \cdot (1 + k)}
$$

**Step 3: Factor out the original length term.**

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}} \cdot \sqrt{(1 + k)}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 1 into Step 3.**

$$
V_{h,new} = V_{h,old} \cdot \sqrt{(1 + k)}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the percentage increase in hull speed.**

$$
\% \Delta V_h = \left(\sqrt{(1 + k)} - 1\right) \cdot 100
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
A traditional *Vallam* of $L_{WL} = 8 \, m$ is extended by $k = 0.21$ (21%). Find the new hull speed.

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 1.34 \cdot 2.828 = 3.79 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
V_{h,new} = 3.79 \cdot \sqrt{1.21} = 3.79 \cdot 1.100 = 4.17 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
\% \Delta V_h = (1.100 - 1) \cdot 100 = 10\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 21% increase in length yields only a 10% increase in hull speed — this is a **non-linear (square-root) relationship** commonly tested in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation: Beam Modification Effect on Stability

When a boat's beam $B$ is increased by modification factor $m$, the transverse metacentric height $GM$ approximately scales with the square of the beam for small-angle stability:

$$
GM \approx \dfrac{I_T}{V} - KB
$$

where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of waterplane area.

For a rectangular approximation:

$$
I_T \approx \dfrac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12}
$$

After beam modification $B_{new} = B \cdot (1 + m)$:

$$
I_{T,new} \approx \dfrac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3 \cdot (1 + m)^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
GM_{new} \approx GM_{old} \cdot (1 + m)^3
$$

This **cubic relationship** is why traditional boat builders carefully avoid excessive beam increase — a small beam change produces a disproportionately large stability gain (or loss, if reduced).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Modification Impact

| Modification | Parameter Affected | Direction of Change | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length Extension | $L_{WL}$ ↑ | $V_h$ ↑ (square root) | Higher hull speed |
| Beam Increase | $B$ ↑ | $GM$ ↑ (cubic) | Greater initial stability |
| Deadrise Increase | Hull V-angle ↑ | Slamming ↓ | Smoother ride in waves |
| Engine Addition | Mass ↑ | Draft ↑ | Reduced freeboard risk |
| FRP Sheathing | Skin mass ↑ | Strength ↑ | Longer hull life |
| Chine Addition | $C_B$ ↑ | Stability ↑ | Planing tendency |
| Rocker Reduction | Curvature ↓ | Tracking ↑ | Less maneuverable |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Modification Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block represents the **decision and execution flow** of a traditional boat modification project — from initial defect identification to final sea trial.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Traditional Boat Baseline] --> B[Operational Need Identified]
    B --> C{Need Category}
    C -->|Performance| D[Hull Form Modification]
    C -->|Strength| E[Structural Modification]
    C -->|Material| F[Material Substitution]
    C -->|Propulsion| G[Powerplant Conversion]
    C -->|Function| H[Outfit and Layout Change]
    D --> D1[Resize LWL, B, T]
    D --> D2[Adjust Cb, Cp, LDR]
    D --> D3[Add chine, flare, sheer]
    E --> E1[Frame reinforcement]
    E --> E2[Keel strengthening]
    E --> E3[Plank thickness change]
    F --> F1[Wood to Plywood]
    F --> F2[Wood to FRP Sheathing]
    F --> F3[Coir to Synthetic Rope]
    G --> G1[Sail to Outboard]
    G --> G2[Sail to Inboard Diesel]
    G --> G3[Hybrid Sail-Engine]
    H --> H1[Open to Cabin]
    H --> H2[Passenger conversion]
    H --> H3[Fishing gear addition]
    D1 --> I[Design Re-Calculation]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Stability and Resistance Check]
    J --> K{Pass Criteria}
    K -->|Yes| L[Construction Modification]
    K -->|No| C
    L --> M[Sea Trial and Validation]
    M --> N[Modified Traditional Boat]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Explanation

- **Node A → B:** The original traditional craft enters the modification pipeline.
- **Node C:** A *decision diamond* classifies the modification need.
- **Sub-nodes (D1–H3):** Represent specific modification actions.
- **Node I:** Central design verification hub — all changes converge here.
- **Node J → K:** Stability and resistance validation; failed designs loop back to C.
- **Node L → M → N:** Final construction, sea trial, and delivery of the **Modified Traditional Boat**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, students are advised to draw such a **flowchart** when asked *"Explain the modification process"* — it fetches full marks under the *diagrammatic representation* component.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Modification" in the context of traditional boat building. List any four types of modifications commonly carried out on indigenous craft.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Modification refers to the deliberate structural, dimensional, or material alterations made to a traditional boat to improve its performance, safety, or operational utility without completely redesigning it from scratch.
**Four types:**
1. Hull form modification (shape, entry angle)
2. Material modification (wood to FRP/plywood)
3. Propulsion modification (sail to engine)
4. Outfit modification (open deck to cabin)

*(Distribution: 1 mark – definition, 2 marks – four types)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is a "chine" in a boat hull? How does the addition of a chine act as a modification to a traditional round-bottom hull?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **chine** is the longitudinal line formed where the side of the hull meets the bottom panels at a distinct angle, replacing the smooth curve of a round-bottom hull.
When added to a traditional round-bottom craft (like a *Vallam*), a chine provides:
- Greater lateral resistance (better tracking)
- Increased transverse stability
- A flatter planing surface at higher speeds

*(Distribution: 1 mark – definition, 2 marks – modification effect)*
