### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark per missing unit**.
> - In derivation questions, skipping the substitution step leads to **loss of 2–3 marks**.
> - For modification classification questions, students write *one-word answers* (e.g., "Hull form") without **justification** — always explain *what* is modified and *why*.
> - Do not confuse **modification** with **modernization** — modification = alteration of existing craft; modernization = redesign.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hull Form & Structural Modifications

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **hull form modifications** carried out on traditional Kerala boats. Discuss how each modification affects the hydrodynamic performance using the relevant dimensionless coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional fishing *Vallam* of waterline length $L_{WL} = 7 \, m$ and block coefficient $C_B = 0.55$ is modified by increasing its length to $9 \, m$ while keeping the volume approximately the same.
(i) Calculate the new block coefficient.
(ii) Comment on the effect of this modification on the **Speed-Length Ratio** and **hull speed**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hull Form Modifications (7 Marks):**
1. **Length Extension (1 Mark):** Increases $L_{WL}$ → reduces $C_B$ for the same volume → improves SLR and hull speed.
2. **Beam Increase (1 Mark):** Increases $B$ → increases $C_B$ → increases $GM$ cubically → better initial stability.
3. **Deadrise Modification (1 Mark):** Increasing V-angle reduces slamming and improves seakeeping in choppy seas.
4. **Rocker Adjustment (1 Mark):** Reducing rocker (flatter keel line) improves directional tracking.
5. **Sheer and Flare Modification (1 Mark):** Increases freeboard at bow, reducing wet deck in head seas.
6. **Chine and Spray Rail Addition (1 Mark):** Provides lateral resistance and deflects spray.
7. **Transom Modification (1 Mark):** Converting double-ender to transom stern allows inboard engine installation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Use the block coefficient relation: $C_B = \dfrac{V}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$

Since volume $V$ is constant, and assuming $B$ and $T$ remain unchanged:

$$
C_{B,new} = C_{B,old} \cdot \dfrac{L_{WL,old}}{L_{WL,new}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
C_{B,new} = 0.55 \cdot \dfrac{7}{9}
$$

$$
C_{B,new} = 0.55 \cdot 0.7778 = 0.428
$$

**Step 3 (i):** Therefore, the **new block coefficient is $C_B \approx 0.43$** (a slimmer, faster hull form). **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 (ii):** Compute old and new hull speeds:

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{7} = 1.34 \cdot 2.646 = 3.55 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{9} = 1.34 \cdot 3.000 = 4.02 \; \text{knots}
$$

**Step 5:** Percentage increase in hull speed:

$$
\% \Delta V_h = \dfrac{4.02 - 3.55}{3.55} \cdot 100 = 13.24\%
$$

**Step 6:** New Speed-Length Ratio:

$$
SLR_{old} = \dfrac{3.55}{\sqrt{7}} = 1.342
$$

$$
SLR_{new} = \dfrac{4.02}{\sqrt{9}} = 1.340
$$

*(Note: SLR is mathematically fixed at 1.34 for displacement hulls at hull speed — the modification now allows the boat to *reach* this SLR more efficiently.)*

**Conclusion (2 Marks):** The modification produces a **13% gain in hull speed** with a slimmer hull form (lower $C_B$), confirming that length extension is one of the most effective hull form modifications for speed enhancement in traditional craft.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Material & Propulsion Modifications *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** Discuss the **material modifications** traditionally adopted in boat building — from solid wood to plywood to FRP. Compare their advantages and limitations in the context of Kerala's traditional crafts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional sailing *Dhow* of displacement $\Delta = 8$ tonnes is converted to a motorized vessel by installing a 25 HP outboard engine. The engine weighs **95 kg** and the auxiliary fuel tank adds **40 kg**. The original crew + cargo load was 600 kg. Determine the **new displacement** and the **percentage increase in draft**, assuming the waterplane area remains constant at $A_W = 12 \, m^2$ and the seawater density is $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Material Modifications (7 Marks):**
1. **Solid Wood → Marine Plywood (2 Marks):**
   - *Advantages:* Reduced cost, uniform strength, less warping, easy availability.
   - *Limitations:* Susceptible to delamination, requires epoxy sealing.
2. **Plywood → FRP (Fibreglass) (2 Marks):**
   - *Advantages:* Waterproof, low maintenance, high strength-to-weight ratio, easy moulding.
   - *Limitations:* High initial mould cost, poor reparability, environmental concerns.
3. **Coir Rope → Synthetic Fibre (1 Mark):**
   - Higher tensile strength, rot resistance, longer life.
4. **Iron Fastenings → Stainless Steel / Copper (1 Mark):**
   - Corrosion resistance, reduced staining of timber.
5. **Lead Ballast → Concrete or Iron Pig (1 Mark):**
   - Cost reduction, local availability.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert engine and fuel tank mass to tonnes:

$$
m_{engine} = 95 \; kg = 0.095 \; t
$$

$$
m_{fuel} = 40 \; kg = 0.040 \; t
$$

**Step 2:** Compute new displacement:

$$
\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + m_{engine} + m_{fuel} + m_{crew+cargo}
$$

$$
\Delta_{new} = 8 + 0.095 + 0.040 + 0.6
$$

$$
\Delta_{new} = 8.735 \; t
$$

**Step 3:** Convert displacement to newtons ($1 \, t \approx 9.81 \, kN$):

$$
W_{new} = 8.735 \cdot 9.81 = 85.69 \; kN
$$

**Step 4:** Compute original draft (for reference, original $W_{old}$ in kN):

$$
W_{old} = (8 + 0.6) \cdot 9.81 = 84.366 \; kN
$$

**Step 5:** Use $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_W \cdot T$ to find drafts:

$$
T_{old} = \dfrac{W_{old}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_W} = \dfrac{84366}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 12}
$$

$$
T_{old} = \dfrac{84366}{120651} = 0.699 \; m
$$

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{W_{new}}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 12} = \dfrac{85690}{120651} = 0.710 \; m
$$

**Step 6:** Percentage increase in draft:

$$
\% \Delta T = \dfrac{0.710 - 0.699}{0.699} \cdot 100 = 1.57\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The propulsion modification increases displacement by **9.2%** but draft increases by only **1.57%** due to the large waterplane area, confirming the boat's suitability for engine retrofitting without major redesign.

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Modifications" (Module 4)**
>
> - **Definition:** Alterations to traditional craft for performance/safety/economic gain.
> - **6 Major Types:** Hull form, Structural, Material, Propulsion, Outfit, Functional.
> - **Key Coefficients to Memorize:** $C_B$, $C_P$, LDR, SLR, $\lambda$ (Slenderness).
> - **Hull Speed Formula:** $V_h = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ in knots.
> - **Beam–Stability Relation:** $GM \propto B^3$ (cubic, very sensitive).
> - **Length–Speed Relation:** $V_h \propto \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ (square-root, less sensitive).
> - **Material Evolution Path:** Solid wood → Plywood → FRP composite.
> - **Propulsion Evolution Path:** Paddle → Sail → Outboard → Inboard → Hybrid.
> - **Kerala Specials:** Vallam chine addition, Uru wheelhouse retrofit, Kattumaram to twin-outrigger FRP.
> - **Always cite units in numerical answers** — major KTU mark-deduction point.
> - **Modification ≠ Modernization** — modification alters existing craft; modernization is a full redesign.
